Welcome to the IAICF National Curriculum Prototyping and Co-design Workshop Dashboard
Interdisciplinary AI Competency Framework for Higher Education  ·  Version 3.0  ·  April 2026

Funded by Advance HE, the Interdisciplinary AI Competency Framework for Higher Education (IAICF) is a sector-wide, evidence-based initiative developed by academics, for academics. Its purpose is to ensure that AI literacy becomes a shared graduate capability across every discipline, not a specialism confined to computing or engineering.

The framework spans six domains and 32 ratified competencies, covering conceptual understanding of AI systems, critical evaluation of outputs, ethical and social implications, practical application, governance and regulation, and cognitive diversity and inclusive AI literacy. Each competency is mapped across FHEQ Levels 4 to 8, and the framework distinguishes Universal competencies (expected across every programme) from Contextual competencies (applied where directly relevant to disciplinary and professional formation), with one competency specifically scoped to Arts and Humanities where the National Interdisciplinary Panel ratified that scope.

The framework was ratified through extensive national consultation. The National Survey attracted 856 expressions of interest, with 622 academics from 61 universities completing the survey. A further 150 academics participated in five discipline-specific National Expert Panels, and 103 academics attended the National Interdisciplinary Panel, together contributing over 12 hours of structured expert discussion. Phase 2 concluded with ratification of the framework on 15 April 2026.

Phase 3, which this dashboard accompanies, moves the project from framework ratification to curriculum translation. Programme directors, module leads, and discipline representatives across UK higher education are co-designing the implementation toolkit through structured workshops between 21 April and 15 May 2026. This dashboard is the single-document companion for those workshops.

across 6 domains

Guide to this Dashboard
Preface Background and rationale for the IAICF project, the sectoral evidence base, the full framework structure with domain-level competency counts, and the architecture of the two-tier framework.
The Workshop Purpose, structure, and methodology of the Phase 3 National Curriculum Prototyping and Co-design Workshop. Includes the three-hour workshop schedule, consent and ethics arrangements, and how participant input feeds into IAICF v3.1 and the Implementation Toolkit.
Domain 1 to 6 Each domain tab presents its ratified competencies in workshop-reference format: simplified title and tier indicator on the title line, specialist title as subtitle, full rationale and competency statement, and collapsible sections for the FHEQ Level 4 to Level 8 descriptors and the disciplinary contextualisation notes for each of five discipline groups.
Co-Design Activities Three pen-and-paper canvases used on the workshop day: the First Impressions Audit (5 to 7 minutes), Activity 1B Module-Level Mapping (50 minutes), and Activity 3 Equity Review (30 minutes).
Curriculum Cheatsheets Three look-up references for participants working through the activity canvases: 40 assessment methods across 8 categories with AI stance and domain links; 40 teaching methods across 8 categories including AI-integrated pedagogies; and all 32 competencies mapped to four AI tools each for teaching and assessment use.
Project Team Profiles of the investigators, co-design research partners, academic advisors and curriculum co-design co-hosts, and industry advisors who have contributed to the IAICF project across all three phases.
A note on confidentiality. This dashboard, its contents, and all framework materials are confidential working documents of the Advance HE-funded IAICF project (Fund ID: 0000000204, Ethics Reference: ERN_5490-Feb2026). Please do not redistribute outside the Phase 3 workshop cohort without the Principal Investigator's written consent.
Preface
About the IAICF Project

The IAICF project has been developed by academics, for academics, with the aim of establishing a robust, evidence-based framework for AI competency in higher education. The project is funded by Advance HE and is led by a consortium of academics from five partner institutions: University of Birmingham, University College London, University of East London, University of Birmingham Dubai (UAE), and Suleyman Demirel University (Türkiye), in collaboration with an academic advisory board and co-hosts from the University of Hull, the University of Leeds, and Nanyang Technological University, as well as industry partners DataDecisions.AI, RGBY.AI and Aerogel Core Ltd. This cross-institutional and cross-national partnership reflects the project's ambition to develop a framework that is academically rigorous, internationally relevant, and professionally grounded.

Background and Rationale

By 2040, graduates across every discipline will be required not only to operate AI tools, but to interpret, evaluate and ethically apply them in professional and civic contexts (Robertson, 2025; Centre for Data Ethics and Innovation, 2025). However, the UK higher education sector currently lacks a validated, curriculum-ready mechanism for embedding AI capabilities in a systematic and equitable way across degree programmes (QAA, 2024; Russell Group, 2025). Provision remains highly uneven: structured AI learning is concentrated in computing and engineering, while disciplines such as Business, Law, Health, Education and the Arts receive minimal formal preparation despite rapidly shifting professional expectations.

At the same time, labour-market demands are evolving faster than curricula can adapt, resulting in graduates who are technically familiar with AI tools but insufficiently prepared to exercise critical, contextual and ethical judgement (Robertson, 2025). These gaps are not evenly distributed. Without intentional intervention, inequitable access to AI literacy risks deepening existing participation divides, disproportionately affecting international students, neurodivergent learners and those from widening participation backgrounds, including students in teaching-intensive institutions (HEPI, 2025; Jisc, 2025; Nuffield Foundation, 2025).

In short, AI is on course to become a new axis of stratification within higher education unless the sector develops coherent, scalable and inclusive curriculum tools that extend beyond technical disciplines. This project directly addresses that structural gap by designing, validating and operationalising an Interdisciplinary AI Competency Framework (IAICF) and associated Curriculum Design Toolkit.

The framework is being piloted and usability-tested through national co-design workshops involving programme directors, module leads, and discipline representatives across research-intensive and teaching-intensive universities in the UK, as well as international partner campuses in Dubai, Türkiye and mainland Europe. Outputs will include the competency framework itself, a curriculum toolkit, a compendium of worked discipline case studies, an Advance HE-hosted CPD webinar, and a policy briefing. All resources will be openly available via the Advance HE Knowledge Hub.

The study sits at the intersection of AI literacy, curriculum design, widening participation and educational equity. While a number of digital capability frameworks exist, they operate either at institutional policy level or within technical specialist contexts and do not provide programme teams with a validated, pedagogically usable model that integrates competency development, cognitive accessibility, digital equity and international transferability (Advance HE, 2024; QAA, 2024). By creating a curriculum-ready framework, this project responds to a foundational structural need and equips the sector to prepare graduates for an economy, and a society, in which AI capability will be a baseline expectation rather than an optional advantage.

The IAICF Framework

The IAICF framework is structured around six domains, collectively encompassing 32 ratified competencies that together define what it means for a higher education graduate to be AI-literate, AI-capable, and AI-responsible across disciplinary and professional contexts. Of these, 28 are Universal (expected across all disciplines), 3 are Contextual (applied where directly relevant to the discipline), and 1 is Arts and Humanities scoped. Each competency is presented with a full rationale, a competency statement, FHEQ Level 4 to Level 8 descriptors, and disciplinary contextualisation notes for each of five discipline groups.

Universal Competency

A competency ratified by the National Interdisciplinary Panel as applicable and relevant across all disciplines in higher education. Universal competencies form the core of the IAICF framework and should be embedded in every programme regardless of subject area.

Contextual Competency

A competency ratified as applicable within specific disciplinary, professional, or institutional contexts rather than universally. Contextual competencies are recommended for adoption in relevant programmes and settings where they directly address domain-specific professional responsibilities.

Arts & Humanities Scoped

A competency ratified by the Arts and Humanities panel as specifically scoped to that discipline group, where its framing, terminology, and assessment expectations are aligned with arts and humanities scholarly and creative practice.

The six domains, their descriptions, and the complete list of ratified competencies are:

Domain 1
Conceptual Understanding of Artificial Intelligence Systems
4 competencies · Focus: This domain develops foundational knowledge of how artificial intelligence systems work - their technical construction, the role of data in shaping their behaviour, the diversity of model types, their...
4 Universal
1.1 Understanding How Artificial Intelligence Systems Are Built and Trained by Data · 1.2 Understanding Different Artificial Intelligence Model Types and Their Applications · 1.3 Identifying Fundamental Limitations and Failure Modes of Artificial Intelligence Systems · 1.4 Recognising How Artificial Intelligence Shapes Everyday Services and Decisions
Domain 2
Critical Evaluation of AI Outputs and System Performance
6 competencies · Focus: This domain develops the ability to critically assess what artificial intelligence systems produce - evaluating factual accuracy, detecting bias, analysing logical consistency, assessing contextual ap...
6 Universal
2.1 Evaluating the Accuracy and Reliability of Artificial Intelligence-Generated Information · 2.2 Detecting Bias and Unequal Representation in Artificial Intelligence Outputs · 2.3 Analysing the Logical Consistency of Artificial Intelligence-Generated Reasoning · 2.4 Assessing Artificial Intelligence Outputs for Professional Tone and Contextual Fit · 2.5 Verifying the Authenticity of Artificial Intelligence-Generated Synthetic Media · 2.6 Evaluating the Credibility of Information and Claims About Artificial Intelligence
Domain 3
Ethical and Social Implications of Artificial Intelligence
7 competencies · Focus: This domain develops understanding of the societal, ethical, and human impacts of artificial intelligence technologies. Competencies address privacy and personal data, environmental sustainability, la...
6 Universal 1 Contextual
3.1 Evaluating Personal Data Footprints Within Artificial Intelligence Systems · 3.2 Assessing the Environmental Impact and Sustainability of Artificial Intelligence Technologies · 3.3 Examining Artificial Intelligence's Broader Impact on Work and Society · 3.4 Preserving Human Agency and Decision-Making Authority in Artificial Intelligence-Assisted Processes · 3.5 Analysing Artificial Intelligence's Impact on Information Integrity and Public Discourse · 3.6 Recognising the Psycho-Emotional Effect of Human-Like Artificial Intelligence Responses on Human Users (Contextual) · 3.7 Identifying Professional Contexts Where Artificial Intelligence Use Is Inappropriate
Domain 4
Practical Application and Professional Use of Artificial Intelligence Tools
4 competencies · Focus: This domain develops the ability to use artificial intelligence tools effectively, critically, and responsibly in professional and academic workflows. Competencies address prompt design, tool selectio...
4 Universal
4.1 Designing Effective Prompts and Queries for Artificial Intelligence Systems · 4.2 Selecting and Integrating Artificial Intelligence Tools in Professional Workflows · 4.3 Using Artificial Intelligence as a Tool Whilst Preserving Critical Judgement · 4.4 Diagnosing and Resolving Artificial Intelligence Failures and Unhelpful Outputs in Practice
Domain 5
Governance, Accountability, and Regulation of Artificial Intelligence
7 competencies · Focus: This domain develops understanding of how artificial intelligence systems should be governed, regulated, and ethically managed within organisations and society. Competencies address examining AI tools...
5 Universal 2 Contextual
5.1 Examining Artificial Intelligence Tools for Trustworthiness, Transparency, and Reliability · 5.2 Disclosing and Documenting Artificial Intelligence Use and Contributions in Academic and Professional Work · 5.3 Interpreting Relevant Legal and Regulatory Frameworks Governing Artificial Intelligence · 5.4 Applying Professional Ethics and Sector-Specific Codes to Artificial Intelligence Use · 5.5 Evaluating Intellectual Property, Copyright, and Training Data Ethics in Artificial Intelligence · 5.6 Reflecting on Personal and Professional Accountability in Artificial Intelligence-Assisted Work (Contextual) · 5.7 Understanding Artificial Intelligence Governance and Regulatory Responsibilities in Professional Practice (Contextual)
Domain 6
Cognitive Diversity, Accessibility, and Inclusive AI Literacy
4 competencies · Focus: This domain develops understanding of how artificial intelligence technologies can be used inclusively and accessibly across diverse cognitive profiles, linguistic backgrounds, and learning needs. Com...
3 Universal 1 Arts & Humanities Scoped
6.1 Inclusive and Adaptive Artificial Intelligence Use · 6.3 Evaluating Artificial Intelligence Tools for Equity and Inclusive Access · 6.4 Reflecting on Artificial Intelligence's Impact on Personal Learning and Cognitive Development · 6.AH Using Artificial Intelligence to Participate Equitably in Artistic and Humanistic Expression and Assessment (A/H scoped)
Note on the Arts and Humanities scoped competency: Competency 6.AH ("Participating in and Designing Inclusive AI-Integrated Assessments") is explicitly scoped to Arts and Humanities. The Arts and Humanities panel ratified it as a competency addressing equitable participation in artistic and humanistic expression and assessment through AI, with framing that reflects studio, critique, and portfolio-based assessment cultures.
Phase 3 · IAICF Curriculum Co-design
National Curriculum Prototyping and Co-design Workshop
A national, discipline-representative co-design effort translating the ratified IAICF v3.0 framework into usable, equitable, implementable curriculum. Running 21 April to 15 May 2026 across three countries and seven host universities.
Dates
21 Apr - 15 May 2026
Duration
3 hours per session
Activities
3 co-design canvases
Disciplines
5 discipline groups
Output
IAICF v3.1 + Toolkit
Where Phase 3 sits in the IAICF programme

The IAICF project was developed in three sequential phases under the Advance HE Collaborative Development Fund.

Phase 1 · National Delphi Survey
856
Expressions of Interest
622
Completed Surveys
61
UK Universities
The Phase 1 survey was a substantial instrument of over 140 questions, assessing the proposed framework across several dimensions: importance and relevance of each competency, scope and applicability, interdimensional coherence, clarity of articulation, and achievability across FHEQ levels. Participants rated each competency against these criteria and were invited to propose revisions, additions, or mergers. The rigorous length and structure produced a high-resolution evidence base for Phase 2 panel deliberations.
Phase 2 · National Expert Panels (13 to 15 April 2026)
Five discipline-specific National Expert Panels were convened, running for over 10 hours in total (approximately 2+ hours per discipline). 150 academics attended across the five panels to deliberate on the framework within their disciplinary context.
32
Physical Science,
Engineering & Maths
26
Arts &
Humanities
28
Education
34
Social Sciences,
Law & Business
30
Medicine, Health
& Life Sciences
103
Interdisciplinary Panellists
Following the five discipline panels, a 3-hour National Interdisciplinary Panel convened 103 academics to ratify competencies across disciplines, finalise tier classifications (Universal, Contextual, Arts and Humanities scoped), and resolve cross-disciplinary tensions in framework architecture.
Together the panels contributed over 13 hours of structured expert deliberation with 153 expert participants in total, producing the ratified IAICF v3.0 framework: 32 competencies in FHEQ Level 4 to Level 8 descriptor format, with disciplinary contextualisation notes for each of five discipline groups.
Phase 3 · National Curriculum Prototyping and Co-design Workshop
Phase 3, which this dashboard accompanies, shifts the work from framework ratification to curriculum translation. The ratified framework describes what students should be able to do; Phase 3 asks how that translates into a module, a programme, an assessment strategy, an inclusive teaching practice.

Countries hosting
the workshops

participants

Co-design
activities
What the workshops are for

Each workshop runs for three hours and is organised around three outcomes, each anchored by one of the co-design canvases:

Outcome 1
Framework Audit
First Impressions Audit · 5-7 min
Audit the ratified framework for title clarity, assessability, and contextual assumptions from the perspective of the participant's own teaching context.
Outcome 2
Module Mapping
Activity 1B · 50 min
Map a subset of IAICF competencies onto a real module specification that the participant brings to the workshop, producing a one-semester implementation plan that can be taken away and acted upon.
Outcome 3
Equity Review
Activity 3 · 30 min
Examine the framework for structural equity barriers across six dimensions (access, prior knowledge, language, neurodiversity, culture, structural exclusion) and propose revisions or implementation mitigations.
Structure of the three-hour session
0:00 - 0:15
Welcome, consent refresh, framework overview
0:15 - 0:25
First Impressions Audit · individual, silent reading
0:25 - 1:15
Activity 1B: Module-level mapping · individual with facilitator support
1:15 - 1:30
Break
1:30 - 2:00
Activity 3: Equity Review · individual, then small-group synthesis
2:00 - 2:45
Group discussion · cross-cutting themes, implementation blockers
2:45 - 3:00
Closing synthesis · participant commitments, next steps
Workshop Schedule by Host University

Seven host universities across three countries, running from 21 April to 15 May 2026. Each workshop runs as a self-contained three-hour session; all produce data that feeds into the IAICF v3.1 patch and Implementation Toolkit.

How participant input feeds into IAICF v3.1

All three activity canvases produce structured, anonymisable data that is analysed across the full workshop cohort after 15 May 2026. First Impressions Audit responses surface title and descriptor clarity issues. Activity 1B outputs become a library of worked module mappings that is released as an open implementation resource. Activity 3 outputs become the equity audit log that drives targeted revisions to competency wording, scoping notes, and the forthcoming Implementation Toolkit. A v3.1 patch release of the framework and the Implementation Toolkit itself are both due for sector publication later in 2026.

Consent, ethics, and confidentiality

Phase 3 operates under University of Birmingham ethics approval (Reference: ERN_5490-Feb2026). All participation is voluntary, all outputs are anonymised before analysis and publication, and participants may withdraw their contributions at any point up to publication. The Information Sheet and Consent Form issued at registration remain in force; participants who wish to review them may request copies from the Principal Investigator.

Principal Investigator: Dr Ehsan Khavandkar (University of Birmingham, moving to University College London on 1 May 2026). Correspondence: e.khavandkar@bham.ac.uk / e.khavandkar@ucl.ac.uk (from 1 May 2026).
Domain 1

Conceptual Understanding of Artificial Intelligence Systems

Domain Description

This domain develops foundational knowledge of how artificial intelligence systems work - their technical construction, the role of data in shaping their behaviour, the diversity of model types, their inherent limitations, and their invisible presence in everyday digital life. Competencies in this domain underpin all critical evaluation, ethical reasoning, and practical application developed across the framework.

Competencies in this domain: 4  ·  4 Universal
1.1 Understanding How Artificial Intelligence Systems Are Built and Trained by Data
Universal
Specialist title: Understanding the Data-Driven Architecture and Training Processes of Artificial Intelligence Systems
Rationale
Artificial intelligence systems are not intelligent in any human sense. They are statistical systems that learn patterns from large volumes of data and produce outputs based on those patterns. Students who understand how data is collected, prepared, and used to train AI systems are better equipped to question why a system behaves as it does, where its outputs might be unreliable, and why human oversight remains essential. This foundational understanding underpins every other competency in the framework - without it, critical evaluation, ethical reasoning, and responsible use lack conceptual grounding.
Competency Statement
Explain how artificial intelligence systems function as data-driven statistical models, understanding the stages through which data is collected, prepared, and used to train systems, and recognising how design decisions and training data fundamentally shape system behaviour and outputs.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Describe how artificial intelligence systems use training data to generate outputs rather than applying human reasoning or independent knowledge.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Explain how the quality and composition of training data influence the reliability and limitations of artificial intelligence outputs.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Examine how specific design decisions and training data choices shape artificial intelligence system behaviour within a disciplinary context.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Evaluate how training data construction and modelling choices affect the reliability and risk of artificial intelligence outputs in professional settings.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Critique how statistical learning processes in artificial intelligence shape knowledge production and introduce systematic limitations across disciplines.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes
Physical Science, Engineering and Mathematics
The data lifecycle and model training dimensions of this competency connect naturally to existing disciplinary knowledge of datasets, experimental design, and computational modelling. Students can contextualise AI training processes through familiar examples such as predictive modelling in physics simulations, material property prediction, or engineering fault detection systems. The emphasis on statistical rather than rule-based reasoning is particularly relevant given the discipline's existing engagement with probabilistic and data-driven methods.
Arts and Humanities
The most relevant dimension for this discipline is understanding how training data composition shapes what AI systems can and cannot represent - including whose voices, cultural traditions, and creative works are present or absent in training datasets. Students can contextualise this through examples such as large language models trained predominantly on Western literary traditions, or image generation systems that reproduce culturally specific visual norms. The design decision dimension connects naturally to questions of representation and cultural power.
Education
The training data and design decision dimensions are most relevant here, particularly in relation to AI systems used in educational technology - adaptive learning platforms, automated feedback tools, and personalised recommendation systems. Students can examine how training data drawn from particular learner populations shapes what these systems can and cannot do for diverse cohorts. The foundational nature of this competency makes it particularly important for students who will design or evaluate educational AI tools professionally.
Social Sciences, Law and Business
The statistical nature of AI outputs and the role of training data in shaping system behaviour are most relevant to this discipline group, particularly in relation to AI systems used in social research, legal analysis, and business decision-making. Students can contextualise through examples such as predictive policing algorithms, credit scoring systems, or automated recruitment tools, examining how training data drawn from historically biased datasets produces systematically biased outputs.
Medicine, Health and Life Sciences
The data lifecycle and training process dimensions connect directly to clinical and research contexts - diagnostic AI systems, drug discovery models, and patient outcome prediction tools are all built and trained in ways that directly affect their reliability and limitations. Students can contextualise through examples such as medical imaging AI trained predominantly on data from specific demographic groups, producing systems that perform less reliably across diverse patient populations. The emphasis on human oversight is particularly resonant in clinical practice contexts.
1.2 Understanding Different Artificial Intelligence Model Types and Their Applications
Universal
Specialist title: Understanding Different AI Model Types and Their Applications
Rationale
Artificial intelligence encompasses distinct model types that operate differently and suit different tasks. Generative models produce content, predictive models forecast outcomes, classification models categorise information, and reinforcement learning models optimise through feedback. Students who can distinguish between these types are better equipped to evaluate whether a system is appropriate for a specific task, interpret its outputs correctly, and recognise when a different approach would serve better. This understanding supports informed and responsible AI tool selection across all disciplinary and professional contexts.
Competency Statement
Identify and compare major artificial intelligence model types - including generative, predictive, classification, and reinforcement learning models - explaining their applications, strengths, and limitations, and recognising how different model architectures influence the reliability and suitability of AI-supported decisions.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Name common artificial intelligence model types, describing how each supports different tasks in everyday or disciplinary contexts.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Explain how different artificial intelligence model types operate and compare their typical applications and limitations.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Differentiate between artificial intelligence model architectures, assessing their suitability for specific disciplinary tasks and professional applications.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Evaluate how different artificial intelligence model types influence decision-making quality and risk in professional contexts.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Critique how artificial intelligence model design choices affect interpretability, reliability, and knowledge generation across disciplines.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes
Physical Science, Engineering and Mathematics
Predictive and classification models are most immediately relevant here - students can contextualise through examples such as molecular property prediction, structural fault classification, or environmental monitoring systems. The reinforcement learning dimension connects to optimisation problems in engineering design and computational science. The emphasis on model suitability and reliability assessment aligns naturally with existing disciplinary norms around experimental validity and computational accuracy.
Arts and Humanities
Generative models are most immediately relevant - large language models for text generation, image synthesis models, and audio generation systems all directly affect creative and scholarly practice. Students can contextualise through examining how generative AI reproduces or distorts cultural and linguistic patterns, and what the limitations of generative models mean for their use in creative, critical, and interpretive work. The question of what model architecture is appropriate for a humanities task is a natural entry point for this discipline.
Education
Classification and predictive models are most relevant in educational technology contexts - learning analytics platforms, automated assessment tools, and early warning systems for student risk all rely on classification and prediction. Students can examine how these model types work, what their limitations are, and what this means for how much trust should be placed in AI-generated educational recommendations. Generative models are also increasingly relevant in relation to AI writing assistants and feedback tools.
Social Sciences, Law and Business
Predictive and classification models are most relevant here - credit scoring, automated legal document analysis, social media sentiment classification, and economic forecasting all use these model types. Students can contextualise through examining how predictive models are used in policy analysis, legal research, and business intelligence, and what their limitations mean for the reliability of AI-assisted professional decisions. The generative model dimension is increasingly relevant in relation to AI-assisted report writing and policy analysis tools.
Medicine, Health and Life Sciences
All four model types have direct clinical and research applications. Classification models support diagnostic imaging and pathology. Predictive models support patient outcome forecasting and treatment planning. Generative models are increasingly used in drug discovery and synthetic data generation. Reinforcement learning is used in treatment optimisation and robotic surgery systems. Students can contextualise through examining the specific model type underlying a clinical AI system they encounter in practice, assessing its limitations and appropriate use.
1.3 Identifying Fundamental Limitations and Failure Modes of Artificial Intelligence Systems
Universal
Specialist title: Understanding Fundamental Limitations and Failure Modes of AI Systems
Rationale
Artificial intelligence systems fail in characteristic ways that stem from their statistical nature rather than from bugs or errors that can be fixed. They generate plausible but incorrect information, fail unpredictably outside their training contexts, confuse correlation with causation, and express inappropriate confidence in uncertain outputs. Students who can identify these failure modes are less likely to over-rely on AI outputs and better equipped to maintain the critical oversight that responsible AI use requires. This competency provides the conceptual foundation for the critical evaluation skills developed throughout Domain 2.
Competency Statement
Identify the inherent limitations of artificial intelligence systems arising from their statistical design, including hallucination, brittleness in unfamiliar contexts, inability to reason causally, and overconfident outputs, recognising why these limitations make persistent human oversight necessary across all applications.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Recognise common artificial intelligence failure modes such as hallucination and overconfident outputs, observing that these differ fundamentally from human errors.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Explain why artificial intelligence systems produce characteristic failures such as hallucination and brittleness, connecting these to their statistical design.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Analyse how specific artificial intelligence limitations affect reliability and appropriate use within a chosen disciplinary context.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Evaluate how architectural constraints in artificial intelligence systems determine appropriate use cases and necessary safeguards in professional settings.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Examine the epistemological implications of artificial intelligence's statistical nature for knowledge reliability across disciplinary and professional contexts.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes
Physical Science, Engineering and Mathematics
The brittleness and causal reasoning limitations are most relevant here - AI systems trained on one physical context failing unpredictably in another, or systems confusing correlated variables with causal relationships in experimental data, are directly applicable concerns. Students can contextualise through examples such as materials property prediction models failing outside their training distribution, or AI-assisted data analysis confusing statistical correlation with physical causation. The emphasis on persistent human oversight aligns with existing disciplinary norms around experimental verification and peer review.
Arts and Humanities
Hallucination is the most immediately relevant failure mode - large language models generating plausible but fabricated citations, misattributing quotations, or producing historically inaccurate accounts with inappropriate confidence directly affects scholarly practice. Students can contextualise through examining AI-generated literary analysis, historical summaries, or critical arguments that sound authoritative but contain fundamental errors. The inability to reason causally is also relevant to humanities disciplines that engage with complex historical, cultural, and interpretive causation.
Education
All failure modes are relevant in educational technology contexts, but overconfidence is particularly significant - AI systems that present incorrect feedback with high confidence can actively mislead learners. Students can contextualise through examining automated essay feedback tools, AI tutoring systems, and adaptive learning platforms, assessing what happens when these systems encounter student work that falls outside their training distribution. The persistent human oversight dimension is directly relevant to professional responsibilities around student welfare and academic integrity.
Social Sciences, Law and Business
The causal reasoning limitation is most critically relevant here - social science, legal analysis, and business intelligence all depend on distinguishing correlation from causation, and AI systems that confuse the two in policy analysis, legal research, or market forecasting can produce seriously misleading professional outputs. Students can contextualise through examples such as AI systems identifying spurious correlations in social datasets, or legal AI tools hallucinating case citations. The brittleness dimension is relevant to AI systems applied in novel social or regulatory contexts.
Medicine, Health and Life Sciences
All failure modes carry direct patient safety implications. Hallucination in clinical AI systems - generating plausible but incorrect diagnoses or drug interaction information - is a critical safety concern. Brittleness means diagnostic AI trained on one patient population may fail unpredictably with different demographic groups. Overconfidence in uncertain predictions directly affects clinical decision-making quality. Students can contextualise through examining specific clinical AI tools and assessing what their failure modes mean for appropriate use and the necessity of clinical oversight.
1.4 Recognising How Artificial Intelligence Shapes Everyday Services and Decisions
Universal
Specialist title: Identifying the Subtle Presence of AI in Everyday Digital Services and Decision-Making
Rationale
Artificial intelligence is increasingly embedded invisibly within everyday digital services - shaping search results, recommendations, financial assessments, and automated decisions without explicit disclosure to users. Understanding why systems are designed to operate invisibly, and how this invisibility affects individual access to information and opportunity, is a foundational conceptual competency. Students who recognise how algorithmic systems are architecturally embedded in digital infrastructure are better equipped to question the systems shaping their experiences and to engage critically with AI as informed citizens and professionals.
Competency Statement
Recognise the presence of artificial intelligence embedded within everyday digital services, explaining how algorithmic systems shape decisions, information access, and user experiences without explicit disclosure, and understanding the design and architectural reasons behind this invisible operation.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Identify examples of artificial intelligence operating within everyday digital services, recognising that algorithmic systems influence daily experiences without explicit disclosure.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Explain how embedded artificial intelligence systems shape information exposure and automated decisions in common digital platforms.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Analyse how artificial intelligence embedded in digital infrastructure influences access to information, services, and opportunities in a disciplinary context.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Evaluate how invisible algorithmic systems shape social behaviour, information flows, and institutional decision-making in professional and organisational contexts.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Examine how embedded artificial intelligence systems structurally shape patterns of information access and opportunity distribution across social contexts.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes
Physical Science, Engineering and Mathematics
The most relevant dimension here is understanding how AI is embedded in research infrastructure - recommendation algorithms in academic databases, automated peer review screening tools, and AI-assisted literature discovery systems all shape what knowledge scientists and engineers encounter. Students can also examine how AI is embedded in the digital tools and platforms central to their professional practice, from engineering design software to scientific data repositories.
Arts and Humanities
The most relevant dimension is how AI embedded in digital platforms shapes cultural consumption, information exposure, and creative discovery - recommendation algorithms on streaming platforms, search engine personalisation, and social media curation all influence which cultural artefacts, perspectives, and voices reach audiences. Students can examine how these systems embed particular cultural assumptions and whose work is made visible or invisible by algorithmic curation.
Education
The most relevant dimension is how AI is embedded in educational platforms and institutional systems - virtual learning environments, library search tools, student information systems, and administrative platforms all use embedded AI in ways that shape what students and educators encounter. Students can examine how algorithmic systems in educational technology influence learning experiences and institutional decision-making in ways that are not always visible to those affected.
Social Sciences, Law and Business
The most relevant dimension is how AI is embedded in the systems that mediate social and economic life - credit scoring, insurance pricing, recruitment screening, social media moderation, and public service allocation all use embedded AI that affects life opportunities without explicit disclosure. Students can contextualise through examining how these systems operate in their disciplinary field, assessing their implications for equity, accountability, and professional practice.
Medicine, Health and Life Sciences
The most relevant dimension is how AI is embedded in clinical and administrative healthcare systems - appointment scheduling, triage support, diagnostic imaging analysis, and electronic health record systems all use embedded AI that shapes clinical workflows and patient experiences. Students can examine how these systems operate in healthcare settings they will encounter professionally, assessing what their invisible operation means for clinical accountability and patient rights.
Domain 2

Critical Evaluation of AI Outputs and System Performance

Domain Description

This domain develops the ability to critically assess what artificial intelligence systems produce - evaluating factual accuracy, detecting bias, analysing logical consistency, assessing contextual appropriateness, verifying authenticity of synthetic media, and evaluating the credibility of information about AI itself. Competencies in this domain build a suite of critical evaluation skills that apply across all disciplinary and professional contexts where AI-generated outputs inform decisions, communication, or analysis.

Competencies in this domain: 6  ·  6 Universal
2.1 Evaluating the Accuracy and Reliability of Artificial Intelligence-Generated Information
Universal
Specialist title: Evaluating and Verifying the Accuracy and Reliability of AI-Generated Information
Rationale
Artificial intelligence systems generate outputs from statistical patterns rather than verified knowledge, producing factual errors, outdated information, and fabricated references presented with unwarranted confidence. Students who systematically verify AI-generated information against authoritative sources before accepting it develop a critical habit that protects the integrity of their academic and professional work. This competency establishes the foundational verification practice that underpins responsible use of artificial intelligence across all disciplines and professional contexts.
Competency Statement
Evaluate the factual accuracy and reliability of artificial intelligence-generated information by examining knowledge boundaries and evidence quality, applying verification practices that cross-reference AI outputs against authoritative sources before using them in academic analysis, professional communication, or evidence-based decisions.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Recognise that artificial intelligence outputs may contain factual errors, applying basic fact-checking against reliable sources before accepting claims.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Explain how training data limitations affect factual accuracy in artificial intelligence outputs, applying verification strategies to confirm credibility.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Assess the reliability of artificial intelligence-generated information by evaluating evidence quality and cross-referencing claims against disciplinary sources.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Apply structured verification frameworks to evaluate artificial intelligence-generated claims against professional standards for evidence-based analysis.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Critique methodological approaches to evaluating factual accuracy in artificial intelligence outputs within research and policy contexts.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes
Physical Science, Engineering and Mathematics
Verification practices connect naturally to existing disciplinary norms around experimental replication and peer-reviewed evidence. Students can apply this competency by cross-referencing AI-generated technical information - such as material properties, mathematical solutions, or scientific facts - against primary literature, established databases, and computational verification tools. The emphasis on systematic verification aligns with scientific method principles already central to this discipline.
Arts and Humanities
Verification is particularly critical here because hallucinated citations, misattributed quotations, and historically inaccurate claims are common in AI-generated humanities content. Students can apply this competency by checking AI-generated literary references against primary texts, verifying historical claims against archival sources, and cross-referencing AI-produced critical arguments against established scholarly interpretations. The discipline's existing tradition of close reading and source criticism provides a natural foundation.
Education
Verification practices are directly relevant to the professional responsibility of educators who use AI-generated content in teaching materials. Students can apply this competency by checking AI-generated explanations, learning objectives, and curriculum content against authoritative educational sources, professional standards documents, and subject-specific guidance. The competency also applies to evaluating AI-generated student feedback for factual accuracy before it reaches learners.
Social Sciences, Law and Business
Verification is particularly critical in legal and policy contexts where AI-generated information may contain fabricated case citations, inaccurate statistics, or outdated regulatory information. Students can apply this competency by cross-referencing AI-generated legal information against primary legislation and case law, verifying social science claims against peer-reviewed research, and checking business data against authoritative market sources. The professional consequences of relying on unverified AI outputs are particularly significant in these regulated contexts.
Medicine, Health and Life Sciences
Verification is a direct patient safety concern here. AI-generated clinical information - drug interactions, diagnostic criteria, treatment protocols - must be verified against current clinical guidelines, pharmacological databases, and peer-reviewed evidence before informing any clinical decision. Students can apply this competency within the existing evidence-based practice framework central to health professional education, treating AI outputs as preliminary information sources requiring the same critical appraisal as any other evidence.
2.2 Detecting Bias and Unequal Representation in Artificial Intelligence Outputs
Universal
Specialist title: Detecting Bias and Unequal Representation in AI Outputs
Rationale
Artificial intelligence systems reproduce and amplify biases embedded in their training data, producing outputs that may reflect gender stereotypes, racial prejudices, cultural misrepresentations, or socioeconomic assumptions. Students who can detect bias in AI outputs are equipped to identify when automated conclusions unfairly represent populations or perpetuate systemic inequalities. This competency focuses specifically on bias within AI-generated content - recommendations, language, images, and decisions - rather than on whether AI tools themselves are accessible, which is addressed in Domain 6.
Competency Statement
Identify and analyse evidence of bias, stereotyping, and unequal representation in artificial intelligence outputs, examining how training data composition and algorithmic processes produce outputs that may marginalise groups or reflect historical prejudices, and assessing whether outputs are sufficiently fair for professional use.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Observe examples where artificial intelligence outputs reflect stereotypes or limited representation, recognising that training data shapes these patterns.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Explain how training data composition produces bias in artificial intelligence outputs, comparing examples across different demographic contexts.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Analyse artificial intelligence outputs for evidence of bias and representational limitations, assessing their fairness for disciplinary or professional use.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Evaluate bias and representational limitations in artificial intelligence outputs, proposing strategies to detect and mitigate unfair results in professional decision-making.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Develop approaches for investigating bias and representation in artificial intelligence outputs, contributing to equitable evaluation across diverse populations.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes
Physical Science, Engineering and Mathematics
Bias detection is relevant in relation to AI systems used in research - datasets that underrepresent certain experimental conditions, geographic regions, or material types produce models with systematic blind spots. Students can apply this competency by examining AI tools used in their discipline for evidence of training data gaps that affect output reliability across different scientific contexts. Engineering applications - such as facial recognition systems or autonomous vehicle sensors that perform less reliably for certain demographic groups - provide concrete contextualisation.
Arts and Humanities
Bias detection is most immediately relevant here in relation to how AI systems represent or misrepresent cultural, linguistic, and historical diversity. Students can examine how large language models reproduce Western cultural assumptions, how image generation systems reflect gender and racial stereotypes, and how automated translation tools perform unequally across languages. The discipline's existing engagement with representation, power, and cultural critique provides a rich foundation for applying this competency analytically.
Education
Bias in AI outputs has direct implications for equitable learning - automated feedback tools that perform differently for students from different linguistic backgrounds, or learning recommendation systems that reflect existing educational inequalities, directly affect student outcomes. Students can apply this competency by examining educational AI tools for evidence of differential performance across student groups, assessing implications for equitable access to high-quality automated support.
Social Sciences, Law and Business
This is one of the most directly relevant competencies for this discipline group. AI systems used in recruitment, credit scoring, criminal justice, and social welfare allocation have well-documented bias problems with serious social consequences. Students can apply this competency by examining specific AI systems used in their disciplinary field - predictive policing algorithms, automated CV screening tools, social media content moderation systems - assessing evidence of bias and its implications for social justice and professional responsibility.
Medicine, Health and Life Sciences
Bias in clinical AI outputs has direct patient safety and health equity implications. Diagnostic AI systems trained predominantly on data from particular demographic groups perform less reliably for underrepresented populations, potentially reinforcing existing health inequalities. Students can apply this competency by examining clinical AI tools for evidence of differential performance across patient groups, assessing implications for equitable clinical practice and the professional responsibility to provide fair care regardless of demographic background.
2.3 Analysing the Logical Consistency of Artificial Intelligence-Generated Reasoning
Universal
Specialist title: Interrogating the Logical Consistency and Reasoning Chains of AI Solutions
Rationale
Artificial intelligence systems generate explanations that appear logically structured while containing flawed reasoning. Because these systems predict plausible sequences of text rather than performing genuine logical inference, they produce arguments that sound coherent but may contain unsupported assumptions, circular reasoning, or misleading steps. Students who can analyse AI-generated reasoning protect themselves from being persuaded by superficially coherent but ultimately flawed arguments, maintaining the critical thinking standards that disciplinary and professional work demands.
Competency Statement
Analyse the reasoning presented by artificial intelligence systems, identifying logical fallacies, unsupported assumptions, circular reasoning, and misleading steps that may produce convincing but incorrect conclusions in academic, analytical, or professional decision-making contexts.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Recognise when artificial intelligence explanations contain unclear reasoning or unsupported claims that require checking against established knowledge.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Explain how artificial intelligence systems produce flawed reasoning despite appearing coherent, identifying logical gaps in familiar disciplinary contexts.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Analyse reasoning chains in artificial intelligence outputs, identifying fallacies and evaluating whether conclusions follow logically from premises.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Evaluate the logical validity of artificial intelligence-generated reasoning against professional and disciplinary standards for evidence-based decision-making.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Develop approaches for assessing reasoning structures in artificial intelligence outputs, advancing understanding of logical inference limitations across disciplines.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes
Physical Science, Engineering and Mathematics
Logical consistency analysis connects directly to mathematical proof verification, scientific argument evaluation, and engineering problem-solving. Students can apply this competency by examining AI-generated mathematical solutions for logical gaps, checking AI-produced scientific explanations against established physical laws, and evaluating AI-assisted engineering analyses for unsupported assumptions. The discipline's existing emphasis on formal reasoning and proof provides a strong foundation for identifying when AI-generated reasoning fails to meet disciplinary logical standards.
Arts and Humanities
Critical analysis of argument structure is central to humanities scholarship, making this competency a natural extension of existing disciplinary skills. Students can apply it by examining AI-generated literary interpretations, historical arguments, and philosophical analyses for logical consistency - identifying when AI produces plausible-sounding but internally inconsistent arguments, or when conclusions do not follow from the evidence presented. The discipline's tradition of close critical reading applies directly to reading AI-generated reasoning critically.
Education
Logical consistency in AI-generated explanations is directly relevant to pedagogical quality - explanations that sound clear but contain logical gaps can actively confuse learners. Students can apply this competency by evaluating AI-generated learning materials, explanations, and feedback for logical coherence, assessing whether the reasoning presented would genuinely support student understanding or introduce misconceptions. This connects directly to professional responsibilities around curriculum quality and learning design.
Social Sciences, Law and Business
Legal reasoning, policy analysis, and social science argumentation all depend on logical validity, making this a particularly high-stakes competency in these professional contexts. Students can apply it by examining AI-generated legal arguments for logical consistency, evaluating AI-produced policy analyses for unsupported causal claims, and checking AI-assisted business cases for circular reasoning. The professional consequences of relying on logically flawed AI-generated arguments in legal or policy contexts are particularly significant.
Medicine, Health and Life Sciences
Clinical reasoning depends on logical validity - AI systems that produce plausible-sounding but logically flawed clinical arguments could support poor patient care decisions. Students can apply this competency by examining AI-generated differential diagnoses, treatment recommendations, and clinical summaries for logical consistency, assessing whether conclusions follow from the evidence presented and whether causal claims are appropriately supported. This connects directly to evidence-based practice frameworks central to health professional education.
2.4 Assessing Artificial Intelligence Outputs for Professional Tone and Contextual Fit
Universal
Specialist title: Evaluating AI Outputs for Professional Tone, Cultural Sensitivity, and Contextual Appropriateness
Rationale
Artificial intelligence systems frequently produce outputs that are factually correct but contextually inappropriate - using the wrong register, misjudging audience expectations, overlooking cultural nuances, or ignoring power dynamics. These are not communication preferences but dimensions of output quality that artificial intelligence systems systematically fail to assess. Students who evaluate AI outputs for tone and contextual fit develop a critical quality assessment skill that protects professional credibility and ensures AI supports rather than undermines effective communication across disciplinary and professional contexts.
Competency Statement
Assess whether artificial intelligence outputs match the professional tone, disciplinary conventions, and contextual expectations of specific communication situations, identifying when factually correct outputs require revision to meet standards of professional credibility and contextual appropriateness.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Describe situations where artificial intelligence outputs, though factually correct, require revision to match appropriate professional tone or audience expectations.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Explain how professional context and audience shape appropriate communication, adapting artificial intelligence outputs to better match disciplinary conventions.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Assess artificial intelligence outputs for contextual appropriateness, evaluating tone, register, and audience alignment within a professional or disciplinary setting.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Evaluate artificial intelligence outputs for professional appropriateness in complex organisational contexts, ensuring they meet disciplinary and ethical communication standards.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Examine how contextual and audience factors determine communication quality in artificial intelligence outputs beyond factual accuracy across professional contexts.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes
Physical Science, Engineering and Mathematics
Contextual fit is relevant in relation to technical communication - AI-generated scientific reports, engineering specifications, and research summaries may be factually accurate but use inappropriate levels of technical detail, adopt the wrong register for the intended audience, or fail to meet disciplinary conventions for presenting uncertainty and limitations. Students can apply this competency by evaluating AI-generated technical documents against the communication standards of their specific disciplinary audience, whether that is a specialist research community, a non-specialist client, or a regulatory body.
Arts and Humanities
Tone and contextual fit are particularly nuanced in humanities communication - AI-generated literary analysis, historical narratives, and critical arguments may adopt inappropriate registers, miss disciplinary conventions around hedging and qualification, or produce generic academic prose that lacks the distinctive voice expected in humanities scholarship. Students can apply this competency by evaluating AI-generated academic writing against disciplinary standards for argument, voice, and critical engagement, assessing where AI outputs fail to meet the contextual expectations of humanities scholarly communication.
Education
Tone and contextual fit are directly relevant to professional communication in educational settings - AI-generated feedback to students, communications to parents, reports to senior leadership, and policy documents all require different registers and levels of formality. Students can apply this competency by evaluating AI-generated educational communications against the specific audience and professional context, assessing whether tone, language level, and register are appropriate for the intended recipient and purpose.
Social Sciences, Law and Business
Professional tone is critical in legal, policy, and business communication contexts where inappropriate register can undermine credibility or have professional consequences. Students can apply this competency by evaluating AI-generated legal documents for appropriate formal register, checking AI-produced policy briefs against the conventions of their intended institutional audience, and assessing AI-generated business communications for appropriate professional tone. The stakes of contextually inappropriate communication are particularly high in regulated professional contexts.
Medicine, Health and Life Sciences
Tone and contextual fit are critical in clinical communication - AI-generated patient communications, clinical letters, and discharge summaries must use appropriate language levels, maintain clinical professionalism, and match the communication needs of specific patient populations. Students can apply this competency by evaluating AI-generated clinical documents against the communication standards of their professional context, assessing whether tone, language complexity, and register are appropriate for the intended recipient.
2.5 Verifying the Authenticity of Artificial Intelligence-Generated Synthetic Media
Universal
Specialist title: Detecting and Verifying AI-Generated Deepfakes and Synthetic Media
Rationale
Advances in generative artificial intelligence have made it straightforward to produce highly convincing synthetic media - manipulated video, audio, and images that can be used for fraud, impersonation, or disinformation. Students who can verify the authenticity of digital media before trusting it are better equipped to protect themselves and their organisations from deception. This competency strengthens resilience against AI-enabled manipulation and supports responsible engagement with digital evidence across professional and civic contexts.
Competency Statement
Verify the authenticity of digital media by applying critical observation skills and available verification tools to detect artificial intelligence-generated synthetic content, reducing risks of impersonation, fraud, and disinformation in professional and public communication contexts.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Recognise basic signs that images, audio, or video may be artificially generated, identifying simple indicators that digital media requires verification.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Explain how synthetic media is produced, applying basic verification techniques to question the authenticity of digital content.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Assess digital media authenticity using observation skills and verification tools, evaluating indicators of manipulation and associated risks.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Apply systematic verification methods to assess the credibility of digital evidence and communication materials in professional contexts.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Examine approaches to detecting and verifying synthetic media, contributing to understanding of digital authenticity and resilience against AI-enabled disinformation.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes
Physical Science, Engineering and Mathematics
Synthetic media verification is relevant in research integrity contexts - AI-generated or manipulated research images, falsified experimental data visualisations, and synthetic scientific photographs are emerging concerns in scientific publishing. Students can apply this competency by developing awareness of how synthetic media tools could be used to fabricate research evidence, and by applying verification practices to digital research materials they encounter. The competency also applies to evaluating AI-generated scientific visualisations and simulation outputs for authenticity.
Arts and Humanities
Synthetic media verification has significant implications for historical and cultural research - AI-generated historical photographs, fabricated archival documents, and synthetic audio recordings of historical figures all pose challenges for humanities scholarship. Students can apply this competency by examining digital primary sources for indicators of manipulation or synthesis, connecting verification practices to existing disciplinary skills around source authentication and archival research. The creative dimensions of synthetic media also raise important questions about authorship and cultural authenticity.
Education
Synthetic media verification is relevant to media literacy education and to the professional responsibility of educators to model critical digital citizenship. Students can apply this competency both in their own practice - verifying digital teaching materials and resources before using them - and in supporting learners to develop verification skills. The competency is particularly relevant for educators working with young people who encounter synthetic media in their everyday digital lives.
Social Sciences, Law and Business
Synthetic media verification has direct professional implications in legal evidence, journalism, public relations, and policy communication. AI-generated synthetic media used in legal proceedings, political disinformation campaigns, or corporate fraud are real and growing concerns. Students can apply this competency by developing verification practices appropriate to their professional context - legal admissibility of digital evidence, journalistic verification standards, or organisational communication security protocols.
Medicine, Health and Life Sciences
Synthetic media verification is relevant in clinical communication and medical education contexts - AI-generated medical images, fabricated clinical photographs, and synthetic patient data all pose risks to clinical decision-making and research integrity. Students can apply this competency by verifying digital clinical materials and research images for authenticity, connecting verification practices to existing professional standards around clinical evidence quality and research integrity.
2.6 Evaluating the Credibility of Information and Claims About Artificial Intelligence
Universal
Specialist title: Evaluating Claims and Information About Artificial Intelligence Technologies
Rationale
Public discourse about artificial intelligence is saturated with exaggerated claims, unfounded fears, and conflicting assertions from developers, journalists, and policymakers. Students who can evaluate the credibility of information about AI - distinguishing evidence-based analysis from hype, misinformation, and commercial interest - apply the same critical evaluation skills developed across Domain 2 at a meta-level. This competency completes the domain by extending critical evaluation from AI outputs to the broader information landscape surrounding AI technologies in academic, professional, and civic contexts.
Competency Statement
Evaluate the credibility of information and claims about artificial intelligence by distinguishing evidence-based assessments from speculation, hype, and misinformation, comparing sources for expertise and potential bias, and verifying assertions about AI capabilities, risks, and impacts before accepting them.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Distinguish credible from unreliable claims about artificial intelligence, recognising common exaggerations and misconceptions in media or marketing materials.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Compare claims about artificial intelligence across multiple sources, explaining how evidence quality and source expertise influence reliability.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Evaluate the credibility of claims about artificial intelligence by assessing evidence quality, source expertise, and logical consistency across competing narratives.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Analyse discourse about artificial intelligence in professional and policy contexts, distinguishing evidence-based assessments from speculation and conflicts of interest.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Examine how epistemological uncertainty and commercial interests shape public understanding of artificial intelligence capabilities and societal impacts.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes
Physical Science, Engineering and Mathematics
Evaluating claims about AI capabilities is particularly relevant in relation to vendor assertions about AI tools used in research and engineering practice - claims about the accuracy, reliability, and applicability of AI systems for specific scientific or engineering tasks require the same evidence-based scrutiny applied to any scientific claim. Students can apply this competency by evaluating marketing materials and technical documentation for AI research tools against the evidence standards of their discipline.
Arts and Humanities
Critical evaluation of claims about AI is directly relevant to humanities scholarship on technology, culture, and society. Students can apply this competency by examining media narratives about AI for rhetorical framing and ideological assumptions, evaluating policy claims about AI's cultural impacts against evidence, and applying existing disciplinary skills in source criticism and discourse analysis to information about AI technologies. The humanities tradition of interrogating claims about technology and progress provides a rich foundation.
Education
Evaluating claims about AI in education is directly relevant to professional practice - the educational technology market makes extensive claims about AI-powered learning tools that require critical scrutiny. Students can apply this competency by evaluating vendor claims about educational AI products against peer-reviewed evidence, assessing policy assertions about AI's transformative potential in education, and developing the critical literacy needed to make evidence-informed professional decisions about AI adoption in educational settings.
Social Sciences, Law and Business
Critical evaluation of AI claims is essential for professionals who advise on policy, legal frameworks, and organisational strategy in relation to AI. Students can apply this competency by evaluating regulatory impact assessments of AI technologies, scrutinising business cases for AI adoption, and assessing legal arguments about AI capabilities and limitations. The discipline's existing engagement with evidence-based policy and critical analysis of commercial interests provides a strong foundation.
Medicine, Health and Life Sciences
Critical appraisal of claims about clinical AI tools is a direct extension of the evidence-based practice skills central to health professional education. Students can apply this competency by evaluating published evidence for clinical AI tools using existing critical appraisal frameworks, scrutinising vendor claims about diagnostic AI accuracy against peer-reviewed validation studies, and assessing regulatory submissions for AI medical devices. The existing culture of evidence-based practice provides the strongest disciplinary foundation for this competency across all five groups.
Domain 3

Ethical and Social Implications of Artificial Intelligence

Domain Description

This domain develops understanding of the societal, ethical, and human impacts of artificial intelligence technologies. Competencies address privacy and personal data, environmental sustainability, labour transformation, human agency, information integrity, psycho-emotional responses to AI, and the ethical limits of AI delegation. Together they equip students to engage with AI as responsible professionals and citizens who understand the human consequences of algorithmic systems and can advocate for ethical practice in their disciplinary and organisational contexts.

Competencies in this domain: 7  ·  6 Universal 1 Contextual
3.1 Evaluating Personal Data Footprints Within Artificial Intelligence Systems
Universal
Specialist title: Evaluating Personal Data Footprints in AI Systems
Rationale
Artificial intelligence systems depend on personal data to generate predictions, recommendations, and automated decisions, raising significant concerns about privacy, surveillance, consent, and secondary data use. Students who can evaluate how their own and others' personal data flows through AI systems are better equipped to protect individual rights, identify governance gaps, and advocate for responsible data practices in professional and organisational contexts. This competency connects personal digital experience to broader ethical and legal responsibilities around data protection.
Competency Statement
Evaluate how artificial intelligence systems collect, process, and use personal data, identifying risks related to surveillance, consent, and secondary data use, and applying responsible practices that protect privacy rights in academic, professional, and organisational contexts.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Describe examples of personal data that artificial intelligence systems collect or infer, recognising that AI use involves data that may be stored or redistributed.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Explain how artificial intelligence systems collect and use personal data, identifying privacy risks related to surveillance, consent, and data security.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Assess how artificial intelligence systems process personal data, analysing privacy risks and evaluating safeguards that protect individual rights.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Evaluate the ethical and legal implications of artificial intelligence-driven data use, examining privacy, consent, and governance responsibilities in organisational contexts.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Examine conceptual and policy approaches to personal data governance in artificial intelligence systems, addressing privacy protection and responsible data stewardship.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes
Physical Science, Engineering and Mathematics
Personal data governance is relevant in relation to research data management and the use of AI tools that process experimental or participant data. Students can apply this competency by examining what data AI research tools collect about their usage, understanding the data protection implications of using commercial AI platforms for research purposes, and considering the privacy implications of AI systems used in engineering contexts that process individual user data.
Arts and Humanities
Personal data footprints are relevant in relation to digital humanities research that uses AI to process cultural, historical, or social data about individuals. Students can apply this competency by examining the privacy implications of AI tools used to analyse digitised archives, social media data, or cultural datasets, and considering the ethical responsibilities around processing personal data contained in historical records and digital cultural artefacts.
Education
Personal data governance has direct professional implications in educational settings where AI tools process sensitive student data. Students can apply this competency by examining the data collection practices of educational AI platforms, understanding what student data is processed and how it is used, and developing professional awareness of the legal and ethical obligations around student data protection. This connects directly to professional responsibilities under data protection legislation in educational institutions.
Social Sciences, Law and Business
Personal data governance is central to professional practice across this discipline group. Social researchers processing personal data, legal professionals handling client information, and business analysts working with customer data all have significant data protection responsibilities when using AI tools. Students can apply this competency by examining the data protection implications of AI systems used in their professional field, assessing compliance with relevant legislation, and developing responsible data governance practices for AI-assisted professional work.
Medicine, Health and Life Sciences
Patient data governance is one of the most critical professional responsibilities in this discipline group. AI systems that process patient health data raise significant privacy, consent, and security concerns that directly affect patient rights and clinical trust. Students can apply this competency by examining the data protection implications of clinical AI tools, understanding what patient data these systems collect and how it is used, and developing professional awareness of the legal and ethical obligations around health data protection under relevant legislation and professional codes of conduct.
3.2 Assessing the Environmental Impact and Sustainability of Artificial Intelligence Technologies
Universal
Specialist title: Assessing the Environmental Impact and Sustainability of AI Technologies
Rationale
Training and operating large-scale artificial intelligence systems requires substantial energy consumption, specialised hardware, and significant computational resource, contributing to carbon emissions and environmental strain. As sustainability becomes a professional responsibility across all sectors, students who can assess the environmental implications of AI adoption are better equipped to advocate for responsible technology choices within organisations. This competency focuses on the ethical responsibility to minimise environmental harm, distinct from the technical infrastructure of AI systems addressed in Domain 1.
Competency Statement
