Assess the environmental consequences of artificial intelligence development and deployment, including energy consumption, carbon emissions, and resource demands, advocating for environmentally responsible practices in the selection, use, and governance of AI technologies.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Recognise that artificial intelligence systems consume significant energy and resources, identifying simple examples of their environmental impact.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Explain how artificial intelligence systems contribute to environmental impacts through energy consumption, hardware production, and data centre operation.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Assess the environmental implications of artificial intelligence deployment, analysing energy consumption and carbon emissions associated with specific AI technologies.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Evaluate sustainability challenges associated with artificial intelligence adoption, examining organisational strategies for environmentally responsible technology deployment.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Examine policy and analytical approaches to environmental sustainability in artificial intelligence, addressing responsible technological development and ecological impact.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes
Physical Science, Engineering and Mathematics
Environmental impact assessment connects directly to existing disciplinary engagement with energy systems, computational efficiency, and sustainable engineering. Students can apply this competency by estimating the energy cost of AI tools used in their research - large-scale simulation, data analysis, and model training all carry environmental costs that can be quantified and compared. Engineering students can contextualise through examining the lifecycle environmental impact of AI hardware and data centre infrastructure.
Arts and Humanities
Environmental sustainability of AI is relevant to critical and cultural studies of technology, raising questions about the material costs of digital culture and the environmental implications of AI-generated creative work. Students can apply this competency by examining the carbon cost of generative AI tools used in creative and scholarly practice, and by engaging critically with narratives about AI as an immaterial or inherently clean technology. The discipline's existing engagement with ecological criticism and technology studies provides a strong foundation.
Education
Environmental sustainability of educational AI tools is a relevant professional concern, particularly as institutions adopt AI platforms at scale. Students can apply this competency by assessing the environmental implications of AI tools adopted in their educational context, advocating for environmentally responsible procurement decisions, and developing awareness of how individual AI usage choices aggregate into significant institutional environmental impacts. This connects to the growing professional expectation that educators model environmental responsibility.
Social Sciences, Law and Business
Environmental sustainability of AI is directly relevant to business strategy, policy analysis, and legal frameworks around corporate environmental responsibility. Students can apply this competency by examining how AI adoption affects organisational carbon footprints, assessing the environmental dimensions of AI governance frameworks, and considering the legal and regulatory implications of AI's environmental impact under emerging sustainability legislation. Business students can contextualise through examining ESG reporting requirements in relation to AI infrastructure.
Medicine, Health and Life Sciences
Environmental sustainability is increasingly relevant in healthcare contexts as AI systems are adopted at scale in clinical and research settings. Students can apply this competency by assessing the environmental implications of AI tools used in healthcare - large-scale medical imaging AI, genomics analysis platforms, and drug discovery systems all carry significant energy costs. The growing emphasis on sustainable healthcare practice provides a professional context for advocating environmentally responsible AI adoption in clinical and research settings.
3.3 Examining Artificial Intelligence's Broader Impact on Work and Society
Universal
Specialist title: Examining AI's impact on work and society
Rationale
Artificial intelligence is transforming how work is organised across every sector, automating routine tasks, reshaping professional roles, and creating new forms of human-AI collaboration. Students who examine these transformations are better prepared to adapt to changing labour markets, contribute to organisational strategies that integrate AI responsibly, and advocate for approaches that respect the dignity and value of human work. Understanding AI's societal impact enables graduates to engage with technological change as informed and critical participants rather than passive recipients.
Competency Statement
Examine how artificial intelligence reshapes job roles, professional responsibilities, and required skills across sectors, recognising risks of task displacement alongside opportunities for human-AI collaboration and the emergence of new professional pathways within evolving labour markets.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Observe examples of how artificial intelligence affects work and employment, recognising that automation changes job roles and skill requirements across sectors.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Explain how artificial intelligence reshapes work practices, interpreting how automation and human-AI collaboration influence required skills across professions.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Analyse how artificial intelligence affects labour markets and professional roles, assessing implications for skills development and organisational practice.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Evaluate the societal and organisational implications of artificial intelligence-driven labour transformation, examining strategies for responsible workforce adaptation.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Examine relationships between artificial intelligence, labour market transformation, and evolving professional skill requirements across disciplinary sectors.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes
Physical Science, Engineering and Mathematics
AI's impact on work is directly relevant to engineering and scientific practice - automated data analysis, AI-assisted experimental design, and computational modelling are already reshaping research workflows and professional roles. Students can apply this competency by examining how AI is changing the skills required in their disciplinary field, assessing which professional tasks are being augmented or automated, and considering what uniquely human capabilities remain central to scientific and engineering practice.
Arts and Humanities
AI's impact on creative and cultural labour is a particularly urgent concern for this discipline group - generative AI tools are directly affecting the markets for writing, translation, illustration, and other creative professions. Students can apply this competency by examining how AI is reshaping creative industries and cultural institutions, assessing the implications for professional creative practice, and engaging critically with debates about authorship, creative value, and the future of humanistic work in an AI-enabled economy.
Education
AI's impact on educational work is directly relevant to professional identity and practice - automated marking, AI tutoring systems, and administrative AI tools are reshaping teacher and lecturer roles. Students can apply this competency by examining how AI is changing the professional responsibilities of educators, assessing which pedagogical tasks are being augmented or automated, and considering what uniquely human capabilities remain central to effective teaching and learning support in an AI-enabled educational environment.
Social Sciences, Law and Business
Labour market transformation is a core analytical concern for this discipline group - economists, sociologists, legal scholars, and business strategists all engage directly with AI's impact on work as a research and professional practice topic. Students can apply this competency through disciplinary analysis of labour market data, legal frameworks around automated employment decisions, business strategies for responsible workforce adaptation, and social policy approaches to managing AI-driven labour displacement.
Medicine, Health and Life Sciences
AI's impact on healthcare work is directly relevant to professional identity and scope of practice - diagnostic AI, robotic surgery systems, and automated administrative tools are reshaping clinical roles and responsibilities. Students can apply this competency by examining how AI is changing professional responsibilities in their specific health discipline, assessing which clinical tasks are being augmented or automated, and considering what uniquely human capabilities - therapeutic relationship, clinical judgement, patient advocacy - remain irreplaceable in healthcare practice.
3.4 Preserving Human Agency and Decision-Making Authority in Artificial Intelligence-Assisted Processes
Universal
Specialist title: Preserving Human Agency and Decision-Making Authority in AI-Assisted Processes
Rationale
A critical risk in AI-assisted work is automation bias - the tendency to defer to algorithmic recommendations without applying sufficient professional judgement or ethical scrutiny. Students who design workflows that preserve human authority over AI-assisted outcomes ensure that accountability remains with individuals rather than being obscured by automated processes. This competency focuses on the structural dimension of maintaining human control - designing decision points, review checkpoints, and accountability mechanisms that keep humans as genuine decision-makers rather than implementers of AI recommendations.
Competency Statement
Design and apply decision-making approaches that ensure human authority and judgement determine outcomes in artificial intelligence-assisted processes, preventing uncritical acceptance of algorithmic recommendations and maintaining professional accountability in academic, organisational, and societal contexts.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Recognise that artificial intelligence provides decision support rather than making decisions, identifying situations where human judgement must determine final outcomes.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Explain why human authority must be preserved in artificial intelligence-assisted processes, applying basic practices to ensure professional judgement determines outcomes.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Assess decision-making workflows involving artificial intelligence, identifying when and how professional judgement should determine outcomes rather than automated recommendations.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Design human-AI decision workflows that structurally preserve human authority, incorporating review checkpoints that ensure professionals make final decisions.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Examine governance approaches for preserving human decision-making authority in artificial intelligence systems, addressing accountability structures in complex decision environments.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes
Physical Science, Engineering and Mathematics
Human agency preservation is relevant in relation to AI-assisted research and engineering design - automated experimental optimisation systems, AI-generated design recommendations, and algorithmic data interpretation tools all carry the risk of automation bias if their recommendations are accepted without critical scrutiny. Students can apply this competency by developing explicit validation practices for AI-assisted research outputs and engineering decisions, ensuring human expertise and judgement remain central to professional decision-making even when AI tools support the process.
Arts and Humanities
Human agency is a particularly resonant concept in humanities scholarship, connecting to long-standing disciplinary debates about authorship, creative autonomy, and the relationship between human intentionality and technological mediation. Students can apply this competency by developing explicit practices for maintaining critical authorial and scholarly agency when using AI tools - ensuring that AI assists rather than determines interpretive, creative, and argumentative choices. The competency also provides a foundation for engaging with philosophical and critical questions about human agency in AI-mediated cultural production.
Education
Human agency preservation is directly relevant to professional educational decision-making - AI-generated learning recommendations, automated assessment results, and algorithmic student risk predictions all carry the risk of automation bias if accepted uncritically. Students can apply this competency by developing practices for maintaining professional pedagogical judgement when using AI tools, ensuring that human expertise, knowledge of individual learners, and professional ethics determine educational decisions rather than algorithmic recommendations.
Social Sciences, Law and Business
Human agency preservation is critical in high-stakes professional decision-making contexts - AI-assisted legal judgements, policy recommendations, and business decisions all carry significant consequences if human oversight is insufficient. Students can apply this competency by examining structural approaches to preserving human authority in AI-assisted professional workflows, designing accountability mechanisms that ensure professionals make genuine decisions rather than ratifying algorithmic recommendations, and advocating for governance frameworks that maintain human responsibility in automated decision chains.
Medicine, Health and Life Sciences
Human agency preservation is a direct patient safety and professional ethics concern - clinical AI tools that generate diagnostic recommendations, treatment suggestions, and risk scores carry significant automation bias risks if clinicians defer to them without applying independent professional judgement. Students can apply this competency by developing explicit practices for maintaining clinical authority in AI-assisted decision-making, ensuring that human clinical expertise, patient knowledge, and professional ethics determine care decisions rather than algorithmic outputs.
3.5 Analysing Artificial Intelligence's Impact on Information Integrity and Public Discourse
Universal
Specialist title: Analysing the Impact of AI on Information Integrity and Social Discourse
Rationale
Generative artificial intelligence enables synthetic text, images, and video to be produced and distributed at unprecedented scale, amplifying misinformation, distorting public debate, and undermining trust in reliable information sources. Students who analyse how algorithmic systems shape information exposure and influence public opinion are better equipped to participate responsibly in public discourse and protect the integrity of information environments essential to democratic societies. This competency addresses the systemic impact of AI on information ecosystems, distinct from the technical detection of synthetic media addressed in 2.5.
Competency Statement
Analyse how artificial intelligence-generated content and algorithmic recommendation systems influence public opinion, information exposure, and the spread of misinformation, recognising the professional and civic responsibility to uphold information integrity in digital communication environments.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Describe examples where artificial intelligence systems generate, amplify, or spread inaccurate information, recognising risks to information quality in public communication.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Explain how artificial intelligence-generated content and recommendation systems influence information exposure and public understanding.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Analyse how algorithmic systems influence information flows and public discourse, assessing risks of misinformation within digital communication environments.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Evaluate how artificial intelligence affects information integrity, examining implications for professional communication and democratic discourse.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Examine how artificial intelligence systems reshape information ecosystems, public discourse, and institutional trust in knowledge environments.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes
Physical Science, Engineering and Mathematics
Information integrity is relevant in relation to AI-generated scientific misinformation - synthetic research papers, AI-generated data, and algorithmically amplified pseudoscience all threaten the integrity of scientific communication. Students can apply this competency by examining how AI-generated misinformation affects public understanding of scientific issues such as climate change, vaccines, and emerging technologies, and by considering professional responsibilities around science communication in an environment of algorithmically amplified misinformation.
Arts and Humanities
Information integrity and public discourse are central concerns for humanities disciplines that engage with media, communication, culture, and history. Students can apply this competency through critical analysis of how AI-generated content and algorithmic recommendation systems reshape cultural and political discourse, examining the implications for democratic participation, historical memory, and the integrity of cultural knowledge. The discipline's existing engagement with media criticism, propaganda analysis, and discourse theory provides a rich foundation.
Education
Information integrity has direct professional implications for educators who are responsible for developing students' critical media literacy. Students can apply this competency by examining how AI-generated misinformation affects the information environments their learners navigate, developing pedagogical approaches to teaching critical evaluation of AI-generated content, and considering professional responsibilities around modelling and teaching information integrity practices in educational settings.
Social Sciences, Law and Business
Information integrity is directly relevant to professional practice across this discipline group - social researchers navigating AI-generated data contamination, legal professionals encountering AI-generated evidence, and business communicators operating in environments of algorithmic amplification all face significant professional challenges. Students can apply this competency by examining how AI affects information integrity in their specific professional field, assessing implications for research validity, legal evidence standards, and organisational communication responsibility.
Medicine, Health and Life Sciences
Health misinformation amplified by AI recommendation systems has direct public health implications - AI-generated medical misinformation, algorithmically amplified health conspiracy theories, and synthetic health content all affect patient behaviour and public health outcomes. Students can apply this competency by examining how AI-driven misinformation affects the health information environments their patients navigate, developing professional awareness of the public health implications of algorithmic amplification, and considering responsibilities around countering health misinformation in their professional practice.
3.6 Recognising the Psycho-Emotional Effect of Human-Like Artificial Intelligence Responses on Human Users
Contextual
Specialist title: Recognising the Psycho-Emotional Effect of Human-Like AI Responses on Human Users
Panel validation note: Confirmed applicable by the Sciences, Engineering and Mathematics; Education; and Social Sciences, Law and Business panels. Not confirmed as applicable by the Arts and Humanities or Medicine, Health and Life Sciences panels. The interdisciplinary panel retained this competency as Contextual, recommended for disciplines and professional contexts where students regularly engage with conversational AI systems or work in roles involving human-AI interaction design, counselling, pastoral care, or therapeutic practice.
Rationale
AI systems increasingly use conversational language and empathetic phrasing that trigger psychological responses typically reserved for human relationships, encouraging unconscious attribution of consciousness, emotional understanding, or social agency to algorithmic systems. Students who understand these mechanisms are better equipped to recognise when conversational design is shaping their responses, maintain appropriate professional and personal boundaries, and engage productively with AI tools without developing inappropriate trust or emotional reliance on systems that simulate rather than possess understanding.
Competency Statement
Identify and evaluate psychological tendencies to attribute human-like qualities to artificial intelligence systems, recognising how anthropomorphic design and emotionally responsive interfaces influence trust, emotional attachment, and disclosure behaviours in ways that may compromise professional judgement or personal boundaries.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Recognise that conversational artificial intelligence triggers responses similar to human interaction, identifying examples where empathetic language influences trust despite systems lacking genuine understanding.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Explain how anthropomorphic artificial intelligence design triggers psychological responses, interpreting how empathetic language may influence trust or emotional attachment despite systems lacking consciousness.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Analyse psychological mechanisms underlying responses to anthropomorphic artificial intelligence, assessing how conversational design influences trust and professional boundary maintenance.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Evaluate the psychological and ethical implications of anthropomorphic artificial intelligence design in professional contexts, examining how emotionally responsive systems influence behaviour and boundaries.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Examine theoretical frameworks for understanding psychological responses to anthropomorphic artificial intelligence, addressing trust formation and ethical implications of conversational AI design.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes
Physical Science, Engineering and Mathematics
Most relevant in relation to human-computer interaction research and the design of AI systems - students in computer science, human factors engineering, and related fields encounter the psycho-emotional dimensions of AI design as a professional practice concern. The competency applies to understanding how design choices in AI interfaces influence user trust and behaviour, informing more ethically responsible system design.
Education
Most relevant in relation to AI tutoring systems and conversational educational AI - students training as educators need to understand how anthropomorphic educational AI affects learner relationships, emotional engagement, and dependency patterns. This informs professional judgements about appropriate use of conversational AI in educational settings and the pastoral responsibilities that arise when learners form emotional attachments to AI systems.
Social Sciences, Law and Business
Most relevant in relation to consumer behaviour, social psychology, and human-AI interaction research - students in psychology, sociology, marketing, and related fields encounter psycho-emotional responses to AI as both a research topic and a professional practice concern. The competency applies to understanding how anthropomorphic AI design is used commercially to influence consumer behaviour, and the ethical implications of deliberately designed emotional responses to AI systems.
3.7 Identifying Professional Contexts Where Artificial Intelligence Use Is Inappropriate
Universal
Specialist title: Recognising Tasks Requiring Exclusively Human Judgement and Capability
Rationale
Not all tasks benefit from artificial intelligence assistance. Some contexts require exclusively human capabilities - genuine emotional connection, moral accountability, artistic judgement, therapeutic care, or contextual wisdom - that cannot be replicated algorithmically without ethical compromise. Students who can identify these contexts are better equipped to make principled decisions about when AI assistance enhances professional work and when it undermines fundamental human values. This competency addresses the ethical dimension of AI delegation - recognising that some responsibilities cannot be transferred to automated systems regardless of technical capability.
Competency Statement
Identify professional, interpersonal, and creative contexts where artificial intelligence assistance is ethically inappropriate, recognising tasks that require exclusively human capabilities such as moral responsibility, emotional intelligence, and contextual wisdom that cannot be delegated to algorithmic systems.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Name examples of tasks requiring human capabilities that artificial intelligence cannot ethically replicate, recognising why certain professional responsibilities must remain with humans.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Explain why certain professional and interpersonal contexts require exclusively human judgement, distinguishing human ethical responsibility from algorithmic processing.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Assess whether artificial intelligence assistance is ethically appropriate for specific professional tasks, analysing when algorithmic support undermines essential human values.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Evaluate professional contexts to determine ethically appropriate boundaries for artificial intelligence integration, preserving human judgement where accountability cannot be delegated.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Examine ethical frameworks for determining appropriate and inappropriate artificial intelligence use, addressing the irreplaceable role of human capability in professional and social contexts.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes
Physical Science, Engineering and Mathematics
The most relevant dimension here is recognising contexts within engineering and scientific practice where human ethical accountability cannot be delegated to AI - safety-critical engineering decisions, research integrity judgements, and ethical review of experimental protocols all require human moral responsibility that AI cannot substitute. Students can apply this competency by identifying specific points in their professional practice where human judgement is ethically non-negotiable, regardless of AI technical capability.
Arts and Humanities
This competency is particularly resonant for Arts and Humanities disciplines where questions of human creativity, authorship, interpretation, and cultural judgement are central professional concerns. Students can apply it by examining which creative, scholarly, and cultural practices require human intentionality, contextual wisdom, and ethical responsibility that AI cannot replicate - including therapeutic arts practice, pastoral counselling, cultural custodianship, and interpretive scholarship that carries ethical responsibilities toward communities and traditions.
Education
The most directly relevant dimension for this discipline is recognising which aspects of educational practice require human presence, relationship, and moral responsibility that AI cannot substitute - pastoral care, safeguarding judgements, therapeutic support, and the relational dimensions of teaching that depend on genuine human connection. Students can apply this competency by developing professional clarity about where AI can appropriately support their practice and where human presence and accountability are ethically non-negotiable.
Social Sciences, Law and Business
This competency is highly relevant across this discipline group - legal judgements carrying moral weight, social work decisions affecting vulnerable people, and business decisions with significant social consequences all require human accountability that cannot be delegated to algorithmic systems. Students can apply it by examining specific professional contexts in their field where AI assistance would be ethically inappropriate, developing professional frameworks for maintaining human responsibility in AI-assisted practice.
Medicine, Health and Life Sciences
This competency is most immediately and urgently relevant to this discipline group. Clinical decisions carrying life-or-death consequences, end-of-life care, mental health support, and the therapeutic relationship all require human presence, empathy, and moral accountability that AI cannot replicate without profound ethical compromise. Students can apply this competency by developing professional clarity about which clinical and care responsibilities must remain with human practitioners regardless of AI technical capability, grounding this in relevant professional ethics codes and patient rights frameworks.
Domain 4

Practical Application and Professional Use of Artificial Intelligence Tools

Domain Description

This domain develops the ability to use artificial intelligence tools effectively, critically, and responsibly in professional and academic workflows. Competencies address prompt design, tool selection and workflow integration, maintaining critical judgement when using AI as a cognitive tool, and diagnosing and resolving AI interaction failures. Together they equip students with the practical skills needed to engage productively with AI systems while maintaining professional standards, intellectual integrity, and human oversight across all disciplinary contexts.

Competencies in this domain: 4  ·  4 Universal
4.1 Designing Effective Prompts and Queries for Artificial Intelligence Systems
Universal
Specialist title: Crafting Effective Prompts and Queries for Artificial Intelligence Systems
Rationale
Artificial intelligence systems respond directly to the clarity, structure, and specificity of the instructions they receive. Poorly designed prompts produce vague, incomplete, or misleading outputs, while carefully constructed prompts significantly improve the relevance and reliability of AI-generated responses. Students who can design effective prompts are better equipped to use AI tools productively across disciplinary and professional contexts, maintaining control over the quality and direction of AI interactions rather than accepting whatever a system produces by default.
Competency Statement
Design clear, specific, and contextually appropriate prompts to obtain reliable outputs from artificial intelligence systems, applying techniques such as providing context, worked examples, and structured instructions, and refining queries iteratively based on system responses.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Write simple, clear prompts for artificial intelligence systems, recognising that instruction clarity influences the quality and relevance of outputs.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Apply structured prompting techniques, providing context and examples to improve the relevance and accuracy of artificial intelligence outputs.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Design prompts tailored to disciplinary tasks, evaluating how wording and contextual framing influence artificial intelligence output quality.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Develop and refine prompting strategies for complex professional tasks, applying iterative interaction to improve artificial intelligence-assisted analysis and communication.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Examine prompting methodologies that optimise human-artificial intelligence interaction, contributing to understanding of effective knowledge generation using AI systems.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes

This competency applies broadly across all discipline groups:

This competency applies broadly and consistently across all five discipline groups - the core skill of designing effective prompts for AI systems is equally relevant whether a student is conducting scientific literature reviews, writing legal arguments, developing educational materials, producing creative work, or analysing clinical data. The practical contextualisation differs by discipline in terms of the specific tasks prompts are designed for, but the underlying competency - structuring instructions clearly, providing relevant context, and iterating based on outputs - translates directly across all five groups without significant disciplinary variation. Programme teams in each discipline should contextualise this competency through the specific professional tasks and AI tools most relevant to their field, using disciplinary examples in teaching activities while keeping the core competency universal in its framing.

4.2 Selecting and Integrating Artificial Intelligence Tools in Professional Workflows
Universal
Specialist title: Selecting and Integrating Artificial Intelligence Tools in Professional Workflows
Rationale
Professionals increasingly work alongside multiple artificial intelligence systems that assist with analysis, communication, and decision support. Effective professional use requires evaluating available tools against criteria including capability, reliability, privacy implications, and disciplinary suitability, then integrating them into workflows that preserve human oversight and accountability. Students who develop this competency are prepared to coordinate human-AI collaboration within organisations, ensuring AI technologies improve efficiency while maintaining professional standards and the quality of decision-making.
Competency Statement
Evaluate, select, and integrate appropriate artificial intelligence tools into professional workflows, assessing capability, reliability, and privacy implications, and designing collaboration processes where automated assistance supports tasks while human expertise retains judgement and decision-making responsibility.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Describe differences between common artificial intelligence tools, recognising that different systems suit different tasks in academic or professional contexts.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Select appropriate artificial intelligence tools for familiar tasks, explaining how system capabilities influence their usefulness in supporting professional activities.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Evaluate artificial intelligence tools for disciplinary suitability and reliability, integrating selected tools into a professional workflow with defined human oversight.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Design human-AI collaboration workflows integrating multiple artificial intelligence tools, maintaining professional oversight, accountability, and contextual judgement throughout.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Examine frameworks for integrating artificial intelligence technologies within professional systems, addressing human-AI collaboration and responsible workflow transformation.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes
Physical Science, Engineering and Mathematics
Tool selection and workflow integration are directly relevant to research and engineering practice - choosing between AI-assisted data analysis tools, simulation platforms, and literature review systems requires disciplinary judgement about capability, reliability, and appropriate use. Students can apply this competency by evaluating AI tools used in their research or engineering workflows against criteria including accuracy, reproducibility, and data privacy, and by designing research workflows that maintain scientific rigour while benefiting from AI assistance.
Arts and Humanities
Tool selection involves important questions about creative and scholarly integrity - choosing AI tools for translation, archival research, textual analysis, or creative work requires judgement about disciplinary suitability, cultural sensitivity, and the implications of AI assistance for scholarly and creative authenticity. Students can apply this competency by evaluating AI tools relevant to their creative or scholarly practice against criteria including cultural and linguistic appropriateness, and by designing workflows that preserve scholarly and creative agency while benefiting from AI assistance.
Education
Tool selection and workflow integration are directly relevant to professional educational practice - choosing AI tools for lesson planning, assessment design, feedback generation, and administrative tasks requires judgement about pedagogical suitability, student data privacy, and institutional policy compliance. Students can apply this competency by evaluating educational AI tools against criteria including pedagogical effectiveness, data protection, and equity of access, and by designing teaching workflows that maintain professional educational judgement while benefiting from AI support.
Social Sciences, Law and Business
Tool selection carries significant professional responsibility in regulated contexts - choosing AI tools for legal research, social science data analysis, or business intelligence requires careful assessment of reliability, bias risk, data privacy, and regulatory compliance. Students can apply this competency by evaluating AI tools relevant to their professional field against criteria including accuracy, bias, and compliance with relevant legal and regulatory frameworks, and by designing professional workflows that maintain human accountability for AI-assisted decisions.
Medicine, Health and Life Sciences
Tool selection in clinical and research contexts carries direct patient safety implications - choosing AI tools for diagnostic support, clinical documentation, or health research requires rigorous assessment of validation evidence, regulatory approval status, and data security standards. Students can apply this competency by evaluating clinical AI tools against evidence-based criteria including diagnostic accuracy, population coverage, and regulatory compliance, and by designing clinical workflows that maintain human clinical oversight of AI-assisted processes.
4.3 Using Artificial Intelligence as a Tool Whilst Preserving Critical Judgement
Universal
Specialist title: Using AI as a Cognitive Partner Whilst Maintaining Validation and Judgement
Rationale
Artificial intelligence systems can support complex intellectual work including analysis, synthesis, and communication, but their outputs may contain errors, misrepresent sources, or lack disciplinary nuance. Students who use AI as a cognitive tool whilst consistently validating its outputs develop a disciplined approach to AI-assisted work that maintains intellectual integrity regardless of the task. This competency is not about using AI for specific tasks but about developing the professional habit of treating AI as a tool that requires critical oversight rather than an authority whose outputs can be accepted without question.
Competency Statement
Apply artificial intelligence tools to support intellectual work including analysis, synthesis, and communication, whilst maintaining validation practices that verify accuracy, check sources, and ensure outputs reflect disciplined human judgement rather than uncritical acceptance of automated suggestions.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Use artificial intelligence to support a cognitive task, recognising the need to review and verify outputs before relying on them.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Apply artificial intelligence to assist intellectual work, comparing outputs against authoritative sources to confirm reliability before use.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Evaluate artificial intelligence outputs used in disciplinary work, implementing validation practices to verify accuracy and meet standards of academic rigour.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Integrate artificial intelligence strategically across professional work, maintaining disciplined validation workflows that ensure outputs reflect expert judgement and verified evidence.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Examine methodologies for artificial intelligence-assisted intellectual work that combine algorithmic support with rigorous validation, preserving epistemic standards across disciplines.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes

This competency applies broadly across all discipline groups:

This competency applies broadly and consistently across all five discipline groups - the core professional habit of using AI tools whilst maintaining critical oversight and validation is equally essential whether a student is conducting scientific research, producing legal arguments, designing educational materials, creating artistic work, or supporting clinical decision-making. The validation practices differ in their specific form by discipline - scientific verification against experimental data, legal cross-referencing against primary sources, clinical appraisal against evidence-based guidelines - but the underlying competency translates directly across all five groups. Programme teams in each discipline should contextualise this competency through the specific validation standards and intellectual integrity norms of their field, making the universal competency practically relevant to disciplinary professional practice.

4.4 Diagnosing and Resolving Artificial Intelligence Failures and Unhelpful Outputs in Practice
Universal
Specialist title: Troubleshooting and Recovering from AI Interaction Failures
Rationale
Artificial intelligence tools frequently produce unsatisfactory outputs - vague responses, unhelpful refusals, contradictory information, or repetitive loops - requiring users to diagnose what went wrong and recover productive interaction. Students who develop systematic strategies for identifying and resolving these failures are better equipped to use AI tools persistently and effectively rather than abandoning them prematurely or accepting poor outputs as inevitable. This practical competency transforms frustrating AI interactions into productive learning experiences and professional problem-solving opportunities.
Competency Statement
Diagnose and resolve common artificial intelligence interaction failures - including vague outputs, unhelpful refusals, contradictory responses, and repetitive loops - by refining prompts, adjusting constraints, simplifying requests, or selecting alternative tools when initial approaches fail to produce usable results.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Recognise common artificial intelligence interaction problems such as vague or unexpected outputs, applying a basic strategy to improve results.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Diagnose why artificial intelligence interactions produce poor results, identifying whether the cause relates to prompt clarity, task complexity, or tool limitations.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Implement structured troubleshooting strategies for artificial intelligence interaction failures, selecting appropriate fixes based on accurate diagnosis of the problem.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Design artificial intelligence interaction workflows that anticipate common failures, incorporating contingency strategies to maintain productivity when systems produce unhelpful outputs.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Examine approaches to diagnosing and resolving artificial intelligence interaction failures, contributing to understanding of resilient human-AI interaction across professional contexts.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes

This competency applies broadly across all discipline groups:

This competency applies broadly and consistently across all five discipline groups - the practical skill of diagnosing and resolving AI interaction failures is equally relevant regardless of disciplinary context. Whether a student is using AI for scientific literature review, legal research, lesson planning, creative writing, or clinical documentation, the same fundamental challenge arises when AI tools produce unhelpful outputs - and the same diagnostic and recovery strategies apply. Programme teams in each discipline should contextualise this competency through the specific AI tools and professional tasks most relevant to their field, helping students develop troubleshooting strategies tailored to the particular AI interactions they will encounter in disciplinary practice.

Domain 5

Governance, Accountability, and Regulation of Artificial Intelligence

Domain Description

This domain develops understanding of how artificial intelligence systems should be governed, regulated, and ethically managed within organisations and society. Competencies address examining AI tools for trustworthiness and reliability, transparent disclosure of AI use, legal and regulatory compliance, professional ethics, intellectual property, and - for relevant professional contexts - personal accountability and organisational governance responsibilities. Together they equip students to engage with AI as responsible professionals who understand the governance landscape within which AI systems operate and the accountability obligations that professional AI use creates.

Competencies in this domain: 7  ·  5 Universal 2 Contextual
5.1 Examining Artificial Intelligence Tools for Trustworthiness, Transparency, and Reliability
Universal
Specialist title: Evaluating the Provenance, Transparency, and Development Context of Artificial Intelligence Systems / Recognising When AI Systems Require Review and Updating
Rationale
Artificial intelligence tools reflect the priorities, incentives, and assumptions of those who build and fund them. Without examining provenance, training data origins, and transparency mechanisms, students risk engaging uncritically with tools that embed hidden biases or governance weaknesses. Equally, AI tools degrade over time as contexts change, producing unreliable or inappropriate outputs. Students who maintain sustained critical examination of AI tools across their full lifecycle - from initial selection through continued use - are better equipped to make responsible adoption decisions and escalate concerns when systems show signs of declining reliability.
Competency Statement
Examine artificial intelligence tools by investigating their provenance, development context, funding sources, training data origins, and transparency mechanisms, whilst recognising warning signs of declining reliability in tools already in use and knowing when to escalate concerns to appropriate specialists or decision-makers.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Recognise that artificial intelligence tools reflect developer decisions and can become unreliable over time, identifying basic signs that a tool's origins or outputs require closer attention.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Explain why examining an artificial intelligence tool's origins and ongoing performance matters, identifying warning signs that suggest review or specialist attention is needed.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Assess an artificial intelligence tool's provenance, funding, transparency, and performance trends, identifying concerns and determining when escalation is appropriate.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Design oversight practices that identify governance weaknesses and performance degradation in artificial intelligence tools before harm occurs in professional contexts.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Examine approaches to provenance, transparency, and performance monitoring in artificial intelligence tools, contributing to scholarship on accountability and responsible deployment.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes
Physical Science, Engineering and Mathematics
This discipline group's panels indicated not applicable to both original competencies. The most relevant dimension of the merged competency for this group is the ongoing reliability and performance monitoring dimension - AI tools used in scientific research and engineering practice can degrade or perform inconsistently across different experimental contexts, and recognising when AI-assisted outputs require additional verification or specialist review is a directly applicable professional skill. Students can contextualise through examining the validation and monitoring practices appropriate for AI tools used in their specific research or engineering domain.
Arts and Humanities
This discipline group validated both original competencies and recommended the merger, meaning both dimensions are directly relevant. The provenance and transparency dimension is particularly resonant - understanding who developed an AI tool, what data it was trained on, and whose cultural perspectives it embeds is directly relevant to scholarly and creative practice. The reliability monitoring dimension applies to recognising when AI tools used in research or creative work are producing outputs that require critical scrutiny or professional review.
Education
This discipline group validated both original competencies and recommended the merger, meaning both dimensions are directly relevant. The provenance dimension applies to evaluating the development context and funding sources of educational AI tools - understanding who built a platform, what data it was trained on, and what commercial interests may shape its design is directly relevant to professional educational decision-making. The reliability monitoring dimension applies to recognising when educational AI tools are producing outputs that no longer appropriately support learning objectives.
Social Sciences, Law and Business
This discipline group's panels indicated not applicable to both original competencies. The most relevant dimension for this group is the provenance and transparency dimension - understanding who developed AI tools used in professional practice, what interests they serve, and whether their outputs can be trusted is directly relevant to legal, policy, and business professional responsibilities. Students can contextualise through examining the provenance and transparency of AI tools used in their professional field, assessing their suitability for high-stakes professional decision-making.
Medicine, Health and Life Sciences
This discipline group's panels indicated not applicable to both original competencies. The most relevant dimension for this group is the reliability monitoring dimension - clinical AI tools that degrade in performance over time, or that were validated on patient populations different from those now being served, carry direct patient safety implications. Recognising warning signs of declining AI tool reliability and knowing when to escalate concerns to clinical governance or technical specialists is a directly applicable patient safety competency. Students can contextualise through examining the clinical governance frameworks for monitoring AI tool performance in healthcare settings.
5.2 Disclosing and Documenting Artificial Intelligence Use and Contributions in Academic and Professional Work
Universal
Specialist title: Documenting and Attributing AI Contributions to Work
Rationale
Transparency in AI use is fundamental to maintaining trust in academic and professional work. When artificial intelligence tools assist with writing, analysis, or design, their contribution must be clearly disclosed and documented. Failure to disclose AI assistance undermines credibility and in many contexts constitutes academic or professional misconduct. Students who develop consistent disclosure and documentation practices demonstrate intellectual honesty, comply with institutional policies, and preserve the integrity of their work when collaborating with AI tools across all disciplinary and professional contexts.
Competency Statement
Disclose and document the use of artificial intelligence tools transparently, specifying systems used, tasks supported, and verification steps applied, ensuring attribution that maintains intellectual honesty and complies with institutional and professional standards for responsible technology use.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Recognise when artificial intelligence tools contribute to academic work, following basic guidance for disclosing AI assistance in assignments or projects.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Explain why disclosing artificial intelligence use supports academic integrity, applying institutional guidance to attribute AI contributions in coursework.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Apply appropriate disclosure and attribution practices for artificial intelligence assistance, documenting tools used, contributions made, and verification steps taken.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Evaluate responsible disclosure practices for artificial intelligence-assisted work, ensuring transparent documentation aligned with institutional and professional standards.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Examine frameworks for transparent attribution of artificial intelligence contributions in scholarly and professional work, addressing standards for responsible technology use.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes
Physical Science, Engineering and Mathematics
Disclosure of AI use in research is directly relevant to research integrity standards - reporting AI assistance in data analysis, literature review, and manuscript preparation is increasingly required by journals, funding bodies, and institutional research governance frameworks. Students can apply this competency by developing disclosure practices aligned with the specific requirements of their disciplinary publication and research reporting standards, ensuring that AI contributions to research outputs are transparently attributed in ways that meet disciplinary integrity expectations.
Arts and Humanities
Disclosure of AI use raises distinctive questions about authorship, creative integrity, and scholarly voice in this discipline group. Students can apply this competency by developing disclosure practices appropriate to the specific forms of AI assistance used in creative and scholarly work - distinguishing between AI assistance with research, writing support, translation, and creative generation, and documenting each appropriately according to disciplinary and institutional expectations. The competency connects to broader questions about authorship and intellectual contribution central to humanities scholarly identity.
Education
Disclosure of AI use is directly relevant to professional modelling of academic integrity - educators who use AI tools in teaching materials, assessment design, and professional communications have a responsibility to model transparent disclosure practices for their students. Students can apply this competency by developing disclosure practices appropriate to professional educational contexts, including how to acknowledge AI assistance in teaching resources, institutional reports, and professional publications while maintaining standards of intellectual honesty appropriate to their professional role.
Social Sciences, Law and Business
Disclosure of AI use carries significant professional responsibility in regulated contexts - legal professionals, researchers, and business practitioners who use AI assistance must comply with professional standards and institutional requirements around disclosure. Students can apply this competency by developing disclosure practices appropriate to their specific professional regulatory context, understanding what AI assistance must be disclosed in legal documents, research publications, and professional reports, and ensuring that disclosure practices meet the standards of their professional regulatory body.
Medicine, Health and Life Sciences
Disclosure of AI use in clinical and research contexts carries direct professional accountability implications - clinical documentation, research publications, and professional communications that involve AI assistance must be transparently attributed to maintain patient trust, research integrity, and professional accountability. Students can apply this competency by developing disclosure practices aligned with the specific requirements of clinical governance frameworks, health research reporting standards, and professional regulatory bodies in their health discipline.
5.3 Interpreting Relevant Legal and Regulatory Frameworks Governing Artificial Intelligence
Universal
Specialist title: Understanding Relevant Legal and Regulatory Frameworks Governing AI
Rationale
Artificial intelligence technologies operate within evolving legal and regulatory frameworks addressing data protection, safety, transparency, and individual accountability. Students entering regulated professions - including healthcare, finance, law, and public administration - must understand what legal requirements apply to AI use in their field, what compliance obligations they carry as individuals, and what consequences arise from non-compliant practice. This competency focuses on individual legal awareness and compliance understanding, distinct from the organisational governance responsibilities addressed in 5.7.
Competency Statement
Interpret key legal and regulatory frameworks governing artificial intelligence use, including data protection, safety, and accountability requirements, recognising how these frameworks shape individual professional practice and create compliance obligations in disciplinary and organisational contexts.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Recognise that artificial intelligence use operates within legal and regulatory frameworks, identifying basic examples of rules governing data protection and responsible technology use.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Explain key regulatory principles affecting artificial intelligence use, interpreting how laws and standards create individual compliance obligations in professional practice.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Apply knowledge of relevant legal frameworks to evaluate artificial intelligence use within a disciplinary context, identifying specific individual compliance requirements.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Evaluate regulatory frameworks governing artificial intelligence, analysing how legal requirements shape individual professional obligations and responsible practice decisions.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Examine artificial intelligence regulation from an individual compliance perspective, contributing to understanding of legal frameworks and their implications for professional practice.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes
Physical Science, Engineering and Mathematics
The most relevant legal frameworks for this discipline group relate to data protection legislation governing research data, intellectual property law in relation to AI-generated research outputs, and sector-specific regulatory requirements for AI use in safety-critical engineering applications. Students can apply this competency by identifying the specific legal requirements that apply to AI use in their research or engineering context, understanding their individual compliance obligations, and developing awareness of the regulatory landscape governing AI in their professional field.
Arts and Humanities
The most relevant legal frameworks for this discipline group relate to copyright and intellectual property law governing AI-generated creative and scholarly content, data protection legislation governing the processing of personal data in humanities research, and emerging regulatory frameworks around AI-generated media and cultural artefacts. Students can apply this competency by examining how existing intellectual property law applies to AI-assisted creative and scholarly work, and by developing awareness of emerging regulatory frameworks that may affect their professional practice.
Education
The most relevant legal frameworks for this discipline group relate to data protection legislation governing student data, safeguarding legislation, and institutional policies on AI use in assessment and teaching. Students can apply this competency by identifying the specific legal requirements that apply to AI use in educational settings, understanding individual professional compliance obligations, and developing awareness of how emerging AI regulation may affect professional practice in educational institutions.
Social Sciences, Law and Business
Legal and regulatory frameworks are particularly central to professional practice across this discipline group - legal professionals, policy analysts, and business practitioners operate within and advise on the regulatory frameworks governing AI. Students can apply this competency by examining specific regulatory frameworks relevant to their professional field - the EU AI Act, UK data protection legislation, financial services AI regulation, or sector-specific AI governance requirements - and developing practical understanding of individual compliance obligations in their professional context.
Medicine, Health and Life Sciences
The most relevant legal frameworks for this discipline group relate to medical device regulation governing clinical AI tools, data protection legislation governing patient health data, and professional regulatory requirements around AI use in clinical practice. Students can apply this competency by identifying the specific regulatory requirements that apply to AI use in their health discipline, understanding individual clinical governance obligations, and developing awareness of the rapidly evolving regulatory landscape for AI medical devices and clinical decision support systems.
5.4 Applying Professional Ethics and Sector-Specific Codes to Artificial Intelligence Use
Universal
Specialist title: Applying Professional Ethics and Sector-Specific Codes to AI Use
Rationale
Professional practice is governed by external ethical standards established by sector-specific bodies - medical, legal, financial, and engineering regulators among others. Introducing artificial intelligence into professional workflows does not remove these obligations. Students who apply established professional codes of conduct when using AI tools ensure that automated assistance does not compromise duties of care, confidentiality obligations, or accountability to clients, patients, and the public. This competency is explicitly about external professional codes, distinct from the internal reflective accountability addressed in 5.6.
Competency Statement
Apply sector-specific professional codes of conduct when using artificial intelligence tools, ensuring that automated assistance does not compromise duties of care, ethical responsibilities, confidentiality obligations, or accountability to clients, patients, organisations, or the wider public.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Recognise that professional ethical standards apply when using artificial intelligence tools, identifying basic obligations such as confidentiality, honesty, and duty of care.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Explain how professional codes of conduct guide responsible artificial intelligence use, interpreting the ethical obligations relevant to a chosen disciplinary practice.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Apply professional ethical principles when using artificial intelligence tools, evaluating potential conflicts between automated recommendations and professional duties.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Evaluate the ethical implications of artificial intelligence use within professional practice, ensuring decisions align with sector-specific codes of conduct and professional obligations.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Examine ethical governance of artificial intelligence in professional practice, contributing to understanding of how sector-specific codes address responsible technological integration.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes
Physical Science, Engineering and Mathematics
Professional ethics codes relevant to this discipline group include engineering professional body codes - such as those of the Institution of Engineering and Technology or the Royal Academy of Engineering - that address responsibilities around safety, sustainability, and honest reporting. Students can apply this competency by examining how their disciplinary professional body's code of conduct addresses AI use, identifying obligations around research integrity, safety-critical decision-making, and transparent reporting that apply when AI tools contribute to professional work.
Arts and Humanities
Professional ethics in Arts and Humanities contexts includes codes governing academic integrity, creative practice, cultural heritage management, journalism, and therapeutic arts. Students can apply this competency by identifying the professional ethics frameworks relevant to their specific disciplinary practice, examining how these frameworks address AI use, and developing principled approaches to AI-assisted creative and scholarly work that honour the ethical obligations of their professional community.
Education
Professional ethics codes in education - including those of the Chartered College of Teaching and sector-specific regulatory bodies - address responsibilities around student welfare, academic integrity, and professional conduct that directly apply when AI tools are used in teaching and assessment. Students can apply this competency by examining how their professional body's code of conduct addresses AI use in educational settings, identifying specific obligations around student data protection, assessment integrity, and the duty of care that apply when AI assists professional educational practice.
Social Sciences, Law and Business
Professional ethics codes are particularly central to this discipline group - the Solicitors Regulation Authority, the British Psychological Society, the Chartered Institute of Management Accountants, and other sector-specific bodies all have codes that directly address or have implications for AI use. Students can apply this competency by examining the specific provisions of their professional body's code of conduct in relation to AI use, identifying obligations around client confidentiality, professional independence, and duty of care that apply when AI tools contribute to regulated professional work.
Medicine, Health and Life Sciences
Professional ethics codes in health professions - including those of the General Medical Council, the Nursing and Midwifery Council, and allied health regulatory bodies - establish clear obligations around patient safety, confidentiality, and duty of care that directly apply when AI tools are used in clinical practice. Students can apply this competency by examining how their specific professional regulatory body's code addresses AI use, identifying the obligations that apply when AI assists clinical decision-making, patient communication, and health data management.
5.5 Evaluating Intellectual Property, Copyright, and Training Data Ethics in Artificial Intelligence
Universal
Specialist title: Evaluating Intellectual Property, Copyright, and Training Data Ethics
Rationale
Artificial intelligence systems generate content derived from patterns learned within large datasets that frequently include copyrighted material, raising complex ethical and legal questions about authorship, ownership, and appropriate use. Students who can evaluate the intellectual property and copyright implications of AI-generated content are better equipped to adopt responsible practices when creating or distributing AI-assisted work. This competency supports respect for human creativity and ensures students operate within evolving intellectual property frameworks across all disciplinary and professional contexts.
Competency Statement
Evaluate the intellectual property and copyright implications of using artificial intelligence-generated content, recognising ethical and legal risks associated with training data, authorship, and content reuse, and applying responsible practices that respect creators' rights and comply with copyright obligations.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Recognise situations where artificial intelligence-generated content may raise copyright or authorship concerns, understanding the importance of respecting intellectual property.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Explain how copyright and intellectual property rules apply to artificial intelligence-generated content, interpreting risks related to reuse, attribution, and authorship.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Assess intellectual property implications of artificial intelligence use in a professional context, evaluating copyright risks and identifying responsible content practices.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Evaluate legal and ethical challenges surrounding artificial intelligence training data, authorship, and copyright within professional and creative disciplinary contexts.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Examine intellectual property governance in artificial intelligence, addressing copyright, authorship, and training data ethics across disciplinary and professional contexts.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes
Physical Science, Engineering and Mathematics
Intellectual property considerations are relevant in relation to AI-generated research outputs, patentable innovations developed with AI assistance, and the use of copyrighted datasets for AI model training in research. Students can apply this competency by examining the intellectual property implications of AI tools used in their research - understanding what rights attach to AI-generated research outputs, what obligations arise from using copyrighted training data, and how patent law applies to AI-assisted inventions in their engineering or scientific field.
Arts and Humanities
Intellectual property and copyright are particularly central concerns for this discipline group - AI systems trained on literary, artistic, and musical works without explicit permission directly affect the livelihoods and rights of creative practitioners. Students can apply this competency by examining the copyright implications of AI tools used in creative and scholarly practice, understanding what obligations arise from using AI-generated content in published work, and engaging critically with debates about authorship, creative ownership, and the ethics of training AI systems on cultural works without consent.
Education
Intellectual property considerations are directly relevant to professional educational practice - AI-generated teaching materials, assessment resources, and educational content all raise questions about copyright, authorship, and appropriate use. Students can apply this competency by examining the intellectual property implications of AI tools used to create educational materials, understanding what copyright obligations apply when AI-generated content is used in teaching, and developing responsible practices for creating and sharing AI-assisted educational resources within institutional intellectual property frameworks.
Social Sciences, Law and Business
Intellectual property law is directly relevant to professional practice across this discipline group - legal practitioners advising on AI intellectual property cases, business strategists navigating AI-related IP risks, and researchers using AI to analyse copyrighted materials all need to understand the evolving legal landscape. Students can apply this competency by examining the specific intellectual property risks relevant to their professional field, understanding how copyright law applies to AI-generated professional outputs, and developing responsible practices that comply with current legal frameworks while remaining alert to rapidly evolving case law and regulation.
Medicine, Health and Life Sciences
Intellectual property considerations are relevant in relation to AI-assisted drug discovery, AI-generated clinical research outputs, and the use of patient data to train AI systems. Students can apply this competency by examining the intellectual property implications of AI tools used in health research and clinical practice, understanding what rights and obligations arise from AI-assisted research outputs, and developing awareness of the ethical dimensions of using patient data in AI training without explicit consent.
5.6 Reflecting on Personal and Professional Accountability in Artificial Intelligence-Assisted Work
Contextual
Specialist title: Understanding Personal and Professional Accountability in AI-Assisted Work
Panel validation note: Confirmed applicable by the Education; Social Sciences, Law and Business; and Medicine, Health and Life Sciences panels. The Sciences, Engineering and Mathematics and Arts and Humanities panels recommended merger with 5.7. The interdisciplinary panel decided 5.6 and 5.7 should not be merged and designated both as Contextual. This competency is recommended for disciplines and professional contexts where students regularly encounter high-stakes accountability decisions in AI-assisted practice - particularly health, education, law, social work, and public administration.
Rationale
Using artificial intelligence does not transfer accountability. Students who regularly reflect on their personal accountability position when working with AI are better equipped to verify outputs, exercise professional judgement when automated recommendations conflict with their expertise or ethical responsibilities, and accept responsibility for decisions made with AI assistance. This reflective habit - focused entirely on internal professional responsibility rather than external codes or organisational structures - protects both the student and those affected by their work from the consequences of uncritical acceptance of algorithmic recommendations.
Competency Statement
Reflect critically on personal and professional accountability when using artificial intelligence tools, recognising that responsibility for decisions, outputs, and consequences remains with the individual rather than the AI system, regardless of the level of automated assistance involved.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Recognise that individuals remain accountable for work produced using artificial intelligence tools, identifying examples where AI assistance does not reduce personal responsibility.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Explain why personal accountability remains with individuals rather than artificial intelligence systems, reflecting on how professional obligations apply when using automated assistance.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Reflect on personal accountability when using artificial intelligence in professional tasks, recognising obligations to verify outputs and accept responsibility for decisions made.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Critically reflect on accountability in artificial intelligence-assisted professional practice, ensuring automated assistance enhances rather than obscures individual responsibility for outcomes.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Examine accountability structures in human-artificial intelligence collaborative work, addressing responsibility distribution and professional obligation across practice contexts.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes
Education
Personal accountability reflection is directly relevant to professional educational practice - teachers and lecturers who use AI tools to generate feedback, assessment designs, or learning materials remain personally accountable for their quality, accuracy, and appropriateness for their specific learners. Students can apply this competency by developing reflective practices around their use of AI in professional educational work, regularly examining where personal accountability lies when AI assists decisions that affect student welfare, learning outcomes, and academic integrity.
Social Sciences, Law and Business
Personal accountability is critical in high-stakes professional contexts across this discipline group - legal practitioners, social workers, policy analysts, and business leaders who use AI assistance remain personally and professionally accountable for the decisions and outputs that affect clients, service users, and the public. Students can apply this competency by developing reflective practices around AI-assisted professional decision-making, examining where personal professional accountability lies when AI tools contribute to consequential decisions in their field.
Medicine, Health and Life Sciences
Personal accountability reflection is a direct patient safety and professional ethics concern - clinicians who use AI diagnostic support, treatment recommendation tools, and clinical documentation assistance remain personally accountable for the care decisions that affect their patients. Students can apply this competency by developing reflective clinical practices around AI-assisted decision-making, regularly examining where personal clinical accountability lies, and ensuring that AI assistance enhances rather than substitutes for the professional judgement and ethical responsibility that patient care requires.
5.7 Understanding Artificial Intelligence Governance and Regulatory Responsibilities in Professional Practice
Contextual
Specialist title: Understanding AI Governance and Regulatory Responsibilities in Professional Practice
Panel validation note: Confirmed applicable by the Education panel. The Arts and Humanities and Sciences, Engineering and Mathematics panels recommended merger with 5.6. The Social Sciences, Law and Business and Medicine, Health and Life Sciences panels indicated not applicable. The interdisciplinary panel decided 5.6 and 5.7 should not be merged and designated both as Contextual.
