This competency is recommended for disciplines and professional contexts where students will encounter or contribute to organisational AI governance frameworks - particularly education management, public administration, healthcare management, and business leadership roles.
Rationale
Organisations deploying artificial intelligence operate within evolving governance landscapes covering legal compliance, accountability structures, and risk management. Students who understand these organisational governance dimensions are better equipped to navigate institutional responsibilities in professional contexts, recognise when organisations fail their governance obligations, and connect governance principles to practical decisions about responsible technology adoption. This competency focuses entirely on organisational governance structures and responsibilities, distinct from the individual legal compliance awareness addressed in 5.3 and the personal reflective accountability addressed in 5.6.
Competency Statement
Explain why artificial intelligence systems require organisational governance oversight and regulatory compliance, recognising how governance frameworks, accountability standards, and regulatory requirements shape responsible institutional AI adoption and influence professional liability, risk management, and stakeholder trust.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Describe examples where artificial intelligence use in organisations requires governance oversight, recognising that regulated sectors have rules protecting people and ensuring fairness.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Explain why organisations using artificial intelligence must address governance requirements, interpreting how compliance standards influence responsible institutional AI adoption.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Evaluate governance considerations when engaging with artificial intelligence in an organisational context, analysing how regulatory frameworks affect responsible institutional decisions.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Assess governance implications of artificial intelligence adoption in professional settings, examining how compliance and accountability frameworks shape responsible organisational deployment.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Examine organisational governance frameworks for artificial intelligence, addressing regulatory effectiveness and accountability mechanisms for responsible institutional technology adoption.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes
Physical Science, Engineering and Mathematics
Organisational AI governance is relevant in research institutions and engineering organisations where AI systems are developed and deployed at scale. Students can apply this competency by examining how research institutions and engineering organisations develop governance frameworks for responsible AI development and deployment, what compliance obligations apply to AI systems developed or used in their professional context, and how governance principles translate into practical research integrity and engineering accountability frameworks.
Arts and Humanities
Organisational AI governance is relevant in cultural institutions, heritage organisations, and creative industries where AI adoption raises significant questions about IP, cultural sensitivity, and institutional accountability. Students can apply this competency by examining how cultural organisations develop governance frameworks for responsible AI adoption, what institutional accountability obligations arise from AI use in cultural and creative contexts, and how governance principles apply to the specific risks and responsibilities of AI in their professional sector.
Education
Organisational AI governance is directly relevant to educational leadership and management contexts - schools, colleges, and universities increasingly need institutional AI governance frameworks that address procurement, data protection, academic integrity, and equitable access. Students in education leadership programmes can apply this competency by examining how educational institutions are developing AI governance frameworks, what regulatory requirements apply to AI adoption in educational settings, and how governance principles translate into practical institutional policy and procurement decisions.
Domain 6

Cognitive Diversity, Accessibility, and Inclusive AI Literacy

Domain Description

This domain develops understanding of how artificial intelligence technologies can be used inclusively and accessibly across diverse cognitive profiles, linguistic backgrounds, and learning needs. Competencies address adaptive and inclusive AI use, evaluation of AI tools for equity and access, and reflection on AI's impact on personal learning and cognitive development. An Arts and Humanities scoped competency addresses equitable participation in artistic and humanistic expression and assessment through AI. Together these competencies equip students to engage with AI in ways that recognise and respect the full diversity of human cognition, culture, and circumstance.

Competencies in this domain: 4  ·  3 Universal 1 Arts & Humanities Scoped
6.1 Inclusive and Adaptive Artificial Intelligence Use
Universal
Specialist title: Utilising AI as Assistive Technology for Neurodivergent Learners / Adapting AI Explanations for Diverse Cognitive and Linguistic Contexts
Rationale
Artificial intelligence tools are not cognitively neutral. The same tool used in the same way can function as an enabler for one student and a barrier for another, depending on cognitive profile, linguistic background, and learning preference. Students who can select, configure, and adapt AI tools across diverse contexts - including using assistive technologies for neurodivergent learners and adjusting usage strategies for linguistic diversity - are better equipped for equitable participation in cognitively and culturally varied teams and environments. This is a core dimension of AI literacy, not a specialist concern for a subset of learners.
Competency Statement
Select, configure, and apply artificial intelligence tools adaptively across diverse cognitive processing styles, linguistic backgrounds, and learning needs, using assistive technologies including text-to-speech, summarisation, and structured planning aids to ensure equitable and effective participation in academic, professional, and collaborative environments.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Identify artificial intelligence tools and features that support different cognitive and linguistic needs, recognising that configuration and tool selection affect accessibility and equitable participation.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Apply artificial intelligence assistive technologies and adaptive configurations to support learning across varied cognitive and linguistic contexts, explaining how tool selection affects equitable participation.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Evaluate artificial intelligence tools for adaptability across diverse cognitive profiles and linguistic backgrounds, selecting configurations that support equitable engagement in academic and professional contexts.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Analyse how artificial intelligence workflows can be adapted for cognitive diversity and linguistic inclusion, developing evidence-informed strategies for equitable engagement in professional contexts.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Examine adaptive artificial intelligence use across cognitive and linguistic diversity, contributing to scholarship on inclusive AI literacy and equitable participation in AI-enabled environments.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes
Physical Science, Engineering and Mathematics
The Sciences, Engineering and Mathematics panel indicated this competency as footnote only in its original form, suggesting relevance at awareness level rather than as a core competency. The most relevant dimension for this discipline group is the adaptive configuration aspect - understanding how AI tools can be configured to support team members with diverse cognitive profiles and linguistic backgrounds in research and engineering practice. Students can contextualise through examining accessibility features of AI research tools and considering how inclusive AI use supports equitable participation in scientific and engineering teams.
Arts and Humanities
The Arts and Humanities panel recommended the merger of both original competencies, meaning both the assistive technology dimension and the adaptive usage dimension are directly relevant. Students can contextualise through examining how AI assistive technologies support creative and scholarly practice for students with diverse cognitive profiles, and how AI tools can be adapted to support multilingual participation in artistic and humanistic expression. The discipline's existing engagement with accessibility, diversity, and inclusive practice provides a strong foundation.
Education
The Education panel validated both original competencies as applicable, making this one of the most directly relevant competencies for this discipline group. Both dimensions - AI as assistive technology and adaptive AI use for diverse learners - are central to professional educational practice. Students can apply this competency by developing professional expertise in AI assistive technologies relevant to their educational context, understanding how to support learners with diverse cognitive profiles and linguistic backgrounds through appropriate AI tool selection and configuration, and designing learning environments that leverage AI inclusively.
Social Sciences, Law and Business
The Social Sciences, Law and Business panel recommended merger of both original competencies, meaning both dimensions are directly relevant. Students can contextualise through examining how AI assistive technologies support diverse participation in social science research, legal practice, and business contexts, and how adaptive AI use can support colleagues, clients, and service users with diverse cognitive profiles and linguistic backgrounds. The discipline's engagement with social equity and inclusion provides a strong foundation for applying this competency professionally.
Medicine, Health and Life Sciences
The Medicine, Health and Life Sciences panel indicated not applicable to both original competencies. The most relevant dimension for this discipline group is understanding how AI assistive technologies can support patients and service users with diverse cognitive and communication needs, and how adaptive AI use can improve health communication and clinical accessibility. Students can contextualise through examining AI tools used to support patients with communication difficulties, cognitive impairments, or language barriers, and considering how inclusive AI use supports equitable access to healthcare.
6.3 Evaluating Artificial Intelligence Tools for Equity and Inclusive Access
Universal
Specialist title: Ensuring Linguistic and Cultural Equity in AI Tool Selection and Use / Evaluating Accessibility and Equity in Artificial Intelligence Technologies
Rationale
Many artificial intelligence systems are built primarily on English-language data and Western cultural assumptions, performing poorly across other languages and cultural contexts. Simultaneously, cost barriers, technical requirements, and inaccessible interface design exclude users with limited financial resources or diverse physical and sensory abilities. Students who can evaluate AI tools against linguistic, cultural, socioeconomic, and accessibility criteria are equipped to make more equitable technology decisions and advocate for systems that expand rather than restrict participation. This competency connects technical evaluation skills to broader questions of digital equity and social justice.
Competency Statement
Evaluate artificial intelligence tools for linguistic accessibility, cultural appropriateness, affordability, and inclusive design, identifying barriers that exclude users across different languages, socioeconomic circumstances, abilities, and cultural contexts, and applying this evaluation to inform responsible tool selection and advocacy for equitable access.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Recognise that artificial intelligence tools may exclude users through language limitations, cultural assumptions, cost barriers, or inaccessible design, identifying examples affecting diverse populations.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Compare artificial intelligence tools for linguistic coverage, cultural appropriateness, and accessibility, explaining how design and cost barriers reduce equitable access for diverse users.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Evaluate artificial intelligence tools for linguistic equity, cultural sensitivity, affordability, and accessibility standards, assessing implications for equitable participation across diverse contexts.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Design technology selection strategies that prioritise linguistic, cultural, and accessibility equity, advocating for artificial intelligence tools that serve diverse populations in professional settings.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Examine linguistic equity, cultural appropriateness, and inclusive design in artificial intelligence tools, contributing to scholarship on equitable technology access across international populations.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes
Physical Science, Engineering and Mathematics
The Sciences, Engineering and Mathematics panel recommended merging both original competencies, suggesting relevance primarily through the accessibility and inclusive design dimension - understanding how AI research tools may exclude users with diverse physical and sensory abilities or from under-resourced institutional contexts is directly relevant to equitable scientific practice. Students can contextualise through examining the accessibility standards of AI tools used in research and engineering, and considering how inclusive tool selection supports equitable participation in scientific communities.
Arts and Humanities
The Arts and Humanities panel validated both original competencies, meaning both the linguistic and cultural equity dimension and the accessibility and inclusive design dimension are directly relevant. Students can contextualise through examining how AI tools used in creative and scholarly practice perform across different languages and cultural contexts, assessing whether tools serve multilingual and multicultural scholarly communities equitably, and evaluating accessibility standards for creative AI tools used by practitioners with diverse physical and sensory abilities.
Education
The Education panel validated both original competencies, making this a highly relevant competency for professional educational practice. Both dimensions apply directly - evaluating educational AI tools for linguistic and cultural equity ensures they serve diverse learner populations, while assessing accessibility and affordability ensures equitable access regardless of disability or socioeconomic background. Students can apply this competency by developing professional expertise in evaluating educational AI tools against equity and inclusion criteria, advocating for tools that serve all learners rather than reinforcing existing educational inequalities.
Social Sciences, Law and Business
The Social Sciences, Law and Business panel validated the linguistic and cultural equity dimension but not the accessibility and inclusive design dimension. The most relevant dimension for this discipline group is therefore the linguistic and cultural equity aspect - understanding how AI tools used in social research, legal practice, and business contexts may embed cultural assumptions or linguistic limitations that affect their reliability and fairness across diverse populations. Students can contextualise through examining the cultural and linguistic equity implications of AI tools relevant to their professional field.
Medicine, Health and Life Sciences
The Medicine, Health and Life Sciences panel indicated not applicable to both original competencies. The most relevant dimension for this discipline group is the accessibility and inclusive design aspect - understanding how AI health tools may exclude patients with diverse physical, sensory, or linguistic needs is directly relevant to equitable healthcare practice. Students can contextualise through examining the accessibility standards of clinical AI tools, assessing whether health AI systems serve diverse patient populations equitably, and advocating for inclusive design in healthcare technology procurement.
6.4 Reflecting on Artificial Intelligence's Impact on Personal Learning and Cognitive Development
Universal
Specialist title: Reflecting on AI's Impact on Personal Learning and Cognitive Development
Rationale
Artificial intelligence tools can accelerate many learning tasks, yet excessive reliance on automated assistance may weaken foundational cognitive skills. When complex thinking processes are consistently outsourced to AI systems, students risk losing opportunities to develop reasoning abilities, disciplinary intuition, and deep conceptual understanding. Students who reflect critically on how AI affects their own learning are better equipped to use AI tools in ways that strengthen rather than replace the cognitive foundations required for long-term professional expertise and intellectual independence.
Competency Statement
Reflect critically on how artificial intelligence assistance influences personal learning processes, including effects on memory, problem-solving, and conceptual understanding, developing strategies that balance AI-supported efficiency with the deliberate practice needed to build foundational knowledge and long-term expertise.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Reflect on personal experiences using artificial intelligence for learning, recognising how automated assistance may influence understanding, memory, and study habits.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Describe how reliance on artificial intelligence tools affects personal memory, understanding, and problem-solving, identifying strategies for balanced learning.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Evaluate the impact of artificial intelligence on personal cognitive development, analysing how deliberate practice and AI support can be balanced in disciplinary learning.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Design reflective strategies that integrate artificial intelligence into learning whilst preserving deep understanding, disciplinary reasoning, and long-term expertise development.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Examine how artificial intelligence influences personal cognitive development and expertise formation, contributing to understanding of AI-mediated learning across disciplinary contexts.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes

This competency applies broadly across all discipline groups:

This competency applies broadly and consistently across all five discipline groups - the core reflective practice of examining how AI affects personal learning, memory, and cognitive development is equally relevant regardless of disciplinary context. Whether a student is learning scientific reasoning, developing legal analytical skills, building pedagogical expertise, cultivating creative practice, or developing clinical judgement, the risk of over-reliance on AI assistance undermining deep learning is present across all disciplines. Programme teams in each discipline should contextualise this competency through the specific cognitive skills and forms of deep understanding most central to their disciplinary and professional formation - the foundational reasoning abilities, disciplinary intuitions, and professional expertise that cannot be developed through AI-assisted shortcuts.

6.AH Using Artificial Intelligence to Participate Equitably in Artistic and Humanistic Expression and Assessment
Arts & Humanities Scoped
Specialist title: Participating in and Designing Inclusive AI-Integrated Assessments
Panel validation note: Validated as applicable by the Arts and Humanities expert panel. Not confirmed as applicable by the Sciences, Engineering and Mathematics; Education; Social Sciences, Law and Business; or Medicine, Health and Life Sciences panels. The interdisciplinary panel retained this competency as Contextual with Arts and Humanities disciplinary scope. Programme teams outside Arts and Humanities should exercise independent judgement about relevance to their disciplinary context, noting that the original title and framing was specific to the assessment and expression practices of Arts and Humanities disciplines.
Rationale
Traditional artistic and humanistic assessment formats can disadvantage students with diverse cognitive profiles or linguistic backgrounds, limiting how knowledge and creativity are expressed and evaluated. Artificial intelligence enables more flexible and inclusive participation in creative and critical practice, allowing students to engage through diverse formats including visual, audio, and interactive modes. Students who can participate equitably in AI-enabled artistic and humanistic expression and assessment are better prepared to advocate for inclusive practice in professional and creative contexts.
Competency Statement
Participate in artistic and humanistic expression and assessment practices that integrate artificial intelligence to support diverse and inclusive demonstrations of learning, engaging with AI-enabled multimodal formats that ensure equitable access to creative and critical practice regardless of cognitive profile, linguistic background, or prior technical experience.
▸ FHEQ Level Descriptors (Levels 4-8)
Level 4 (Year 1 Undergraduate / Certificate of Higher Education)
Recognise how artificial intelligence supports diverse participation in artistic and humanistic practice, identifying examples where multimodal formats improve equitable access to creative work.
Level 5 (Year 2 Undergraduate / Diploma of Higher Education)
Apply artificial intelligence tools within artistic and humanistic expression and assessment, explaining how multimodal formats support inclusive demonstration of creative and critical learning.
Level 6 (Final-year Undergraduate / Bachelor's Degree)
Evaluate artificial intelligence-enabled approaches to artistic and humanistic expression, analysing how multimodal formats support equitable participation across diverse cognitive and linguistic backgrounds.
Level 7 (Master's Degree)
Develop strategies for artificial intelligence-integrated artistic and humanistic expression and assessment that ensure equitable participation across diverse cognitive and linguistic contexts.
Level 8 (Doctoral / Research)
Examine frameworks for inclusive artificial intelligence-enabled artistic and humanistic expression and assessment, addressing multimodal evaluation and equitable creative practice.
▸ Disciplinary Contextualisation Notes
Arts and Humanities
This competency was validated specifically by the Arts and Humanities panel and is most directly relevant to disciplines including fine art, creative writing, literature, history, philosophy, film, music, drama, cultural studies, and related fields. Students can apply this competency by examining how AI-enabled multimodal formats - audio essays, video arguments, interactive narratives, visual commentaries - can provide more equitable pathways for demonstrating artistic and humanistic learning than traditional written formats alone. At higher FHEQ levels, students in professional roles in arts education, cultural institutions, and creative industries can develop and advocate for inclusive AI-integrated assessment and expression practices that serve diverse creative and scholarly communities.
IAICF Curriculum Co-Design Workshop
First Impressions Audit
TIME: 5–7 minutes
MODE: Individual, silent reading
OUTPUT: One honest observation per question
Advance HE Fund ID: 0000000204  ·  Ethics Ref: ERN_5490-Feb2026
How to use this sheet. As you read the IAICF v3.0 framework, jot down your immediate reactions to the three questions below. One honest observation per question is enough; there are no wrong answers. Leave this sheet with the facilitator at the end of the session.
Participant Details (optional)
Name
Discipline / Role
1
A title that did not match what you expected
Read the competency titles. Find one where the title gave you a different expectation from what the statement or descriptors actually say.
Look for: a title that is vague, misleading, or uses different language from the content beneath it.
Competency reference
What did you expect from the title - and what did you find instead?
2
A descriptor you could not immediately assess
Read the level descriptors for one competency relevant to your discipline. Find one you could not immediately translate into a specific assessment task.
Look for: descriptors that are too vague, use unmeasurable language, or describe a capability without indicating how it could be evidenced.
Competency reference
FHEQ Level (tick one)
☐ L4   ☐ L5   ☐ L6
☐ L7   ☐ L8
What makes this descriptor hard to assess?
If you can, suggest what evidence a student could produce to demonstrate this descriptor:
3
An assumption that does not hold in your context
Find one place in the framework that assumes something about your students, institution, or discipline that is not true for your context.
Look for: assumptions about AI tool access, prior technical knowledge, institutional resources, Western educational norms, or disciplinary practices.
Where in the framework (competency ref or section)
What is assumed - and what is the reality in your context?
Bonus - if you have time
Name one competency or descriptor that works well and would not change. What makes it work?
Please leave this sheet with the facilitator at the end of the session.
Your responses - anonymised - feed directly into IAICF v3.0 framework revisions and the implementation toolkit. Happy for your institution to be credited?   ☐ Yes
IAICF Curriculum Co-Design Workshop
Activity 1B: Module-Level Mapping
TIME: 50 minutes
USE IF: you brought a module specification
OUTPUT: one-semester implementation plan
Advance HE Fund ID: 0000000204  ·  Ethics Ref: ERN_5490-Feb2026
This template is for participants working with a specific module specification. It takes you from competency selection through to a one-semester implementation plan - something you can take away and use immediately. If you are mapping at programme level, use Activity 1A (the programme mapping template) instead.
Your Module
Module name
Discipline
FHEQ Level
☐ L4 (Year 1 UG)  ☐ L5 (Year 2 UG)  ☐ L6 (Final UG)  ☐ L7 (Masters)  ☐ L8 (Doctoral)
Approx. student numbers
Current AI content
☐ Substantial  ☐ Some  ☐ Minimal  ☐ None yet
Main assessment types used
Step 1: Select your competencies (10 minutes)

Tick the competencies most relevant to your module - aim for 4 to 6. You do not need to select evenly across domains. Focus on the ones that connect most naturally to your existing content, learning outcomes, or assessment.

U - Universal: expected across all disciplines; start here if you are new to the framework.
C - Contextual: recommended where directly relevant to your professional or disciplinary context.
A/H - Arts & Humanities scoped.
Ref Competency title U/C Select
Domain 1: Conceptual Understanding of Artificial Intelligence Systems
☐ 1.1 Understanding How Artificial Intelligence Systems Are Built and Trained by Data U ☐
☐ 1.2 Understanding Different Artificial Intelligence Model Types and Their Applications U ☐
☐ 1.3 Identifying Fundamental Limitations and Failure Modes of Artificial Intelligence Systems U ☐
☐ 1.4 Recognising How Artificial Intelligence Shapes Everyday Services and Decisions U ☐
Domain 2: Critical Evaluation of AI Outputs and System Performance
☐ 2.1 Evaluating the Accuracy and Reliability of Artificial Intelligence-Generated Information U ☐
☐ 2.2 Detecting Bias and Unequal Representation in Artificial Intelligence Outputs U ☐
☐ 2.3 Analysing the Logical Consistency of Artificial Intelligence-Generated Reasoning U ☐
☐ 2.4 Assessing Artificial Intelligence Outputs for Professional Tone and Contextual Fit U ☐
☐ 2.5 Verifying the Authenticity of Artificial Intelligence-Generated Synthetic Media U ☐
☐ 2.6 Evaluating the Credibility of Information and Claims About Artificial Intelligence U ☐
Domain 3: Ethical and Social Implications of Artificial Intelligence
☐ 3.1 Evaluating Personal Data Footprints Within Artificial Intelligence Systems U ☐
☐ 3.2 Assessing the Environmental Impact and Sustainability of Artificial Intelligence Technologies U ☐
☐ 3.3 Examining Artificial Intelligence's Broader Impact on Work and Society U ☐
☐ 3.4 Preserving Human Agency and Decision-Making Authority in Artificial Intelligence-Assisted Processes U ☐
☐ 3.5 Analysing Artificial Intelligence's Impact on Information Integrity and Public Discourse U ☐
☐ 3.7 Identifying Professional Contexts Where Artificial Intelligence Use Is Inappropriate U ☐
☐ 3.6 Recognising the Psycho-Emotional Effect of Human-Like Artificial Intelligence Responses on Human Users C ☐
Domain 4: Practical Application and Professional Use of Artificial Intelligence Tools
☐ 4.1 Designing Effective Prompts and Queries for Artificial Intelligence Systems U ☐
☐ 4.2 Selecting and Integrating Artificial Intelligence Tools in Professional Workflows U ☐
☐ 4.3 Using Artificial Intelligence as a Tool Whilst Preserving Critical Judgement U ☐
☐ 4.4 Diagnosing and Resolving Artificial Intelligence Failures and Unhelpful Outputs in Practice U ☐
Domain 5: Governance, Accountability, and Regulation of Artificial Intelligence
☐ 5.1 Examining Artificial Intelligence Tools for Trustworthiness, Transparency, and Reliability [Merged: 5.1+5.6] U ☐
☐ 5.2 Disclosing and Documenting Artificial Intelligence Use and Contributions in Academic and Professional Work U ☐
☐ 5.3 Interpreting Relevant Legal and Regulatory Frameworks Governing Artificial Intelligence U ☐
☐ 5.4 Applying Professional Ethics and Sector-Specific Codes to Artificial Intelligence Use U ☐
☐ 5.5 Evaluating Intellectual Property, Copyright, and Training Data Ethics in Artificial Intelligence U ☐
☐ 5.6 Reflecting on Personal and Professional Accountability in Artificial Intelligence-Assisted Work C ☐
☐ 5.7 Understanding Artificial Intelligence Governance and Regulatory Responsibilities in Professional Practice C ☐
Domain 6: Cognitive Diversity, Accessibility, and Inclusive AI Literacy
☐ 6.1 Inclusive and Adaptive Artificial Intelligence Use [Merged: 6.1+6.5] U ☐
☐ 6.3 Evaluating Artificial Intelligence Tools for Equity and Inclusive Access [Merged: 6.3+6.4] U ☐
☐ 6.4 Reflecting on Artificial Intelligence's Impact on Personal Learning and Cognitive Development U ☐
☐ 6.AH Using Artificial Intelligence to Participate Equitably in Artistic and Humanistic Expression and Assessment A/H ☐
Step 2: Map selected competencies to your module (25 minutes)

For each competency you selected, work through the five columns. The blue-shaded columns (teaching and assessment approaches) are where the cheatsheets are most useful - dip in when you need a specific idea.

Blue columns: suggested starting points from the cheatsheets. Overwrite with your own ideas. If a competency does not fit at all: note why in the Priority column - that is useful data for the research team.
Selected competency (ref & short title) Fits existing LO? Embed without restructuring? Teaching activity (see cheatsheet) Assessment approach (see cheatsheet) Implementation priority & main barrier
☐ Fits existing LO directly
☐ Fits with minor wording change
☐ Needs a new LO added 		☐ Immediate – next semester
☐ Short term – next year
☐ Longer term – needs planning
Main barrier:
☐ Fits existing LO directly
☐ Fits with minor wording change
☐ Needs a new LO added 		☐ Immediate – next semester
☐ Short term – next year
☐ Longer term – needs planning

☐ Fits existing LO directly
☐ Fits with minor wording change
☐ Needs a new LO added 		☐ Immediate – next semester
☐ Short term – next year
☐ Longer term – needs planning

☐ Fits existing LO directly
☐ Fits with minor wording change
☐ Needs a new LO added 		☐ Immediate – next semester
☐ Short term – next year
☐ Longer term – needs planning

☐ Fits existing LO directly
☐ Fits with minor wording change

☐ Fits with minor wording change
☐ Needs a new LO added 		☐ Immediate – next semester
☐ Short term – next year
☐ Longer term – needs planning
Main barrier:
Step 3: Adapt your learning outcomes (10 minutes)

Choose 2-3 existing learning outcomes from your module specification that could be adapted to embed an IAICF competency with minimal restructuring. The aim is small, practical changes - not a module redesign.

Practical tip: the simplest approach is to add a qualifying clause to an existing outcome. For example: "...critically evaluating the accuracy and limitations of any artificial intelligence tools used in the process" (embeds 2.1). Or: "...with explicit reference to relevant professional ethics codes and any artificial intelligence assistance declared" (embeds 5.2 and 5.4). You do not need to rewrite the whole outcome.
Existing learning outcome (copy from module spec) Revised learning outcome - embedding the IAICF competency
Step 4: One-semester implementation plan (10 minutes)

Pick the 2-4 competencies you will realistically embed in the next semester. Be specific about where they go in the module and what the first step is. This is the output you take away and use.

Competency Where in the module Teaching activity Assessment task or element First step before next review
What do I need that I do not currently have - resources, expertise, institutional support, or time - to make this plan happen?
Who else in my department or institution should see this plan - and what is the best way to make the case to them?
Group Share - last 5 minutes
Each participant shares briefly:
  • The module you worked with and which domain(s) your selected competencies came from.
  • One learning outcome adaptation you found surprisingly easy - and one that proved harder than expected.
  • The single most concrete first step in your implementation plan - what you will do before the next module review.
What happens with this template: your completed module mapping will contribute to the IAICF case study bank, showing how the framework maps to real modules across disciplines. It will be anonymised unless you opt in to named attribution. The learning outcome adaptations you develop here are particularly valuable as exemplars for the toolkit.
IAICF Curriculum Co-Design Workshop
Activity 3: Equity Review
TIME: 30 minutes (15 individual + 10 group + 5 individual close)
PURPOSE: Ensure the IAICF framework serves every learner effectively
Advance HE Fund ID: 0000000204  ·  Ethics Ref: ERN_5490-Feb2026
This activity is not about identifying individual failings. It is about systematically examining whether the IAICF framework and its implementation can work equitably across the full diversity of students in UK higher education. Your professional knowledge of your student population is the most important thing you bring to this room. You are not required to disclose personal experiences - conduct this audit from your professional knowledge of student populations.
Part 1: Individual equity audit (15 minutes)

Select 2 to 3 competencies or framework sections that concern you most from an equity perspective. Use the six dimensions below to guide your thinking. Depth on the ones that concern you most is more valuable than breadth across all competencies.

A useful starting point: Domain 6 competencies (6.1, 6.3, 6.4, 6.AH) are explicitly about inclusion and accessibility - they make natural audit subjects. Domain 5 governance competencies (5.3, 5.6, 5.7) raise questions about who benefits from AI regulation. Domain 3.6 raises specific concerns about vulnerable users.
Competency / section & equity dimension
(use the six dimensions below as a guide)		 What is the specific concern? Which students might be disadvantaged and how? Severity Suggested revision or implementation mitigation
☐ Critical
☐ Significant
☐ Moderate
☐ Minor
☐ Critical
☐ Significant
☐ Moderate
☐ Minor
☐ Critical
☐ Significant
☐ Moderate
☐ Minor
☐ Critical
☐ Significant
☐ Moderate
☐ Minor
☐ Critical
☐ Significant
☐ Moderate
☐ Minor
☐ Critical
☐ Significant
☐ Moderate
☐ Minor
☐ Critical
☐ Significant
☐ Moderate
☐ Minor
☐ Critical
☐ Significant
☐ Moderate
☐ Minor
Six Equity Dimensions - use these to guide your audit

For each dimension, the key question is whether the framework creates a structural barrier - not whether a determined individual student could overcome it.

Access and resources
Start with: 6.3 and 4.2
Does this competency or its teaching assume access to paid AI tools, reliable broadband, or specific devices that not all students have? Could students without premium tool access demonstrate the same competency as those with paid subscriptions?
Prior knowledge and experience
Start with: 1.1 and 1.2
Does the competency assume computing or technical background that students from under-resourced schools or non-technical disciplines may not have? What prior knowledge is treated as given rather than taught?
Language and communication
Start with: 2.4 and 6.1
Is the language of the competency, its teaching, and its assessment accessible to students for whom English is an additional language, or who have dyslexia or other language processing differences?
Neurodiversity and learning styles
Start with: 6.1 and 6.AH
Does the competency allow multiple pathways to demonstrate it, or does it privilege a single mode (e.g. written argument) that may systematically disadvantage neurodivergent students?
Cultural and contextual assumptions
Start with: 2.4 and 6.3
Does the competency reflect primarily Western or Anglophone norms about AI, technology, ethics, or professional practice? Are the examples, cases, and tools used culturally specific in ways that exclude international students?
Structural and systemic barriers
Start with: 3.3 and 6.3
Does the framework acknowledge that AI systems themselves embed discrimination, and that some student groups are more likely to be harmed by AI than to benefit from it? Whose knowledge and experience is centred in the framework?
Part 2: Group synthesis (10 minutes)
Round 1 (5 minutes)
Each participant shares one critical concern.
Critical concern raised Student group(s) most affected Suggested change - framework wording, implementation guidance, or institutional action
Round 2 (5 minutes)
Identify patterns and prioritise.
As a group, discuss:
  • Which concerns were raised by more than one person? Those are the highest priority for the framework team.
  • Are problems fixable through framework wording revision, or do they require institutional implementation guidance?
  • Which student group in your institution is currently least well served by an AI competency framework?
Top 3 equity revisions the group agrees are most urgent:
1
2
3
Individual close - before you leave (3 minutes)
One practical step your institution or programme could take in the next term to implement this framework more equitably - be specific about who would need to do what.
One thing in the IAICF framework that gives you genuine confidence it can serve diverse learners well - name the specific competency or design feature.
What happens with your findings: your completed audit will be used to identify equity revisions needed in the IAICF v3.0 framework before publication. Critical and Significant concerns will be addressed in framework wording. Patterns across workshops will inform implementation guidance for institutions. Your institution will be credited collectively - individual contributions will not be identified.
IAICF Curriculum Co-Design Workshop
Assessment Methods Reference
Version 3.0  ·  40 assessment types across 8 categories  ·  Structured examples span all 6 IAICF domains.
How to use this sheet: find the assessment category that fits your discipline and context. Each example shows Task, Evidence, AI stance, and which IAICF competencies it addresses. Adapt freely - the examples are starting points, not prescriptions.
S + F = Summative & Formative Summative only Formative only
WRITTEN ASSIGNMENTS
Essay
S + F
Students construct a sustained written argument demonstrating critical thinking, disciplinary reasoning, and synthesis of evidence across a defined question.
Task: Critically evaluate whether AI governance frameworks adequately protect workers in algorithmically managed workplaces (links to 3.3, 5.3, 5.7). Evidence: Engagement with regulatory literature, original argument, awareness of enforcement gaps. AI stance: AI-permitted - students must declare and critically assess any AI use in a dedicated statement.
Report
S + F
Structured professional document presenting findings, analysis, and recommendations organised into discrete, clearly labelled sections with professional register.
Task: Produce a provenance and transparency report evaluating a named AI tool for use by a healthcare organisation (links to 5.1, 5.3, 5.4). Evidence: Use of NIST AI RMF framework, evidence-based recommendation, professional format. AI stance: AI-integrated - students use AI to draft sections and critically audit the output.
Reflective Writing
Formative
Personal structured reflection on experience, learning process, or professional development, developing metacognitive awareness and critical self-evaluation.
Task: Write 600 words reflecting on a moment when an AI tool surprised, misled, or helped you - and what it revealed about your own cognitive dependence on it (links to 6.4, 4.3). Evidence: Specificity, honesty about uncertainty, connection to IAICF competency descriptors. AI stance: AI-prohibited - the reflection must be authentic and personally experienced.
AI Interaction Transcript Analysis
S + F
Students submit and critically annotate a real AI conversation, demonstrating evaluative and metacognitive competency through structured commentary on the exchange.
Task: Submit a 15-turn prompt-response transcript from a governance or professional ethics task and annotate it for logical consistency, bias, and professional appropriateness (links to 2.3, 2.4, 5.4). Evidence: Quality of annotation, depth of critique, awareness of what AI cannot do professionally. AI stance: AI-integrated - the transcript is the primary artefact of assessment.
Policy Brief
S + F
Concise professional document advising a named decision-maker on a specific issue, requiring synthesis of evidence, regulatory awareness, and practical recommendation.
Task: Draft a two-page AI attribution policy for a professional body in your discipline, citing COPE guidance and sector-specific codes (links to 5.2, 5.4, 5.5). Evidence: Regulatory accuracy, practical specificity, professional register, evidence base. AI stance: AI-permitted as drafting aid - all regulatory claims must be verified against primary sources.
Governance Failure Analysis
Summative
Structured written analysis of a documented AI system failure, applying governance and ethics frameworks to diagnose what went wrong and recommend remediation.
Task: Analyse a real AI incident from the AI Incident Database - diagnose the provenance, transparency, accountability, and regulatory failures that enabled it (links to 5.1, 5.6, 5.7, 3.4). Evidence: Accurate framework application, multi-level diagnosis, actionable recommendations. AI stance: AI-prohibited - reasoning and framework application must be demonstrably the student's own.
PORTFOLIO AND LONGITUDINAL
AI Use Portfolio
S + F
Curated collection of AI-assisted and AI-critical work across a module, with reflective commentary justifying selection and demonstrating competency development over time.
Task: Curate six pieces of work spanning at least four IAICF domains with a 400-word critical introduction per piece explaining what competency it demonstrates and how. Evidence: Domain range, quality of selection rationale, visible growth in critical engagement across the portfolio. AI stance: AI-integrated - portfolio documents real engagement; all AI use must be logged and attributed.
Process Portfolio
Formative
Documentation of iterative development through a task, capturing drafts, decisions, and revisions rather than final output - making thinking visible.
Task: Submit five prompt drafts and their outputs for a governance advisory task, with commentary explaining what changed between iterations and why (links to 4.1, 4.4, 5.1). Evidence: Visible iteration, quality of reflective commentary, developing critical stance toward AI output. AI stance: AI-integrated - the process of working with AI is the object of assessment.
Competency Passport
S + F
Student-maintained evidence record mapped to named competencies, built across a programme - demonstrating progression rather than performance at a single point.
Task: Maintain a digital passport recording one piece of evidence per IAICF competency encountered across your degree, with a 100-word rationale per entry. Evidence: Coverage across domains, quality of evidence selected, reflective rationale per entry. AI stance: AI-permitted for organisation and rationale drafting - evidence must be authentic.
Professional Learning Log
S + F
Structured record linking professional development or placement experience to academic frameworks, demonstrating competency in authentic work contexts.
Task: Document and critically evaluate five AI tool encounters during placement against IAICF competency descriptors - covering governance, ethics, and practical use (links to 4.2, 5.4, 5.6, 5.7). Evidence: Specificity of professional observation, analytical depth, connection to framework. AI stance: AI-permitted for structuring log entries - observations must be authentic to experience.
PRESENTATIONS AND PERFORMANCE
Individual Presentation
Summative
Oral delivery of analysis or argument to an audience, demonstrating communication competency and depth of disciplinary understanding under live conditions.
Task: Present a five-minute evaluation of an AI tool's governance suitability for a specific professional context in your discipline, responding to examiner questions (links to 5.1, 5.3). Evidence: Accuracy, critical depth, ability to respond to questions, professional register. AI stance: AI-permitted for preparation - live delivery must be the student's own.
Oral Examination (Viva)
Summative
Live examiner dialogue testing depth of understanding, reasoning, and professional judgement - designed to surface whether competency is genuinely held or performed.
Task: Ten-minute viva defending your AI tool provenance investigation and governance recommendation; examiner challenges each claim with counter-evidence (links to 5.1, 5.6). Evidence: Ability to justify, respond to challenge, demonstrate genuine reasoning versus recall. AI stance: AI-prohibited in the viva - the point is to test whether understanding is genuinely held.
Video Essay or Podcast
Summative
Student-produced multimedia artefact demonstrating disciplinary knowledge through structured argument or discussion, requiring script discipline and production.
Task: Produce a six-minute podcast episode examining the psycho-emotional design of companion AI systems and their implications for vulnerable users (links to 3.6, 3.7, 5.4). Evidence: Accuracy, ethical framing, structured argument, sourcing, audio quality. AI stance: AI-permitted for research and scripting - must be declared in a production statement.
Pecha Kucha / Lightning Talk
S + F
Highly constrained timed presentation (20 slides, 20 seconds each) requiring disciplined synthesis and precision of argument under time pressure.
Task: 20 slides, 20 seconds each - explain one IAICF contextual competency and make the case for its inclusion in a specific discipline's curriculum (links to 5.6, 5.7, 6.2, 3.6). Evidence: Clarity, pacing, precision of argument, disciplinary specificity under constraint. AI stance: AI-permitted for design - argument and narration must be the student's own.
Stakeholder Briefing Presentation
Summative
Professional presentation designed for a non-specialist senior audience, requiring translation of technical or regulatory content into accessible, actionable language.
Task: Brief a fictional board of trustees on what the EU AI Act means for their organisation's AI deployment plans, in ten minutes with Q&A (links to 5.3, 5.7, 5.5). Evidence: Accuracy of regulatory content, accessibility of language, practical relevance, composure under questioning. AI stance: AI-permitted for research and slide preparation - must be declared.
PRACTICAL AND PROJECT-BASED
Design Challenge
S + F
Open-ended creative task requiring design of a solution, policy, tool, or curriculum artefact under defined real-world constraints and user needs.
Task: Design an inclusive AI use policy for a further education college serving a diverse student population - equitable, plain-language, and practically enforceable (links to 6.1, 6.3, 5.2). Evidence: Equity consideration, practical feasibility, clarity of language, policy format. AI stance: AI-integrated - document the design process and all AI tool decisions made.
Prompt Engineering Challenge
S + F
Structured task requiring students to design, test, and critically evaluate prompt strategies for a defined professional purpose with iterative refinement.
Task: Design and test three prompt strategies for eliciting a transparent, governance-aware AI response to a high-risk professional query in your discipline (links to 4.1, 5.1). Evidence: Purposeful variation, quality of comparative analysis, critical reflection on what each strategy reveals about the tool's training. AI stance: AI-integrated - the tool and the prompting process are the objects of assessment.
AI Audit Report
Summative
Systematic professional evaluation of a real AI system against defined criteria including accuracy, bias, transparency, equity, and governance - presented in audit format.
Task: Audit a publicly available AI tool used in your discipline using a structured checklist covering provenance, bias, transparency, accessibility, and regulatory compliance (links to 5.1, 2.2, 6.3, 5.3). Evidence: Rigour of audit methodology, evidence quality, professional report format, actionable recommendations. AI stance: AI-integrated - the AI system is the object of study; document all tools used in the audit process.
Red-Teaming Exercise
S + F
Students deliberately attempt to make an AI tool produce harmful, biased, or misleading outputs, then document, analyse, and connect findings to governance frameworks.
Task: Attempt to elicit five outputs that would be professionally inappropriate or harmful in your discipline; write a 500-word analysis connecting findings to IAICF governance and ethics competencies (links to 2.2, 3.4, 5.1, 5.3). Evidence: Sophistication of adversarial approach, quality of analysis, framework application. AI stance: AI-integrated - adversarial engagement with AI is the method and the evidence.
Scenario-Based Decision Log
S + F
Students work through a complex AI deployment scenario, logging and justifying each decision at defined decision points - making professional reasoning visible and assessable.
Task: You are the AI governance lead at an NHS trust. Work through seven staged decisions about deploying a diagnostic AI tool - document your reasoning at each checkpoint (links to 5.6, 5.7, 3.4, 5.4). Evidence: Quality of reasoning at each decision point, use of governance frameworks, awareness of competing professional pressures. AI stance: AI-permitted as a thinking partner - reasoning must be the student's own at each decision point.
Human vs AI Parallel Task
S + F
Students complete a task independently, then compare their output to an AI-generated version of the same task, analysing differences and their professional implications.
Task: Complete a professional ethics analysis of an AI deployment case independently, then compare your analysis to an AI-generated version - identify what each captured that the other missed (links to 5.4, 3.7, 4.3). Evidence: Quality of comparison, analytical depth, honest reflection on relative strengths and limitations. AI stance: AI-integrated - the comparison between human and AI reasoning is the core of assessment.
Workflow Design
Summative
Students design and document a complete human-AI workflow for a professional scenario, specifying task allocation, oversight points, and accountability structures.
Task: Design a human-AI workflow for a professional decision process in your discipline - map which tasks AI supports, which are human-only, and where accountability checkpoints occur (links to 3.4, 4.2, 5.6, 5.7). Evidence: Logical task allocation, quality of human oversight design, accountability clarity, professional feasibility. AI stance: AI-permitted for drafting workflow documentation - the design decisions must be justified by the student.
INNOVATIVE AND AI-NATIVE
AI-Assisted Policy Critique
S + F
Students use AI to generate a policy position, then critically dismantle it using disciplinary knowledge, regulatory awareness, and ethical reasoning.
Task: Use AI to draft an AI governance policy for a named organisation. Then write a 600-word critique identifying regulatory gaps, equity failures, and ethical blind spots (links to 5.3, 5.7, 6.3, 3.2). Evidence: Quality of critique relative to AI output, regulatory depth, equity framing, ethical reasoning. AI stance: AI-integrated - the gap between AI output and expert critique is the learning object.
Comparative Prompting Study
S + F
Students compare outputs from multiple AI tools or prompting strategies for the same task, analysing variation, reliability, and implications for professional use.
Task: Submit identical governance-related prompts to three different AI tools. Compare outputs for regulatory accuracy, cultural bias, and professional appropriateness (links to 5.3, 2.4, 6.3). Evidence: Analytical rigour, quality of evaluation criteria, critical synthesis of differences. AI stance: AI-integrated - multi-tool critical comparison is the core task and the research method.
Digital Story or Explainer
S + F
Students create a public-facing digital resource explaining an AI concept accessibly to a defined non-specialist audience, testing communication and equity competency.
Task: Create a two-minute animated explainer or illustrated guide explaining AI accountability for a lay audience - tested with a real non-specialist reviewer (links to 6.1, 5.6, 3.5). Evidence: Accuracy, accessibility, audience awareness, creative quality, lay reviewer feedback. AI stance: AI-permitted for production tools - content accuracy is the student's responsibility.
Co-Created Assessment Criteria
Formative
Students collaborate with staff to negotiate the criteria by which their own work will be assessed - developing evaluative ownership and metacognitive awareness.
Task: In groups, draft the marking rubric for your AI governance portfolio - negotiate with the module team and justify each criterion with reference to IAICF competencies (links to 5.2, 4.3, 6.2). Evidence: Quality of criteria proposed, engagement with negotiation process, use of the resulting rubric in self-assessment. AI stance: AI-permitted as a drafting aid - students must justify all criteria through dialogue.
Equity Audit Report
Summative
Systematic review of an AI tool, policy, or curriculum resource through an equity lens, identifying structural barriers and recommending practical mitigations.
Task: Conduct an equity audit of one AI tool used in your institution - evaluate it for linguistic accessibility, cost barriers, cultural assumptions, and WCAG compliance (links to 6.3, 6.1, 5.3). Evidence: Rigour of audit approach, specificity of barriers identified, practicality of recommendations. AI stance: AI-permitted for research - the audit methodology and findings must be the student's own.
Professional Ethics Case Submission
Summative
Students produce a professionally formatted ethics case submission applying sector-specific codes to an AI-related dilemma, modelling real professional ethics processes.
Task: Draft a formal ethics case submission to your profession's regulator about an AI system being used inappropriately in your sector (links to 5.4, 3.7, 5.6). Evidence: Accuracy of professional code application, formal submission format, specificity of concerns raised. AI stance: AI-prohibited - professional ethics reasoning must be demonstrably the student's own.
EXAMINATION-BASED
Open-Book Exam
Summative
Time-constrained exam allowing defined resources, testing application and reasoning under pressure rather than recall - appropriate for governance and regulatory content.
Task: Analyse a provided AI deployment scenario - identify regulatory breaches under EU AI Act and GDPR, diagnose accountability gaps, and recommend remediation in 90 minutes (links to 5.3, 5.6, 5.7). Evidence: Speed of regulatory analysis, accuracy of diagnosis, use of frameworks under time pressure. AI stance: AI-prohibited - tests ability to apply regulatory knowledge independently.
Take-Home Exam
Summative
Extended timed response completed outside formal conditions (typically 24-48 hours), testing synthesis, independent research, and application to novel cases.
Task: Given a real AI incident released on the day, produce a 1,200-word governance failure analysis covering provenance, accountability, regulatory breach, and equity implications within 24 hours (links to 5.1, 5.6, 5.7, 6.3). Evidence: Analytical depth, response to novel case, regulatory accuracy, evidence of independent reasoning. AI stance: AI-declared - institution's policy on AI use applies; all AI use must be stated.
Adaptive Online Quiz
Formative
Technology-delivered quiz adjusting difficulty based on student performance, providing personalised formative feedback and identifying conceptual gaps for follow-up.
Task: Weekly ten-question adaptive quiz covering AI terminology, regulatory frameworks, governance principles, and ethical concepts - with immediate explanatory feedback per question. Evidence: Engagement, improvement across attempts, conceptual accuracy in later sessions. AI stance: AI-prohibited - tests foundational knowledge students must hold independently.
PEER, COLLABORATIVE AND CO-CREATED
Peer Assessment
S + F
Students evaluate each other's work against shared criteria, developing evaluative literacy, metacognition, and professional critical reading skills.
Task: Using a shared rubric, assess two peers' AI governance audit reports - provide written feedback and a provisional grade with justification (links to 5.1, 5.7). Evidence: Accuracy of grade relative to staff marker, quality and specificity of written feedback. AI stance: AI-prohibited for the assessment act itself - professional judgement must be the student's own.
Structured Peer Debate
S + F
Formally structured argument between student groups for and against a proposition, assessed on reasoning quality, evidence use, and response to counter-argument.
Task: Debate - "AI systems that display emotionally responsive behaviour should require explicit regulatory approval before deployment." Groups argue assigned positions (links to 3.6, 5.3, 5.7). Evidence: Quality of argument, evidence use, response to counter-argument, professional conduct. AI stance: AI-permitted for research and preparation - live debate must be unscripted.
Collaborative Wiki or Knowledge Base
Formative
Co-produced digital knowledge resource built iteratively, with individually attributed contributions tracked over time and subject to peer review.
Task: Build a shared wiki mapping AI regulatory developments globally - each student contributes and maintains one jurisdiction or sector, with peer review of each other's entries. Evidence: Accuracy, regularity of contribution, quality of curation, integration with peers' entries (links to 5.3, 5.5). AI stance: AI-permitted for research - all content must be verified, attributed, and editorially owned.
Mock Professional Tribunal
S + F
Students enact a professional disciplinary or regulatory hearing where AI use is at issue, developing argumentation, professional knowledge, and procedural literacy.
Task: Role-play a professional disciplinary hearing - one student defends AI use in a clinical or legal context, another prosecutes the case, a panel deliberates using professional codes (links to 5.4, 5.6, 3.7). Evidence: Depth of preparation, accuracy of professional code application, quality of argument, procedural conduct. AI stance: AI-permitted for preparation - performance must be live and substantively the student's own.
AUTHENTIC AND WORK-INTEGRATED
Live Client Brief
Summative
Real or near-real brief from an external partner, producing a professional deliverable with genuine utility and client accountability beyond the module.
Task: Produce an AI readiness and governance assessment for a community organisation or SME partner, delivered as a formal presentation to their senior leadership (links to 5.1, 5.3, 5.7, 6.3). Evidence: Professional quality, relevance to client context, ability to respond to client challenge. AI stance: AI-integrated - professional AI tool use is expected and must be disclosed to the client.
Community Co-Design Project
S + F
Applied project co-designed with a community partner, integrating academic learning with genuine civic need and producing outputs the community can actually use.
Task: Work with a community organisation to develop plain-language AI literacy guidance materials for their service users, tested with real users before submission (links to 6.1, 6.3, 3.5). Evidence: Co-design quality, accessibility of materials, user testing feedback, equity framing. AI stance: AI-integrated - tool use must be transparent to community partners throughout.
Consultancy Report
Summative
Professional-format analysis and recommendations produced for a defined client or stakeholder with sector-specific expertise and actionable recommendations.
Task: Advise a public sector body on appropriate AI governance for a specific service - covering regulatory compliance, accountability structures, and equity implications (links to 5.3, 5.6, 5.7, 6.3). Evidence: Policy accuracy, professional register, evidence-based recommendations, equity consideration. AI stance: AI-permitted as research assistant - all recommendations must be professionally defensible.
IAICF Curriculum Co-Design Workshop
Teaching and Learning Methods Reference
Version 3.0  ·  40 methods across 8 categories  ·  Structured examples span all 6 IAICF domains including AI-native approaches.
Choosing a method: consider cohort size, available contact time, whether the competency requires individual professional judgement or collaborative sense-making, and whether AI tools should be used in the teaching activity itself. AI-Integrated Methods (purple section) are purpose-built for this framework.
AI-Integrated Methods (purple section) contain methods purpose-built for IAICF competency development across all domains. These are the methods least likely to be familiar to participants and most likely to change practice. Domain links in brackets show which IAICF competencies each method primarily addresses.
LECTURE AND STRUCTURED INPUT
Flipped Classroom with Pre-Task
Students engage with recorded content before class; contact time used entirely for application, discussion, and critical work - maximising active engagement.
Before: Students watch a 12-minute video on how the EU AI Act classifies risk levels. During: Class applies the risk classification to five real AI systems in their discipline. Outcome: Contact time becomes analytical rather than transmissive; deeper engagement with regulatory content (links to 5.3).
Any size
Live Prompting or Live Audit Demo
Instructor demonstrates expert AI engagement in real time - prompting, auditing, or troubleshooting - narrating every decision as it is made.
Demo: Facilitator conducts a live provenance investigation of a named AI tool, narrating their reasoning at each step. Students suggest next moves and challenge assumptions in real time. Outcome: Expert critical practice becomes visible and imitable rather than assumed (links to 5.1, 4.1).
Any size
Mini-Lecture with Embedded Check
Short focused input of 10-15 minutes followed immediately by a live poll or exit question to diagnose understanding and anchor subsequent discussion.
Input: Ten minutes on AI accountability structures - individual, organisational, regulatory. Check: "In this scenario, who bears primary accountability and why?" - students submit live responses. Outcome: Immediate diagnostic data; subsequent discussion anchored in students' own reasoning (links to 5.6, 5.7).
Any size
Expert Practitioner Session
Practitioner from industry, policy, or a regulated profession shares real AI governance experience and fields questions using the course framework as a lens.
A public sector AI ethics lead describes three governance decisions they navigated under the EU AI Act. Students prepare questions using IAICF Domain 5 competencies as a structured lens. Outcome: Regulatory content becomes lived professional reality rather than abstract compliance (links to 5.3, 5.7).
Any size
DISCUSSION AND DIALOGUE
Socratic Seminar
Facilitator uses probing questions to draw out reasoning, surface assumptions, and deepen argument - following the discussion wherever intellectual honesty leads.
Question: "If an AI system produces a discriminatory outcome, is the developer, deployer, or user accountable - and does your answer change if the system was certified compliant?" Facilitator follows the argument, surfacing contradictions and untested assumptions. Outcome: Students develop and defend positions they did not hold at the start (links to 5.6, 5.3, 3.4).
Small-medium
Structured Academic Controversy
Students research and argue a position, then swap sides, requiring genuine engagement with opposing views rather than confirmation of initial beliefs.
Topic: "AI systems displaying emotional responsiveness should require explicit regulatory approval before deployment in professional contexts." Round 1: Groups argue their assigned position with evidence. Round 2: Groups swap and argue the opposing position with equal rigour (links to 3.6, 5.3).
Small-medium
Fishbowl Discussion
Small inner group discusses while outer group observes silently, then roles rotate - deepening listening, surfacing blind spots, and widening participation.
Inner group: Debate whether professional accountability frameworks are adequate for AI-assisted decisions. Outer group: Notes arguments missed, assumptions made, perspectives absent from the discussion. Outcome: Richer analysis; surfaces equity and disciplinary perspectives that dominant voices overlook (links to 5.6, 3.7).
Small-medium
Devil's Advocate Debate
Facilitator or designated student deliberately argues against emerging consensus to stress-test reasoning and surface weaker evidence and assumptions.
After group agrees AI literacy should be universal in HE, facilitator systematically challenges: "On what evidence? For which disciplines? At what cost?" Students must defend each claim with specific evidence or revise their position. Outcome: Intellectual resilience; reveals where understanding of the framework is genuinely shallow (links to all domains).
Small-medium
Think-Pair-Share with Escalation
Individual reflection, paired discussion, then whole-group synthesis - escalating from private to public to widen participation before confident voices dominate.
Prompt: "Name one assumption your programme currently makes about AI that might disadvantage students from low-income backgrounds." Pairs share with another pair; best responses surface to the whole group. Outcome: Every voice engaged before social hierarchy shapes the discussion (links to 6.3, 6.1).
Small-medium
PROBLEM AND ENQUIRY-BASED
Problem-Based Learning (PBL)
Groups work on a complex real-world problem to drive independent enquiry and collaborative knowledge construction across multiple sessions.
Problem: An AI diagnostic tool has produced systematically biased recommendations affecting a minority ethnic patient group in a named NHS trust. Students investigate root causes, governance failures, regulatory obligations, and remediation options over three weeks. Outcome: Simultaneous engagement with ethics, governance, equity, and practical application (links to 2.2, 3.4, 5.3, 5.6, 6.3).
Any size
Case-Based Learning
In-depth analysis of a documented real or composite case, developing analytical, evaluative, and applied competency through disciplinary frameworks.
Case: The use of AI risk assessment tools in UK criminal justice - documented failures, legal challenges, and regulatory responses. Students apply IAICF Domain 5 governance and Domain 3 ethics competencies to diagnose what failed. Outcome: Abstract competencies become grounded in documented professional consequences (links to 5.3, 5.6, 3.4, 2.2).
Any size
Wicked Problem Workshop
Students engage with a genuinely contested problem with no clean solution - developing tolerance for ambiguity and systemic thinking under professional uncertainty.
Problem: How should a university respond when it discovers that an AI tool it uses has been producing linguistically biased outputs affecting non-native English speakers? No correct answer exists - students navigate competing values, practical constraints, and regulatory obligations. Outcome: Professional judgement and ethical reasoning under genuine uncertainty (links to 6.3, 6.1, 5.3, 5.7).
Any size
Enquiry-Based Learning
Students generate their own research questions and conduct structured investigation, developing research autonomy and critical enquiry across the module.
Prompt: "How is AI governance being implemented differently across two sectors relevant to your discipline?" Students design their own data collection, conduct enquiry, and present comparative findings. Outcome: Genuine research ownership; diverse disciplinary governance perspectives surface in the room (links to 5.3, 5.7).
Any size
COLLABORATIVE AND ACTIVE LEARNING
Jigsaw
Students become domain experts in one area then teach peers - building interdependence, distributed expertise, and accountability across the group.
Groups each master one IAICF domain, then regroup so each new group has one expert per domain. Experts teach their domain and answer disciplinary questions from generalist peers. Outcome: Every student teaches and learns; curriculum coverage becomes collaborative rather than transmissive (links to all six domains).
Small-medium
Gallery Walk
Student-produced outputs displayed around the room; peers move between stations to read, comment, and build on each other's work.
Groups post their AI governance audit reports on large paper around the room. Peers leave structured responses: one strength identified, one gap found, one recommendation added. Outcome: Rapid cross-pollination of ideas across disciplines without formal presentation pressure (links to 5.1, 5.7).
Small-medium
Collaborative Concept Mapping
Groups build a shared visual map of concepts and relationships, externalising understanding and surfacing disagreements and misconceptions in real time.
Groups map relationships between IAICF Domains 3, 5, and 6 on a shared digital canvas - connecting ethics, governance, and inclusion. Disagreements about connections are discussed and resolved through evidence. Outcome: Shared conceptual architecture; surfaces where students hold different understandings of how the domains interact.
Small-medium
Role Play / Professional Simulation
Students enact professional roles in a realistic scenario, developing judgement, communication, and contextual application under controlled professional pressure.
Students role-play an AI ethics committee meeting reviewing a university's proposed algorithmic admissions tool. Roles include student representative, disability officer, legal counsel, and data protection officer. Outcome: Multi-stakeholder governance thinking; equity and accountability competencies tested simultaneously (links to 5.7, 6.3, 3.4).
Small-medium
AI-INTEGRATED TEACHING METHODS
AI Socratic Partner
Students use an AI chatbot as a thinking partner, practising argument, questioning, and critical evaluation in a structured low-stakes environment.
Task: Have a ten-minute structured argument with an AI about whether professional accountability should be shared with AI developers when AI causes harm. Challenge every response; ask it to steelman the opposing view; document where it fails to reason legally. Outcome: Develops governance argumentation; students discover the limits of AI reasoning in professional ethics contexts (links to 5.6, 5.4).
Any size
Prompt Design Workshop
Students design, test, and iteratively refine prompts for a defined professional purpose, developing systematic understanding of AI input-output relationships.
Challenge: Design a prompt that produces a reliable, jurisdiction-specific, regulatory compliance summary for a named AI tool. Students test three versions, compare outputs for regulatory accuracy, and present findings with an analysis of what each prompt design revealed. Outcome: Practical understanding that AI regulatory knowledge requires careful elicitation and critical verification (links to 4.1, 5.3).
Any size
AI Output Critique Lab
Students receive AI-generated professional content and systematically evaluate it for accuracy, bias, appropriateness, and regulatory compliance.
Students receive an AI-generated AI governance policy for a healthcare organisation without knowing it was AI-produced. They annotate it for regulatory gaps, equity omissions, and accountability weaknesses using IAICF Domain 5 competencies as a structured evaluation checklist. Outcome: Critical distance from AI outputs; transferable evaluation skills for any AI-generated professional content (links to 5.3, 5.6, 5.7, 6.3).
Any size
Human vs AI Parallel Task
Students complete a task independently, then compare their output to an AI-generated version of the same task, analysing differences and professional implications.
Students independently write a professional ethics analysis of an AI deployment case. AI does the same task simultaneously. Class compares: what did the human capture about professional accountability that AI missed, and vice versa? Outcome: Precise understanding of what professional judgement adds that AI cannot replicate (links to 5.4, 3.7, 4.3).
Any size
AI Red-Team Lab
Students deliberately attempt to make AI tools produce harmful, biased, or governance-violating outputs, then analyse what this reveals about safety and design.
Task: Attempt to elicit from an AI tool an output that would breach a named professional code of conduct in your discipline. Document what worked, what did not, and what the results imply for governance design and professional liability. Outcome: Safety-conscious AI literacy; connects directly to IAICF Domain 5 governance and Domain 3 ethics competencies (links to 5.4, 5.6, 3.4).
Any size
Iterative AI Co-Creation with Decision Log
Students work with AI across multiple sessions to develop a complex professional output, documenting every decision and reflecting on the collaboration.
Over four weeks, students co-create an AI governance framework for a named organisation with AI as a drafting collaborator. Each session documented in a decision log: what was changed, why, what the AI got wrong, what human judgement was required. Outcome: Deep practical governance competency; understanding of AI as a tool requiring active critical professional oversight (links to 5.7, 4.2, 4.3, 5.6).
Any size
Comparative AI Tool Analysis
Students compare outputs from multiple AI tools for the same professional task, developing critical evaluation skills and tool selection judgement.
Students submit an identical governance-related query to three AI tools and compare outputs for regulatory accuracy, cultural bias, and professional appropriateness. Findings presented in a structured comparative analysis with recommendations for professional use. Outcome: Practical tool selection competency; awareness that tool choice has governance and equity implications (links to 4.2, 5.1, 6.3).
Any size
AI Provenance Investigation Lab
Students systematically investigate who built an AI tool, how it was funded, what it was trained on, and what governance structures exist - developing scrutiny habits.
Each student investigates a different AI tool using Hugging Face model cards, developer documentation, and third-party audits as primary sources. Findings shared in a structured peer briefing; class builds a comparative provenance map of the tools they use. Outcome: Critical scrutiny of AI tools as a professional habit rather than a one-off exercise (links to 5.1, 5.5).
Any size
TECHNOLOGY-ENHANCED AND DIGITAL
Interactive Branching Scenario
Digital scenario requiring sequential decisions with consequences that change based on each choice - enabling risk-free experiential governance learning.
Scenario: You are AI ethics lead at a university. At each of seven decision points, choose a governance action regarding a student-facing AI tool. The simulation shows immediate and delayed consequences - some regulatory, some reputational, some affecting equity. Outcome: Consequence-based governance learning; connects professional decisions to real-world accountability outcomes (links to 5.7, 5.6, 6.3).
Any size
Live Polling and Structured Discussion
Real-time audience response generating immediate visual data, enabling diagnostic use and student-generated discussion anchors.
Open question: "Which aspect of AI governance most concerns you in your intended profession?" - displayed as a live word cloud. Facilitator leads discussion from the patterns that emerge, prioritising responses that reflect equity or accountability concerns. Outcome: Student-generated agenda; immediate insight into disciplinary governance concerns (links to 5.3, 5.6, 5.7).
Any size
Collaborative Digital Annotation
Students annotate a shared document or AI output simultaneously, with individual contributions visible and subject to peer response.
Students collaboratively annotate an AI-generated AI governance policy using Hypothesis or Google Docs comment mode. Each student must add at least three regulatory accuracy comments and one equity observation. Outcome: Visible collective critical thinking; students learn from peers' regulatory and equity expertise (links to 5.3, 6.3).
Any size
Asynchronous Video Discussion
Students record short video or audio responses to a prompt, enabling richer engagement than text forums and accommodating diverse communication styles.
Prompt: "Describe one AI tool you have encountered professionally or academically and evaluate it against one IAICF governance competency." Peers respond to each other's videos with structured questions; facilitator curates common threads for synchronous debrief. Outcome: Multimodal engagement; surfaces practitioner experience and disciplinary diversity (links to 5.1, 4.2).
Any size
Padlet Collaborative Resource Wall
