Asynchronous digital noticeboard for sharing examples, cases, and resources - building a student-generated knowledge base across the module.
Students post one real-world AI governance case or regulatory development per week, tagged by domain and sector. Facilitator curates the wall and opens each session by highlighting two or three posts for group discussion. Outcome: Student-generated curriculum content; real-world regulatory developments enter the learning environment continuously (links to 5.3, 5.7).
Any size
REFLECTIVE AND METACOGNITIVE
Structured Reflection with Framework
Guided reflection using a named model to develop depth and consistency in reflective practice, linking experience to competency development.
After each AI tool interaction across the module, students complete a three-part structured reflection: what the AI did, what I changed and why, what this reveals about the competency I am developing. Reflections submitted fortnightly with brief facilitator response. Outcome: Progressive self-awareness about AI limitations and personal cognitive dependence (links to 6.4, 4.3, 4.4).
Any size
AI Use Audit and Self-Assessment
Students document their AI use across a module, categorise it by type, and evaluate which uses developed versus which may have undermined competency.
Students track every AI interaction across four weeks, categorising by cognitive task delegated (recall, analysis, synthesis, communication, governance reasoning). Mid-module self-assessment: which categories strengthened competency, which bypassed it? Outcome: Metacognitive ownership of learning strategy; evidence base for final reflective assessment (links to 6.4, 4.3).
Any size
Exit Ticket
Formative
Three questions on a card: What is one thing you now understand about AI governance that you did not before? What question do you still have? How might you apply one thing from today in your professional context? Facilitator opens the next session by addressing the three most common questions. Outcome: Formative data; students feel heard; complexity normalised as part of professional learning (links to all domains).
Any size
Muddiest Point
Students identify the most confusing concept from a session, giving the facilitator precise diagnostic data and normalising intellectual struggle.
End of a session on regulatory frameworks: "Write down the single muddiest point - the thing that is still unclear or that you are unsure about in practice." Facilitator collects, identifies the three most common responses, and opens the next session addressing each directly. Outcome: Responsive teaching; confusion normalised; regulatory complexity becomes a shared learning challenge (links to 5.3, 5.7).
Any size
Competency Self-Rating
Students rate their own confidence against named IAICF competencies, building evaluative literacy and ownership of their development trajectory.
Mid-module: Students rate confidence against each IAICF competency on a 1-5 scale with a written justification of each rating. Results shared with personal tutor or workshop facilitator to inform planning. Outcome: Student agency; reveals perceived gaps for programme review; data for longitudinal competency tracking (links to all domains).
Any size
EXPERIENTIAL AND WORK-BASED
Live Client Governance Project
Working on a real brief from an external partner, producing a governance or ethics deliverable with genuine professional utility and client accountability.
Students produce an AI readiness and governance assessment for a community organisation or SME partner. Delivered to the client as a formal presentation with Q&A from senior stakeholders. Outcome: Professional pressure; authentic governance competency application; client feedback as formative data (links to 5.1, 5.3, 5.7, 6.3).
Small-medium
Professional Observation and Debrief
Students observe AI being used in a professional context, then debrief systematically using IAICF competency descriptors as an analytical lens.
Students observe an AI tool in use during a placement visit or virtual site visit to a partner organisation. Debrief uses IAICF Domain 5 governance competencies as a structured evaluation framework for what they observed. Outcome: Real-world grounding; bridge between academic framework and professional reality (links to 5.4, 5.6, 5.7).
Small-medium
Action Learning Set
Small group meets regularly to work on real professional AI problems using structured questioning and peer challenge - without providing answers.
Groups of four meet fortnightly; one member presents a real AI governance or ethics challenge they face in their professional or placement context. Peers ask questions only - no advice - enabling the presenter to develop their own solution. Outcome: Professional reasoning ownership; peer accountability; real-world governance problems enter the learning environment (links to 5.6, 5.7, 3.4).
Small-medium
Peer Teaching
Students teach a competency, method, or concept to peers, deepening their own understanding through the discipline of preparation and live delivery.
Students are each assigned one IAICF contextual competency; they design and deliver a 15-minute introduction including one assessment idea. Peers complete a structured feedback form; facilitator debrief follows. Outcome: Deep competency engagement; collaborative coverage of the contextual competencies that no single discipline owns fully (links to 5.6, 5.7, 3.6, 3.7).
Small-medium
IAICF Curriculum Co-Design Workshop
Technologies Reference - per Competency
Version 3.1  ·  32 competencies across 6 domains  ·  Specific tools mapped to teaching and assessment use.
Structure: each competency shows four specific tools for teaching and four for assessment, with concrete descriptions of how to use each in the exact competency context. Universal competencies are embedded across all disciplines; Contextual competencies are recommended where directly relevant; A/H competencies are Arts and Humanities scoped.
Domain 1
Conceptual Understanding of Artificial Intelligence Systems
1.1
Understanding How Artificial Intelligence Systems Are Built and Trained by Data
Universal
For Teaching
ChatGPT / Claude: Have students submit identical prompts to both tools, compare outputs and discuss why differences exist given different training data and architectures. Google Teachable Machine: Students train a simple image or sound classifier from scratch, observing how data volume and diversity affect prediction accuracy. Transformer Explainer (poloclub.github.io): Interactive visual walkthrough of how a transformer processes tokens - use for a live annotated demo during lecture. AI Incident Database (incidentdatabase.ai): Browse cases where training data failures caused real-world harm; anchor conceptual teaching in documented consequences.
For Assessment
ChatGPT / Claude: Students submit a prompt, capture the response, and annotate it identifying where training data limitations are likely to have shaped the output. Google Teachable Machine: Assessment task - train a classifier with biased data, measure accuracy drop, write a 300-word analysis of what this reveals about data lifecycle decisions. Mentimeter / Padlet: Live diagnostic poll - "Which stage of the AI data lifecycle is most likely to introduce bias?" Responses feed group discussion. Transformer Explainer: Students screenshot and annotate a specific processing step, explaining in plain language what is happening and why it matters.
1.2
Understanding Different Artificial Intelligence Model Types and Their Applications
Universal
For Teaching
ChatGPT (generative) + Teachable Machine (classification) + Runway ML (image gen): Use three tools in sequence to demonstrate generative, classification, and predictive models doing different tasks on the same input. AI Model Cards (huggingface.co): Students browse model cards for three different architectures; facilitator guides reading of intended use, limitations, and evaluation data. Google AutoML / IBM Watson: Show enterprise-level model selection decisions; discuss why organisations choose predictive over generative for specific tasks. Papers with Code (paperswithcode.com): Browse benchmark leaderboards to show how different model types perform on different task categories.
For Assessment
Mentimeter: Image quiz - show an AI output (poem, prediction, image, game move) and ask students to vote which model type produced it; discuss rationale. Padlet: Groups each research one model type and post a real-world use case, limitation, and disciplinary example. ChatGPT + Claude: Students ask both tools to explain their own architecture; critically compare the responses for accuracy. Quizlet adaptive quiz: Model types, applications, and limitations reviewed through spaced repetition before application tasks.
1.3
Identifying Fundamental Limitations and Failure Modes of Artificial Intelligence Systems
Universal
For Teaching
AI Incident Database: Browse documented hallucination and overconfidence failures by sector; assign groups one sector each to present what went wrong and why. Claude / ChatGPT: Deliberately prompt both tools with questions outside their training knowledge - observe hallucination in real time, discuss why it happens architecturally. Elicit (elicit.org): Show students how an AI literature search tool presents confidence it does not have; compare against manual search. Perplexity AI: Compare cited versus uncited AI responses; discuss what citations do and do not guarantee about factual reliability.
For Assessment
ChatGPT / Claude: Red-team exercise - each student must elicit one hallucination, screenshot it, verify the claim against authoritative sources, and write a 250-word analysis of the failure mode demonstrated. AI Incident Database: Case analysis - students select one documented AI failure, identify the limitation responsible, and present to peers. Perplexity AI: Students submit a disciplinary question, receive an AI response with citations, manually verify each citation for accuracy, and report findings. Mentimeter live poll: "Which AI failure mode poses the greatest risk in your intended profession?" Results feed a structured debate.
1.4
Recognising How Artificial Intelligence Shapes Everyday Services and Decisions
Universal
For Teaching
Algorithm Watch (algorithmwatch.org): Browse their database of documented algorithmic systems affecting public life; assign students to research one system affecting their discipline. AdChoices / Ad Settings (Google): Show students their own personalisation profile; discuss what data AI systems have inferred about them. YouTube / Spotify with tracking disabled then enabled: Contrast recommendation behaviour to make algorithmic personalisation visible and discussable. Lightbeam / Privacy Badger (browser extensions): Visualise real-time tracking and data collection during a live browsing session; anchor discussion of invisible AI infrastructure.
For Assessment
Padlet: Students post one example of algorithmic influence they discovered during the week, tagging it by sector and type of AI system involved; peers respond with questions. Algorithm Watch: Students select one documented algorithmic system, map its data inputs, decision outputs, and affected populations, and present findings in a structured two-page brief. Personal data audit task: Students download their own data from one platform (Google Takeout, Instagram), identify what AI has inferred, and reflect on implications. Exit ticket: Three questions - name one AI system you encountered today, describe how it influenced your experience, identify what you do not know about how it works.
Domain 2
Critical Evaluation of AI Outputs and System Performance
2.1
Evaluating the Accuracy and Reliability of Artificial Intelligence-Generated Information
Universal
For Teaching
Perplexity AI: Use as a paired comparison tool - generate an answer with Perplexity (cited) and ChatGPT (uncited); teach students to trace every cited claim back to its source. Elicit / Consensus: Show how AI literature synthesis can fabricate or misrepresent papers; assign manual verification of three AI-generated citations as a structured class activity. FactCheck.org / Full Fact (fullfact.org): Model professional fact-checking workflows alongside AI output verification; discuss shared methodology. ChatGPT: Deliberately ask it about a recent event after its knowledge cutoff; use the confident hallucination as a teaching case.
For Assessment
Elicit: Assessment task - students submit a disciplinary question, receive an AI-generated literature summary, manually verify every claim against original sources, and report accuracy rate with annotated evidence. Perplexity AI: Students submit five claims from an AI-generated report and assess each for accuracy using the cited sources; final product is an annotated verification report. ChatGPT / Claude: Students request an AI-generated explanation of a concept they know well from their discipline; identify specific errors, omissions, and misrepresentations with disciplinary evidence. Padlet: Weekly peer-sharing wall - each student posts one AI inaccuracy they caught that week with evidence of the error; class reviews and discusses patterns.
2.2
Detecting Bias and Unequal Representation in Artificial Intelligence Outputs
Universal
For Teaching
DALL-E / Midjourney / Adobe Firefly: Generate images of "a doctor", "a criminal", "a CEO", "a nurse" across tools; compare representational patterns and discuss training data implications. IBM AI Fairness 360 (aif360.res.ibm.com): Introduce fairness metrics; use interactive demos to show how the same model scores differently on different fairness criteria. Gender Shades project (media.mit.edu/projects/gender-shades): Teach the landmark bias audit methodology; students apply the same logic to a tool of their choice. ChatGPT: Prompt the same question with different demographic framing and compare outputs for implicit differences.
For Assessment
DALL-E / Adobe Firefly: Structured bias audit - students generate 20 images across five professional categories, document representational patterns, and write a 400-word analysis referencing training data theory. IBM AI Fairness 360: Students run the tutorial dataset through fairness metrics, interpret results, and present findings explaining what each metric reveals and its limitations. ChatGPT / Claude: Comparative prompting - students generate outputs on a sensitive topic with neutral, male-coded, and female-coded framing; analyse differences for evidence of training data bias. Padlet: Groups post a documented example of AI bias from a sector of their choice, with evidence and proposed mitigation; peers add responses and challenge assumptions.
2.3
Analysing the Logical Consistency of Artificial Intelligence-Generated Reasoning
Universal
For Teaching
ChatGPT / Claude with chain-of-thought prompting: Ask tools to "show their working" step by step on a disciplinary problem; students analyse whether each step follows logically. Wolfram Alpha: Use as a ground-truth comparator for mathematical and factual reasoning; compare AI chain-of-thought against verified step-by-step solutions. Microsoft Copilot in Word: Show students how AI-generated essay arguments can be internally consistent but factually wrong; practice identifying where the reasoning chain breaks. Logic diagram tools (Miro / Jamboard): Students map AI reasoning chains visually, identifying where premises lead to conclusions and where logical gaps appear.
For Assessment
ChatGPT - chain of thought: Students ask an AI to solve a disciplinary problem step by step, then evaluate each reasoning step for logical validity, unsupported assumptions, and circular arguments. Wolfram Alpha paired with ChatGPT: Students submit a quantitative problem to both, compare reasoning processes, identify where AI reasoning diverges from verified method. Miro / Jamboard: Students map an AI-generated argument as a logic diagram, annotating each node as valid, questionable, or fallacious with justification. Peer review exercise: Students exchange AI reasoning analyses; reviewer must identify whether the original student's critique correctly located the logical flaw.
2.4
Assessing Artificial Intelligence Outputs for Professional Tone and Contextual Fit
Universal
For Teaching
ChatGPT / Claude: Generate a professional communication (condolence letter, patient consultation note, legal brief) using AI; students evaluate for tone, register, and contextual appropriateness before editing. DeepL / Google Translate paired with ChatGPT: Compare AI outputs for the same communication task in English versus translated versions; discuss appropriateness across contexts. Microsoft Copilot in Outlook: Show how AI-drafted emails differ in register from human-written equivalents; discuss when AI defaults are appropriate and when they are not. Grammarly tone analyser: Use as a diagnostic starting point to discuss what professional tone means, then critique Grammarly's judgements from a disciplinary perspective.
For Assessment
ChatGPT / Claude: Students receive an AI-drafted professional communication, evaluate it against five contextual appropriateness criteria (tone, register, audience awareness, professional credibility, disciplinary conventions), and produce a revised version with annotation. DeepL + ChatGPT: Students evaluate an AI-generated communication for a cross-cultural context and assess appropriateness with specific evidence. Microsoft Copilot: Students use AI to draft a communication, then annotate every element they had to revise and explain why the AI default was contextually inappropriate. Peer critique: Students exchange AI-assisted professional communications and apply a shared appropriateness rubric; discrepancies in scoring are discussed in a debrief.
2.5
Verifying the Authenticity of Artificial Intelligence-Generated Synthetic Media
Universal
For Teaching
Sensity AI / Hive Moderation: Introduce commercial deepfake detection tools; discuss how they work, their limitations, and why no tool is 100% reliable. Deepware Scanner (deepware.ai): Run suspected deepfake videos through the scanner live; discuss confidence scores and what they do and do not tell us. Google Reverse Image Search / TinEye: Teach basic reverse image verification as a first-line check before specialist tools are needed. MIT Media Lab Detect Fakes project (detectfakes.media.mit.edu): Interactive quiz where students distinguish real from deepfake faces; use results to discuss what humans can and cannot detect.
For Assessment
MIT Detect Fakes: Students complete the interactive test, record their accuracy, and write a reflection on which visual cues they missed and why deepfake detection is difficult. Deepware Scanner: Students source three videos from the internet, run each through the scanner, then independently assess authenticity using visual inspection; compare scanner results with their own judgements. Case analysis: Students select a documented deepfake incident from media, reconstruct how it was detected, and evaluate which verification methods would have worked. Group exercise: Students are given a mix of real and synthetic images without labelling; they must decide which are AI-generated and justify their reasoning with specific evidence before seeing the answers.
2.6
Evaluating the Credibility of Information and Claims About Artificial Intelligence
Universal
For Teaching
Media Bias Chart (allsides.com): Analyse how different sources cover the same AI story; teach students to identify framing, selective evidence, and source agenda. Full Fact / FactCheck.org: Apply professional fact-checking to AI-related claims in news articles and marketing materials; model the workflow before students apply it independently. AI company documentation (model cards, system cards, research papers): Compare what companies claim about their tools against what independent researchers find; discuss the gap. Lateral reading technique (NewsGuard / open web): Teach students to leave a source immediately and check what others say about it, rather than reading the source deeply.
For Assessment
Full Fact: Students select an AI-related claim from a news article or company website, apply a structured fact-checking protocol, and produce a 350-word credibility assessment with evidence. Media Bias Chart + news comparison: Students find the same AI story covered by three outlets with different political and commercial orientations; compare framing, evidence used, and claims made. Company vs researcher comparison: Students compare an AI company's published claims about a product against independent academic or journalist analysis; identify specific discrepancies and evaluate whose account is better evidenced. Exit ticket: Present one real AI claim and one AI hype claim; students must identify which is which and justify their answer in two sentences.
Domain 3
Ethical and Social Implications of Artificial Intelligence
3.1
Evaluating Personal Data Footprints Within Artificial Intelligence Systems
Universal
For Teaching
Google Takeout / Instagram Data Download: Students download their own platform data to see what AI systems have inferred and stored; use as the basis for a privacy risk analysis. Privacy Paradox podcast (WNYC): Audio-based teaching resource exploring how users underestimate data exposure; pairs well with a Padlet reflection activity. DuckDuckGo Privacy Essentials / uBlock Origin: Show real-time data collection during browsing; contrast with standard browser behaviour to make invisible data flows visible. ICO Data Rights Checker (ico.org.uk): Walk students through subject access request processes; discuss what GDPR means for AI-driven data use in practice.
For Assessment
Google Takeout audit: Students download and analyse their own platform data, identify at least five inferences AI systems have made about them, and produce a 400-word privacy risk analysis. ICO website + GDPR framework: Students evaluate a real AI product's privacy policy against GDPR requirements, identifying three compliance strengths and two gaps. DuckDuckGo tool: Students run a live session tracking data collection on a website of their choice, document findings, and propose one governance change the organisation should make. Scenario-based viva: Given a case study of an AI system processing sensitive personal data, students must identify risks, applicable regulations, and responsible practice recommendations under examiner questioning.
3.2
Assessing the Environmental Impact and Sustainability of Artificial Intelligence Technologies
Universal
For Teaching
ML CO2 Impact Calculator (mlco2.github.io): Students estimate the carbon cost of training a model; compare against everyday equivalents (flights, car journeys) to build intuition. Codecarbon (codecarbon.io): Show how developers track emissions during model training; discuss organisational responsibility for environmental accounting in AI. Green AI papers (arxiv.org - search "green AI"): Assign one academic paper on energy-efficient AI; students present findings and implications for practice. Our World in Data - Energy / Emissions: Contextualise AI's energy footprint within broader technology sector and national emissions data.
For Assessment
ML CO2 Calculator: Students calculate the estimated carbon cost of three different AI model sizes for a task of their choice, compare, and write a 300-word justification for which to choose on sustainability grounds. Green AI paper review: Students read one peer-reviewed paper on sustainable AI, produce a structured summary, and present to peers. Organisational case analysis: Students evaluate one organisation's published AI ethics or sustainability commitments, assess whether environmental claims are substantiated, and identify gaps. Debate: "The environmental cost of large AI models is justified by their benefits." Students argue assigned positions with evidence from the literature.
3.3
Examining Artificial Intelligence's Broader Impact on Work and Society
Universal
For Teaching
World Economic Forum Future of Jobs Report (weforum.org): Use as a reading anchor; assign students to find one claim about their sector and evaluate it with sector-specific evidence. LinkedIn / Indeed job postings: Students search for roles in their discipline, identify which now require AI competency, and map changing skill requirements over time. AI Incident Database - labour cases: Filter for AI incidents involving worker displacement or algorithmic management; use as case studies for seminar discussion. McKinsey Global Institute AI automation reports: Assign opposing readings (optimistic and pessimistic) and hold a structured debate on labour market futures.
For Assessment
Job posting analysis: Students analyse 20 job postings in their discipline for AI-related requirements, track changes over time using archived postings, and produce a 500-word analysis of skill shift. WEF Future of Jobs: Students identify one profession likely to be significantly transformed by AI, research evidence for and against, and present a structured argument about what this means for curriculum design. Interview or expert talk debrief: Students listen to a practitioner discuss AI's impact on their profession and produce a critical response connecting the practitioner's experience to course frameworks. Reflective essay: How might AI change the specific professional role you are preparing for? Students must use at least three pieces of evidence and address both risk and opportunity.
3.4
Preserving Human Agency and Decision-Making Authority in Artificial Intelligence-Assisted Processes
Universal
For Teaching
AI Incident Database - automation bias: Filter for cases where uncritical AI acceptance caused harm; use as anchors for teaching why human override matters. ChatGPT / Claude with forced disagreement exercise: Students ask AI for a recommendation, then deliberately argue against it using professional judgement; discuss what the exercise reveals about automation bias. H5P branching scenario: Build or use an existing scenario where students must decide when to override an AI recommendation; consequences shown for each choice. Miro decision workflow mapping: Students design a human-AI decision workflow for a professional scenario, explicitly marking where human review is mandatory.
For Assessment
H5P / branching scenario: Students work through a professional decision scenario with AI input; assessment evaluates quality of their human override decisions and reasoning at each checkpoint. Miro workflow: Students design and submit a human-AI decision workflow for a real professional context; assessed on how well they have structured human checkpoints and accountability. ChatGPT override exercise: Students receive an AI recommendation for a high-stakes professional scenario, write a professional response either following or overriding the recommendation, and justify their decision with reference to professional standards. Case study analysis: Students select an AI incident involving automation bias, identify where human override should have occurred, and design a workflow that would have prevented the outcome.
3.5
Analysing Artificial Intelligence's Impact on Information Integrity and Public Discourse
Universal
For Teaching
NewsGuard (newsguardtech.com): Browser extension rating news source credibility; use as a live demonstration of how information quality varies across the web. Twitter/X algorithm disclosure documents: Use Elon Musk-era algorithm releases as a primary source; discuss what transparency reveals and what it still hides. Bellingcat open-source investigation guides (bellingcat.com): Teach verification methods used by investigative journalists; apply to AI-generated content verification. TruthOrFake / Logically Facts: AI-powered fact-checking tools; discuss the irony and usefulness of using AI to detect AI-generated misinformation.
For Assessment
NewsGuard + source analysis: Students evaluate five news sources on an AI-related topic for credibility, cross-reference their ratings with independent assessment, and produce a media ecosystem analysis. Bellingcat methodology: Students apply open-source verification techniques to a piece of potentially AI-generated content found in the wild, documenting each verification step. Social media audit: Students track how one AI-related story spreads across three platforms over one week, mapping distortion and amplification; produce a 500-word analysis of information integrity implications. Policy brief: Students draft a two-page policy recommendation for a named organisation on how to protect information integrity in their sector from AI-generated misinformation.
3.7
Identifying Professional Contexts Where Artificial Intelligence Use Is Inappropriate
Universal
For Teaching
AI Incident Database - inappropriate AI use: Filter for cases where AI was used in contexts requiring human care or judgement with harmful consequences; use as teaching cases. ChatGPT in counselling simulation: Demonstrate what AI produces when asked to provide emotional support; contrast with what a human counsellor would offer; discuss the gap. Professional body guidance documents (GMC, SRA, BPS, etc.): Compare professional ethics codes on technology use with AI capability claims; identify where codes draw clear human-only lines. Coeckelbergh "Political Philosophy of AI" selected readings: Use as philosophical grounding for discussion of irreplaceable human capabilities.
For Assessment
Boundary mapping task: Students produce a visual map of their intended profession showing which tasks AI can support, which it can assist with human oversight, and which must remain exclusively human; assessed on quality of justification for each boundary. Case analysis: Students select one professional context where AI was used in a human-judgement task, evaluate what went wrong, and design a framework for appropriate use. Professional ethics brief: Students write a 500-word briefing for a named professional body on where to draw the line on AI use in their sector, citing professional codes and documented cases. Structured debate: Is there any task that AI will never be able to perform as well as a human in an ethically acceptable way? Students must defend a position with evidence.
3.6
Recognising the Psycho-Emotional Effect of Human-Like Artificial Intelligence Responses on Human Users
Contextual
For Teaching
Replika / Character.ai (with ethical framing): Demonstrate emotionally responsive AI design; discuss what design features trigger attachment and what the ethical implications are for vulnerable users. ChatGPT in extended conversation mode: Show how consistent persona and empathetic phrasing create a sense of relationship; students document their own emotional responses during the exercise. Reeves and Nass "Media Equation" readings paired with live demo: Read a short extract, then test the theory by asking students to be rude to an AI and discuss their discomfort. MIT Moral Machine (moralmachine.net): Use as a discussion anchor for how humans apply moral reasoning differently to human versus AI agents.
For Assessment
Reflective journal: After a structured extended conversation with an AI tool, students write a 400-word reflection on their emotional responses, identifying which design features triggered them and why. Design critique: Students analyse the interface and language design of one emotionally responsive AI tool, identifying specific features designed to trigger anthropomorphic response and evaluating their ethical implications. Case analysis: Students select a reported case of problematic emotional attachment to AI (therapy bots, companion AI, etc.) and apply psychological theory to explain the mechanism and propose a governance response. Debate: "Emotionally responsive AI systems should be banned in therapeutic contexts." Students argue assigned positions using psychological evidence and professional ethics frameworks.
Domain 4
Practical Application and Professional Use of Artificial Intelligence Tools
4.1
Designing Effective Prompts and Queries for Artificial Intelligence Systems
Universal
For Teaching
ChatGPT / Claude / Gemini: Run live prompt comparison sessions - same task, three different prompts, compare outputs; students discover through direct experience why prompt design matters. PromptBase (promptbase.com): Browse professional prompt libraries; discuss what makes a well-designed prompt and why prompt design has become a professional skill. Prompt flow tools (Microsoft Azure Prompt Flow / LangChain basics): For more technical groups, show how prompts are structured in professional deployments. Google's Prompting Essentials guide (cloud.google.com): Use as a structured reading alongside live practice sessions.
For Assessment
Prompt design challenge: Students design three increasingly sophisticated prompts for the same disciplinary task, submit all three with AI outputs, and write a 300-word comparative analysis of what changed and why. Annotated prompt portfolio: Students submit five prompts for different professional tasks, with annotation explaining every design decision (role, context, constraints, output format) and how each element improved the result. Live prompt competition: Groups compete to produce the most useful AI output for a disciplinary challenge using only prompt design; assessed on output quality and prompt sophistication. Peer critique: Students exchange prompt designs and evaluate each other's using a shared rubric covering clarity, context, constraints, and output specification.
4.2
Selecting and Integrating Artificial Intelligence Tools in Professional Workflows
Universal
For Teaching
Tool comparison framework (student-built in Miro): Students map 4-5 AI tools against criteria including capability, privacy implications, cost, disciplinary suitability, and reliability; build a shared selection matrix. G2 / Capterra / Trustpilot: Teach students to critically evaluate user reviews of AI tools; discuss what reviews do and do not tell us about professional suitability. Microsoft 365 Copilot / Notion AI / Gamma: Show AI integrated into professional workflow tools that students will encounter in employment; discuss integration decisions. Privacy policy comparison: Students compare data retention and privacy policies of three AI tools side by side; discuss implications for professional use with sensitive data.
For Assessment
Tool selection report: Students select an AI tool for a specific professional task in their discipline, justify their selection against four criteria (capability, privacy, cost, suitability), and propose a workflow integrating the tool with defined human oversight points. Workflow design: Students design and document a complete human-AI workflow for a professional scenario in their discipline, including which tasks are AI-supported, which are human-only, and where review checkpoints occur. Tool audit: Students evaluate two competing AI tools for the same professional task using a structured rubric; produce a comparative report recommending one tool with evidence-based justification. Reflective log: Students track their AI tool use across a module, documenting selection decisions, reliability observations, and workflow integration challenges; submitted as a structured professional development log.
4.3
Using Artificial Intelligence as a Tool Whilst Preserving Critical Judgement
Universal
For Teaching
ChatGPT / Claude in iterative dialogue mode: Model the difference between accepting the first output and engaging critically with subsequent rounds; demonstrate what rigorous cognitive partnership looks like versus passive acceptance. Zotero + AI summary tools: Show how AI-assisted literature synthesis requires manual verification against source material; model the validation workflow explicitly. NotebookLM: Students upload their own course readings and use AI to generate summaries, then verify each summary claim against the original text. Hypothesis (hypothes.is): Collaborative annotation tool; students annotate an AI-generated analysis, flagging claims that require verification and those that seem reliable.
For Assessment
AI-assisted analysis with validation log: Students use AI to assist a disciplinary analysis task, submit the AI output alongside a validation log documenting every claim checked, every error found, and every judgement made. NotebookLM verification task: Students upload three academic papers, ask AI to synthesise them, then verify ten specific claims from the synthesis against the original sources; report accuracy and note AI's failure modes. Comparative analysis: Students complete the same analytical task independently and with AI assistance; compare the quality of both versions and reflect on what the AI added, what it missed, and what required human correction. Portfolio entry: Students submit one piece of AI-assisted work with full annotation of their validation process, explaining how they maintained intellectual integrity throughout.
4.4
Diagnosing and Resolving Artificial Intelligence Failures and Unhelpful Outputs in Practice
Universal
For Teaching
ChatGPT / Claude - deliberate failure exercise: Facilitator designs prompts guaranteed to produce vague, refused, or circular outputs; students diagnose the failure and iterate to recover productive output. Multiple AI tool switching: When one tool fails a task, demonstrate systematic switching to an alternative; discuss when tool switching is appropriate versus prompt refinement. Prompt debugging worksheet: Structured template for diagnosing failure type (too vague, too complex, constraint conflict, safety refusal) and selecting appropriate fix strategy. Screen recording of live troubleshooting: Facilitator records themselves troubleshooting an AI failure in real time, narrating their diagnostic reasoning; used as a flipped classroom resource.
For Assessment
Troubleshooting log: Students encounter six pre-designed AI interaction failures and must diagnose and resolve each one, documenting: failure type identified, strategy applied, revised prompt, and outcome. Live troubleshooting viva: Students given a failing AI interaction in real time; examiner assesses their diagnostic reasoning and iterative refinement approach over ten minutes. Reflective analysis: Students document three real AI failures they encountered during the module, apply the failure taxonomy from the course, and evaluate whether their recovery strategies were optimal. Group challenge: Teams given the same failing AI interaction must each find a different valid solution; debrief compares approaches and discusses which is most robust and why.
Domain 5
Governance, Accountability, and Regulation of Artificial Intelligence
5.1
Examining Artificial Intelligence Tools for Trustworthiness, Transparency, and Reliability *[Merged: 5.1+5.6]*
Universal
For Teaching
Hugging Face Model Cards (huggingface.co): Students read model cards for real AI systems, identifying what is disclosed and what is missing about training data, intended use, and known limitations. AI Now Institute reports (ainowinstitute.org): Use published audits of commercial AI systems as templates for what scrutiny looks like in practice. NIST AI RMF (nist.gov): Introduce the Risk Management Framework as a professional tool for evaluating AI system trustworthiness; map its dimensions to real tool evaluations. AI Incident Database - provenance failures: Filter for cases where undisclosed training data or hidden conflicts of interest caused documented harm; use as teaching cases.
For Assessment
Model card analysis: Students evaluate the model card of a real AI system against a transparency checklist (developer identity, funding, training data, known limitations, conflict of interest disclosures), identifying gaps and rating overall transparency. Provenance investigation: Students research who built a chosen AI tool, how it was funded, what data it was trained on, and what governance structures exist; produce a two-page provenance report. Performance monitoring simulation: Students receive a dataset of AI outputs over time showing declining accuracy; they must identify when the system crossed a threshold requiring review and justify their escalation decision. AI system audit brief: Students produce a professional audit brief evaluating whether to continue using a named AI tool, covering provenance, transparency, reliability trends, and governance recommendation.
5.2
Disclosing and Documenting Artificial Intelligence Use and Contributions in Academic and Professional Work
Universal
For Teaching
Institutional AI attribution policy documents: Compare real attribution policies from three universities (e.g. UCL, Oxford, Manchester) and three professional bodies; identify commonalities and gaps. Zotero / Endnote: Show how AI tools can be cited as sources using existing reference management infrastructure; discuss what information a citation should capture. ChatGPT / Claude with AI use statement templates: Draft and refine AI attribution statements for different contexts (academic essay, professional report, published research); discuss what level of detail each requires. Committee on Publication Ethics (COPE) guidance: Use as a primary source for discussing authorship and AI attribution in published work.
For Assessment
Attribution policy analysis: Students compare AI attribution policies from three institutions, identify the most and least transparent approaches, and draft a model attribution policy for their own institution. AI use log and statement: Students keep a structured log of AI use throughout a module assignment and produce a formal attribution statement following a specified format; assessed on completeness and honesty. Scenario response: Students receive five scenarios describing different levels of AI involvement in a piece of work and must write appropriate attribution statements for each; assessed on proportionality and transparency. Peer review: Students exchange AI use statements and evaluate each other's for completeness, specificity, and alignment with institutional policy.
5.3
Interpreting Relevant Legal and Regulatory Frameworks Governing Artificial Intelligence
Universal
For Teaching
EU AI Act full text and summary guides (artificialintelligenceact.eu): Use the plain-language summary alongside selected articles; discuss how risk tiers work and which systems fall into each category. ICO guidance on AI and data protection (ico.org.uk): UK-specific regulatory context; particularly relevant for students in regulated sectors. AI Policy Watch / Stanford HAI Policy Hub: Track evolving AI regulation; assign students to monitor one jurisdiction and report regulatory developments. Sector regulator guidance documents (FCA, CQC, Ofcom, etc.): Show students that AI regulation is sector-specific; assign each student a different regulator to research.
For Assessment
EU AI Act risk classification: Students classify five AI systems into EU AI Act risk categories, justify each classification with reference to the Act's criteria, and identify the resulting compliance obligations. Regulatory compliance audit: Students evaluate a real AI product against GDPR and one sector-specific regulatory requirement, identifying compliance strengths and gaps with reference to specific regulatory provisions. Policy brief: Students draft a two-page regulatory briefing for a named organisation entering a new market, explaining which AI regulations apply and what compliance actions are required. Regulatory change tracker: Students monitor AI regulatory developments in one jurisdiction for four weeks, producing a structured update report at the end of the module.
5.4
Applying Professional Ethics and Sector-Specific Codes to Artificial Intelligence Use
Universal
For Teaching
Professional body guidance documents (GMC Good Medical Practice, SRA guidance, BPS ethics code, Engineering Council statement): Students in each discipline read their own profession's relevant guidance on AI. Ethics case study databases (by discipline): Medical ethics cases, legal ethics scenarios, engineering ethics incidents; reframe classic cases to include an AI dimension. ChatGPT - professional ethics scenario: Generate a scenario where AI use conflicts with professional duty of care; students discuss where the professional code draws the line. Regulatory sanctions databases: Search for documented cases where professionals faced sanctions for technology-related ethical failures; discuss prevention.
For Assessment
Professional code application: Students identify three specific provisions of their profession's ethics code that apply to AI use, give a concrete example of how each could be violated, and propose a safeguard for each. Ethics case analysis: Students receive a scenario where AI use creates a conflict with professional duty and must apply their profession's ethics code to decide the appropriate course of action, justifying each step. Policy design: Students draft an AI ethics policy for a professional practice in their discipline, grounding every provision in a specific clause of the relevant professional code. Peer role play: Students enact a professional ethics consultation - one student presents an AI use dilemma, the other applies the professional code to advise; roles rotate and outcomes are debriefed.
5.5
Evaluating Intellectual Property, Copyright, and Training Data Ethics in Artificial Intelligence
Universal
For Teaching
UK IPO AI and copyright guidance (ipo.gov.uk): Primary source for UK copyright law applied to AI; discuss the text and data mining exception and its limits. Getty Images v Stability AI case materials: Real copyright litigation as a teaching case; discuss what the case reveals about training data ethics. Creative Commons licence explorer (creativecommons.org): Students identify which creative works can legally be used to train AI and under what conditions. Adobe Firefly vs DALL-E comparison: Discuss how Firefly's training on licensed content differs from models trained on scraped web data; evaluate the ethical and legal implications.
For Assessment
Copyright risk assessment: Students evaluate three AI-generated outputs in their discipline for copyright risk, identifying what the AI may have reproduced, which rights are at stake, and what responsible practice requires. Training data ethics brief: Students research the training data used by one major AI tool, evaluate it for copyright and ethical compliance, and produce a 400-word assessment with recommendations. IPO policy analysis: Students compare UK copyright law with the EU AI Act's data governance provisions on training data; identify where the frameworks conflict or complement each other. Case study analysis: Students analyse the Getty Images v Stability AI case, identify the legal and ethical principles at stake, and apply those principles to a scenario from their own discipline.
5.6
Reflecting on Personal and Professional Accountability in Artificial Intelligence-Assisted Work
Contextual
For Teaching
AI Incident Database - accountability failures: Filter for cases where professionals attempted to attribute responsibility to AI systems; discuss how courts and professional bodies have responded. Professional liability insurance documentation: Show students that professional indemnity insurance does not cover AI-caused harm attributed to negligent use; discuss implications. ChatGPT in legal / medical / engineering context: Give students a scenario where AI provides advice that turns out to be wrong; discuss where accountability sits and how to demonstrate professional diligence. OECD AI Principles on accountability (oecd.org): Use as a framework for discussing how accountability is structured at individual, organisational, and governmental levels.
For Assessment
Accountability scenario analysis: Students receive a professional scenario where AI-assisted work leads to a negative outcome; they must identify who is accountable, what evidence of due diligence would be required, and how they would demonstrate it. Professional reflection: Students document a real or hypothetical instance of AI-assisted work in their discipline, identify the accountability obligations they held, and evaluate whether they met them. Mock professional tribunal: Students role-play a professional disciplinary hearing where AI use is at issue; assessed on quality of argument and use of professional standards. Policy brief: Students draft accountability guidance for a professional body in their discipline on how members should document AI use to demonstrate due diligence.
5.7
Understanding Artificial Intelligence Governance and Regulatory Responsibilities in Professional Practice
Contextual
For Teaching
EU AI Act impact assessments / conformity assessments: Show students the governance documents organisations must produce for high-risk AI systems; discuss what this means for professional practice. ISO 42001 AI management systems standard (iso.org): Introduce the international standard for organisational AI governance; discuss which elements are most relevant to practice contexts. Organisation AI ethics policies (Google, Microsoft, NHS, BBC): Compare how different organisations translate governance principles into practice; identify common gaps. AI governance case studies from Chartered Institute of Personnel and Development (CIPD) and similar bodies: Sector-specific governance examples.
For Assessment
Governance gap analysis: Students evaluate one organisation's published AI governance policy against ISO 42001 or EU AI Act requirements, identifying gaps and recommending improvements. Governance design task: Students design an AI governance framework for a named organisation in their discipline, specifying accountability structures, compliance monitoring processes, and stakeholder reporting mechanisms. Regulatory compliance case: Students receive a scenario where an organisation has deployed a high-risk AI system without appropriate governance; they must identify what governance obligations were breached and what remediation is required. Stakeholder briefing: Students produce a two-page AI governance briefing for a senior leader in their discipline who is unfamiliar with regulatory requirements; assessed on accuracy, clarity, and practical relevance.
Domain 6
Cognitive Diversity, Accessibility, and Inclusive AI Literacy
6.1
Inclusive and Adaptive Artificial Intelligence Use *[Merged: 6.1+6.5]*
Universal
For Teaching
Microsoft Accessibility features in Copilot / Edge: Demonstrate how AI tools can be configured for assistive use - immersive reader, text-to-speech, focus mode; discuss which features genuinely help and which are cosmetic. Speechify / Natural Reader / Microsoft Read Aloud: Show AI text-to-speech in action for a complex academic text; discuss how different voices, speeds, and formats serve different cognitive needs. Otter.ai / Microsoft Teams live transcription: Demonstrate real-time AI transcription as an accessibility tool; discuss accuracy limitations for non-native speakers or accented speech. Explain Everything / Loom: Multimodal explanation tools; model how to explain an AI concept using text, diagram, and voice simultaneously to reach different cognitive styles.
For Assessment
Accessibility audit: Students evaluate one AI tool against WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility criteria and against the needs of a specific user group (neurodivergent, non-native English speaker, low-tech access); produce a structured report with recommendations. Adaptive explanation task: Students explain the same AI concept in three different formats - written, visual diagram, and two-minute video - targeting different cognitive profiles; assessed on accessibility of each format. Tool comparison for assistive use: Students test two AI tools with assistive technology enabled (screen reader, text-to-speech, keyboard navigation only) and produce a comparative accessibility report. Teaching resource design: Students design an AI literacy teaching resource for a specific neurodivergent audience, applying what they have learned about cognitive diversity and multimodal design; peer-reviewed using shared accessibility criteria.
6.3
Evaluating Artificial Intelligence Tools for Equity and Inclusive Access *[Merged: 6.3+6.4]*
Universal
For Teaching
WCAG 2.1 Accessibility Checker (wave.webaim.org): Students run real AI tool interfaces through the accessibility checker; compare results across tools and discuss implications for diverse users. Multilingual AI comparison: Run the same task in English and one other language across ChatGPT, Google Gemini, and DeepL; compare output quality, cultural appropriateness, and linguistic accuracy. Digital Poverty Alliance resources (digitalpovertyalliance.org): Contextualise AI access barriers within the UK digital poverty landscape; discuss implications for students and communities. WCAG colour contrast checker / keyboard-only navigation test: Live demonstration of accessibility failure in real AI tool interfaces; students experience barriers firsthand.
For Assessment
Cross-linguistic equity audit: Students test one AI tool in English and one other language for the same professional task; evaluate linguistic equity, cultural appropriateness, and output quality; produce a 400-word audit report. WCAG compliance audit: Students run three AI tool interfaces through WAVE accessibility checker, document failures, and rank tools on accessibility grounds with specific evidence. Digital equity case: Students evaluate whether a proposed AI implementation in a public sector context would create or exacerbate digital poverty barriers, using evidence from Digital Poverty Alliance research. Tool recommendation for diverse users: Students are given a brief describing a diverse user group and must select and justify the most equitable AI tool for their context.
6.4
Reflecting on Artificial Intelligence's Impact on Personal Learning and Cognitive Development
Universal
For Teaching
Learning journal with AI tracking: Students maintain a structured weekly journal documenting every AI tool use, what cognitive task they delegated, and what they believe they learned or failed to learn as a result. Retrieval practice tools (Anki / Quizlet): Contrast AI-assisted summarisation (passive) with active retrieval practice (effortful); discuss what each does to memory and understanding. ChatGPT / Claude with deliberate self-testing: Model the difference between asking AI for an answer versus asking AI to test you on a topic; demonstrate how to use AI to strengthen rather than replace cognitive effort. Mollick and Mollick "Assigning AI" framework: Use as a structured reading about different pedagogical relationships between students and AI tools; apply to students' own learning practices.
For Assessment
Cognitive impact reflection: Students submit a 600-word structured reflection on how their AI use across the module has affected their learning, memory, problem-solving, and confidence; must identify at least two specific instances with evidence. Learning strategy audit: Students document their AI use patterns over four weeks, categorise each use by cognitive delegation type (recall, analysis, synthesis, communication), and evaluate which uses strengthened and which weakened their competency development. Comparative task: Students complete a disciplinary task twice - once with AI assistance and once independently; submit both versions with a 300-word reflection on the cognitive differences experienced. Exit interview: Short viva where students discuss one change they have made to their AI use habits as a result of the module and provide evidence that the change improved their learning.
6.AH
Using Artificial Intelligence to Participate Equitably in Artistic and Humanistic Expression and Assessment
A/H
For Teaching
H5P multimodal content tools: Demonstrate how to build assessments that allow video, audio, text, and interactive responses; discuss which AI competencies each format is best suited to assess for diverse learners. VoiceThread: Students record audio or video responses to assessment tasks; discuss how this format changes who can demonstrate competency and what it reveals versus written formats. Padlet / Wakelet: Multiformat evidence collection tools; show how portfolios can accommodate diverse formats without requiring all students to write in standard academic English. QAA and Advance HE inclusive assessment guidance: Primary sources for the policy context underpinning inclusive assessment design in artistic and humanistic disciplines.
For Assessment
Inclusive assessment design: Students redesign an existing assessment in their artistic or humanistic discipline to use at least two modalities, justify their design decisions with reference to cognitive diversity, and evaluate potential equity implications. Accessibility review: Students review a real AI-integrated assessment task used in their institution, identify three equity risks, and propose specific mitigations with reference to QAA guidance. Multimodal portfolio: Students submit a portfolio demonstrating competency in one area using three different formats (written, visual, audio or video); assessed on competency demonstrated, not on format quality. Policy brief: Students draft inclusive assessment guidelines for a programme team in an artistic or humanistic discipline introducing AI-integrated assessments for the first time.
